posts.summary: Dan Shapiro introduces his woodworking blog with a brief, humorous post explaining that he started serious woodworking about a year prior. He promises to share lessons learned, acknowledging that some have come painlessly while others involved minor injuries. The post's title 'Severed?' is a self-deprecating joke about the risks of power tools.
key_points: Shapiro began serious woodworking approximately a year before this post
He frames learning as a spectrum from easy lessons to painful ones requiring first aid
The post serves as a lighthearted introduction to what would become his blog
money_quotes: I'm learning lots of lessons, some the easy way, some with liberal applications of neosporin.
No, I haven't decided if I'll change the title if I get too frisky with the jointer.
posts.key_insight: This brief introductory post establishes Shapiro's maker identity and self-deprecating humor style that would characterize his later writing.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro makes an enthusiastic case for the random orbital sander as the best value power tool available. He provides a concise buying guide with must-have features (8 holes, velcro sandpaper, 5-inch minimum, at least $60) and dismisses unnecessary extras like variable speed or carrying cases. The post explains the tool's versatility—from aggressive material removal with coarse grit to mirror-smooth finishing—all without the complexity of belt sanders. It's an early example of Shapiro's maker culture voice, blending practical advice with humor.
key_points: The random orbital sander offers the best 'chaiminess' (coolness/cost ratio) of any power tool
Key buying criteria: 8-hole, velcro sandpaper, 5-inch, and spend at least $60 to avoid cheap brands
The tool handles both aggressive sanding and fine finishing without swirl marks, replacing the need for multiple specialized sanders
Cheap tools are usually fine, but this is one category where quality matters
money_quotes: The Random Orbital Sander is the single best-bang-for-the-buck powertool out there.
It's 99% of the sanding goodness you'll need in one, handy, under-$100 package.
Chaiminess = coolness / cost. It isn't yiddish, but it should be.
I plan to become rich by coaxing people into bad powertools and retiring on the 5% kickback.
I'm normally a big fan of buying cheap tools, but on this one, trust me–you want the real deal.
posts.key_insight: The random orbital sander is the one power tool that punches far above its price point, handling nearly all sanding tasks without the learning curve or complexity of specialized alternatives.

posts.summary: Shapiro makes a straightforward case for upgrading your tablesaw blade, arguing that a quality 80-tooth blade is the single best improvement you can make to a table saw. He contends that blade quality matters more than the saw itself, noting that a cheap saw with a good blade outperforms an expensive saw with a bad one. The post advises keeping your old low-tooth blade for rough lumber work and using the new blade for hardwoods. It's a short, practical maker tip delivered with Shapiro's characteristic humor.
key_points: A quality 80-tooth blade matters more than the table saw itself — a $400 saw with a good blade beats an $800 saw with a bad one
The stock blade that comes with most table saws is inadequate for quality cuts
Keep two blades: an 80-tooth for finish work on hardwoods and a low-tooth-count blade for rough, dirty lumber
You don't need to experiment with mid-range blades — the two-blade system covers everything
money_quotes: Take it off, hang it on the wall, and save it for when your friend comes over to borrow the table saw.
A $400 table saw with a new 80-tooth blade cuts better than an $800 table saw with a crappy low-tooth-count blade.
You'll find your saw has miraculously started producing perfect, glossy-smooth cuts ready for a glue line.
When you're working with nail-filled dirt-covered twisted two-by-fours, switch back to the old Oort 18-toother.
It's made of compressed meteorites hand-culled from the Oort nebula. That's wonderful.
posts.key_insight: The quality of your blade matters far more than the quality of your saw — upgrading a single consumable component transforms the output of the entire tool.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a DIY woodworking tip inspired by a wedding cake emergency. When his sister-in-law needed a pastry bag the day before his wedding, she improvised with Ziploc bags to frost the cake. Shapiro applies the same principle to woodworking: mixing sawdust from sanding with wood glue in a Ziploc bag creates 'Instant Liquid Wood' that can fill gaps and take stain like the surrounding material. The post is a lighthearted, early-blog-era maker tip that exemplifies Shapiro's tinkerer spirit.
key_points: Ziploc bags can substitute for pastry bags in a pinch — a trick that saved a wedding cake
Mixing 3 parts wood glue to 1 part sawdust creates a gap filler that matches the original wood and takes stain
The glue hardens in the bag tip, naturally sealing it for reuse
Cross-domain problem solving — applying a baking hack to woodworking — is a hallmark of maker thinking
money_quotes: You can turn the dust from your Random Orbital Sander of Eternal Happiness into instant liquid wood.
Just add about 3 parts Titebond (or other caesin glue) to one part sawdust and mix it all up in the bag.
Fill the gap high, since it shrinks when it dries, and sand it down when it's all the same homogenous color.
It even takes stain like the surrounding wood.
The best part is that the glue in the tip of the bag will harden, sealing it up for convenient reuse later.
posts.key_insight: Creative cross-pollination between domains — borrowing a baking improvisation for woodworking — is the essence of maker ingenuity.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro humorously recommends upgrading from a standard fiberglass dust mask to a more comfortable respirator-style mask. He uses Darth Vader as a comedic framing device, arguing that the traditional dust mask is itchy, sweaty, and ineffective. The recommended alternative is more comfortable, works better, and is affordable. The post is a lighthearted product recommendation from Shapiro's early blogging days.
key_points: Traditional fiberglass dust masks are uncomfortable and ineffective
A respirator-style mask is a superior and affordable alternative
Practical maker advice delivered through humor and pop culture references
money_quotes: Do you think Darth Vader would put up with an itchy, sweaty, ineffective piece of fiberglass rubbing against his nose all day? Neither should you.
It is crazy comfortable, it works way better, it's cheap, and you won't be blowing strange-looking black things into the kleenex all day.
posts.key_insight: A small equipment upgrade — swapping a cheap dust mask for a proper respirator — can dramatically improve comfort and effectiveness for makers and builders.

posts.summary: A brief post from 2004 where Dan Shapiro shares a lead on cheap, clear maple boards available near Seattle. The seller offers air dried rough lumber with kiln drying and milling services. Shapiro offers to connect interested woodworkers with the source via email. The post is a straightforward community tip with no broader argument or analysis.
key_points: Shapiro found a source for affordable clear maple lumber near Seattle
The boards are air dried and rough but can be kiln dried and milled on request
The post reflects Shapiro's early involvement in woodworking and maker communities
money_quotes: I found a guy who has a ton of cheap, clear maple boards for sale.
If you're near Seattle and need a hookup, shoot me a mail.
posts.key_insight: A brief community-oriented tip sharing a local source for affordable hardwood lumber, reflecting Shapiro's roots in maker culture long before his tech career prominence.

posts.summary: This is an ultra-short post from 2004 where Shapiro simply links to an external resource about knife sharpening (or avoiding whetstones) with minimal commentary. The post is essentially a link-share with a humorous sign-off telling readers there's nothing more to see. It reflects the early blogging era's casual, link-curation style.
key_points: The entire post is a single external link recommendation with no substantive analysis
Represents the early 2000s blogging convention of short link-posts with brief commentary
The self-aware sign-off ('No extended entry. Go home.') shows Shapiro's characteristic humor
money_quotes: You'll thank me.
That's it. No extended entry. Go home.
posts.key_insight: A playful micro-post from the early blogging era that amounts to a single link recommendation with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Shapiro reveals a clever eBay shopping hack: searching for misspelled product names to find underpriced listings. He demonstrates that many eBay sellers can't spell their products correctly, resulting in fewer bidders and lower prices compared to correctly-listed items or Amazon. The post uses woodworking tools as examples, showing that a planer on eBay with correct spelling costs nearly as much as Amazon after shipping. The core economic insight is that efficient markets work against bargain hunters, so introducing inefficiency through misspelled searches creates opportunity.
key_points: eBay items with correct spelling get bid up to near-retail prices, negating the platform's bargain appeal
Misspelled listings attract fewer bidders, creating genuine deals through market inefficiency
Amazon's free shipping and reliability often make it competitive with or cheaper than eBay for correctly-listed items
Local classifieds can offer even better deals despite higher listing fees, due to lower competition
money_quotes: Don't search for planers. Search for planrs, plainers, playners… have a couple of beers and let your mind roam free.
It turns out that many ebay sellers are spelling-impaired, and their confusion is your gain.
The basic idea here is that efficient markets are the bargain hunter's enemy. The less bidders, the better.
Whether you need a sandr, joyner, resiprikating saw, or just a set of forester bits, don't forget to commune with your inner dyslexic.
Amazon is an actual company that's somewhat less likely to vanish in the middle of the night on a midnight trip to vegas with your benchtop planer.
posts.key_insight: Market inefficiencies created by seller misspellings on eBay are exploitable arbitrage opportunities for savvy buyers.

posts.summary: Shapiro contrasts two kinds of engineering: theoretical calculation and hands-on physical intuition. He illustrates this with stories of bouncing on metal beams to test their strength for a motorized couch, and a classmate who tried to design a grass-cutting device using string on a hand-cranked pole without recognizing the obvious physical impracticality. The post argues that real engineering competence requires getting your hands dirty and testing materials physically, not just running calculations. He closes with a professor's wisdom that the best engineers learn by breaking things deliberately.
key_points: There are two kinds of engineering: desk-and-pencil calculation versus physical, hands-on testing — and competence requires both
Pure theoretical reasoning can lead you astray when you lack physical intuition about how materials and forces actually behave
The best way to understand a new material or technique is to beat on it, break it, and experiment with it directly
The best engineers aren't the ones who never fail — they're the ones who fail deliberately and learn from it
money_quotes: There are two kinds of engineering in this world. One kind takes place at a desk with a pencil and paper. The other takes place when you walk into Home Depot and jump on a wheelbarrow to determine if it can move the three-man rock in your yard without collapsing.
It seemed ballpeen-hammer-to-the-forehead-crease obvious to me that you couldn't cut grass by waving string at it, but not so Ziggy.

Ziggy graduated near the top of our class; he got top marks in almost all his engineering and math classes. But it would never occur to him to bounce on an iron shaft in a scrapyard as a basic design methodology.
Don't be a ziggy.
Good electrical engineers don't blow any transistors in this lab. Bad ones blow four or five apiece. Really outstanding engineers destroy one, and they can tell you exactly how they did it.
Go blow a transistor. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Physical intuition and hands-on experimentation are as essential to good engineering as theoretical calculation — don't be the person who can solve the equations but can't tell that string on a hand crank won't cut grass.

posts.summary: Shapiro shares a simple woodworking project: handmade children's building blocks from scrap hardwood. He walks through the quick process of gluing, cutting, sanding, and finishing blocks from maple and walnut scraps. The post frames the project as an ideal gift for baby showers—something that looks impressive and can't be bought in stores, yet requires minimal effort from anyone with basic shop tools. It's a practical maker tip wrapped in the satisfaction of turning scraps into heirlooms.
key_points: The best handmade gifts require few operations but look expensive or can't be purchased
Children's building blocks can be made from shop scraps in about two hours
Safety consideration: blocks must be larger than mouth-sized to be safe for babies
Simple finishing with shellac or nontoxic natural oils makes scrap wood into a polished gift
money_quotes: One of the good things about being a woodworker is that you can whip out great gifts with very little work.
The trick is to pick the gifts–you want stuff that requires very few operations on the machines, but looks cool and either is very expensive or can't be bought.
I did a lousy job of shellacking them, but baby won't care, right?
Try making a set for your next baby shower. You'll thank me for it.
Let me know if you need a lumber hookup in the Seattle area.
posts.key_insight: The best handmade gifts maximize the gap between perceived value and actual effort—simple woodworking techniques turn shop scraps into heirloom-quality children's toys.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro declares that Ridgid power tools have crossed from the 'Good and Expensive' category to 'Expensive and Crappy.' He notes that Ridgid is moving manufacturing overseas, cutting their warranty from lifetime to two years, expanding retail distribution beyond Home Depot, and maintaining premium prices despite the quality decline. He advises readers to avoid Ridgid and buy competitors like DeWalt instead.
key_points: Power tools fall into two categories: Good and Expensive or Cheap and Crappy, with Ryobi as the rare cheap-but-good exception
Ridgid created a new unwanted category—Expensive and Crappy—by cutting quality while maintaining prices
Ridgid's moves include overseas manufacturing, warranty reduction from lifetime to 2 years, and broader retail distribution
When quality drops but price stays the same, brand loyalty becomes irrational
money_quotes: I'm sorry to say, Ridgid tools are now, officially, crappy.
They are, officially, the first Expensive And Crappy tools.
So why deal with that when a Dewalt is the same price? Beats me.
Steer clear of Ridgid in the future; they've gone to seed and there's better options for the same price.
They actually got consumer report's lowest rating–you know, the 'will spontanously combust while molesting your cat' rating.
posts.key_insight: When a brand cuts quality while keeping premium prices, it creates the worst possible market position—expensive and crappy—and consumers should abandon it immediately.

posts.summary: Shapiro recounts his attempt to build an end-grain butcher block cutting board from oak, only to discover that oak's extremely open pores make it unsuitable for food preparation — shellac soaked straight through the 1.75-inch board. He improvises a fix using diluted wood glue and powdered pumice to fill the pores, then sands it smooth. The post is a lighthearted woodworking cautionary tale that ties into the broader online debate about wood versus plastic cutting boards and food safety. His conclusion: just use maple.
key_points: Oak's open-pore structure makes it a poor choice for cutting boards — it acts like a wooden sponge that harbors bacteria
Shapiro improvised a pore-filling technique using diluted Titebond II glue, powdered pumice, and denatured alcohol to salvage the board
The wood-versus-plastic cutting board debate is a surprisingly contentious topic online, comparable to Linux-versus-Windows arguments
End-grain cutting boards are made by gluing strips, re-cutting perpendicular, rotating so grain faces up, and re-gluing — producing the characteristic small-square pattern
money_quotes: The subject approaches the ever-enlightening 'linux versus windows' debates for elucidating online reading.
It's like a water park for bacteria.
The shellac literally soaked through the 1.75" thick board and dripped out the other side.
Oak is basically a big wooden sponge, and as such not exactly suitable for a sterile chopping block.
Pumice: it's not just for Pompeiians any more.
If you see posts dated after that, the filling process worked and I don't have salmonella.
Just start with maple. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Choosing the right material matters more than clever fixes — oak's open pores make it fundamentally wrong for cutting boards, and no amount of improvisation fully substitutes for starting with the right wood.

posts.summary: Shapiro recounts his frustrating experience shopping for walnut boards to build a dining room table. He visits multiple Seattle lumber retailers, finding each lacking in different ways — limited stock, poor quality, high prices, or unhelpful staff. He concludes that retail wood buying is a poor experience and recommends finding a good sawyer and building a direct relationship with them.
key_points: Direct relationships with sawyers yield better quality wood at lower prices than retail lumber yards
Seattle retail lumber options each had significant drawbacks: limited widths, short lengths, excessive sapwood and wane, or inflated pricing
Even wholesaler relationships have trade-offs — you can reject boards but can't hand-pick them
Knowledgeable staff at lumber yards is unreliable, adding friction to an already difficult sourcing process
money_quotes: So wood buying sucks. Find a nice sawyer and stick with that. You'll thank me for it.
I wasn't aware that I had to ask specifically for someone who could use a measuring tape, which got a laugh.
Their prices aren't always sucky, but man, do they bite the wax tadpole for walnut.
Ultimately not picking your own boards stinks.
posts.key_insight: For quality woodworking materials, direct relationships with sawyers beat retail lumber yards on price, selection, and quality.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a quick price comparison for hook-and-loop sandpaper pads between Lowe's and Home Depot, finding Lowe's offers twice the quantity for the same price with a better brand (Porter Cable vs. Norton). He notes both retailers have poor websites, with Lowe's being worse for online stock listings. Despite generally defaulting to Home Depot for smarter staff and better budget tools, he recommends Lowe's for this specific purchase.
key_points: Lowe's offers 50-pack Porter Cable sandpaper pads for $15 vs. Home Depot's 25-pack Norton pads at the same price
Both retailers have poor websites, with Lowe's being notably worse for online inventory
Shapiro generally prefers Home Depot for staff quality and their Ryobi budget tool brand
Norton sandpaper is dismissed as 'notoriously crappy' compared to Porter Cable
money_quotes: If you need a big pack of sandpaper in a hurry, get it from Lowe's, not Home Depot. You'll thank me for it.
Home Depot and Lowes both have crappy websites, but Lowes is worse.
I was going to fact-check my recollection of HD's lousy prices, but they don't have them on line, so tough for them.
I generally default to Home Depot; they tend to hire smarter people, and their cheap tool brand (Ryobi) is one of the best.
They're Norton sandpaper, which is a notoriously crappy.
posts.key_insight: Even loyal Home Depot customers should comparison-shop on consumables like sandpaper, where Lowe's can offer double the quantity of a better brand at the same price.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a collection of Japanese-style table designs he's bookmarked as inspiration for a personal woodworking project. The post is a simple mood board of furniture from various specialty retailers and craftspeople. He notes his need to learn curve-cutting techniques for future builds. The post is a brief, casual sharing of design references rather than an analytical piece.
key_points: Curates Japanese table designs from multiple specialty furniture makers as inspiration for a personal project
Sources include Green Tea Designs, Haiku Designs, G. Kent Furniture, Ortiz Studios, and Contemporary Tansu
Acknowledges a skills gap in freehand curve-cutting that he needs to address
money_quotes: Need to brainstorm some possible upstairs tables… might as well post the results for everyone to see.
I have to figure out how to cut curves–I hate doing anything freehand, but I'll have to learn sooner or later.
posts.key_insight: A brief glimpse into Shapiro's maker side, using his blog as a public notebook for collecting Japanese furniture design inspiration for a personal woodworking project.

posts.summary: A brief product review update where Shapiro reports that Porter Cable sandpaper he previously considered didn't live up to expectations. He finds it no better than Norton and inferior to Mirka brand sandpaper, which he recommends instead. The post is a casual follow-up to an earlier sandpaper sourcing discussion.
key_points: Porter Cable sandpaper performs no better than Norton brand
Mirka sandpaper remains the superior option among those tested
Mirka is available on Amazon for easy purchasing
money_quotes: I tried the aforementioned PC sandpaper yesterday, and it's no better than the Norton, and nowhere near as good as the Mirka.
posts.key_insight: Among sandpaper brands tested, Mirka significantly outperforms both Porter Cable and Norton.

posts.summary: Shapiro shares a tip about finding inexpensive, high-quality drawer rails online as an alternative to the overpriced, low-quality options at Home Depot. The post is a brief 2004-era product recommendation with a link to a hardware supplier. He notes the rails offer full extension, ball bearing movement, and 100-pound capacity for just over $6. He acknowledges he hasn't personally tested them yet but encourages readers to try them.
key_points: Good drawer rails are disproportionately expensive at retail stores like Home Depot
An online hardware supplier offers full-extension ball bearing rails for around $6
The post is a casual product recommendation, not a tested review
money_quotes: Drawer rails are ridiculously expensive, especially for good ones.
Skip Home Depot and get these instead–you'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: A short maker-culture product tip from 2004 about finding better drawer hardware online for less money than big-box retail.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts accidentally cutting his thumb with a box knife while trimming picture frame splines, resulting in a trip to the ER and five stitches. The incident happened because he was using a knife instead of the proper flush cut saw he'd been too cheap to buy. He shares three lessons learned: always consider where the knife will go if it slips, invest in the right tools instead of improvising, and bring a book to the ER. He publicly shares the embarrassing story hoping it will make him more careful in the future.
key_points: Using the wrong tool because of cost savings led to an avoidable injury
Always anticipate where a cutting tool will go if the workpiece gives way suddenly
Public accountability through blogging about mistakes as a strategy for self-improvement
money_quotes: Don't skimp; buy the right tools for the job.
Bring a book if you have to go to the ER.
Make sure that if the work gives suddenly, the knife doesn't hit you.
One week ago I was considering buying a flush cut saw and decided not to because I'm a cheapskate.
Maybe if I embarass myself publically on my blog, I'll be more careful next time.
posts.key_insight: Skimping on the right tools is a false economy that can cost you far more than what you saved.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his excitement about ordering eCabinet Systems, a CAD software for cabinet design recommended by his woodworker neighbor. The software handles the full workflow from design to manufacturing: CAD modeling, rendered previews, wholesale hardware ordering, cut lists, and CNC mill-ready files. He plans to post a follow-up review once he's used it.
key_points: eCabinet Systems integrates the full cabinet-making pipeline from design through manufacturing preparation
The software was recommended by a semiprofessional woodworker neighbor, suggesting real-world credibility
Shapiro was already using TurboCAD 8.0 for general CAD work, indicating an active interest in digital fabrication tools
money_quotes: Evidently it lets you design your cabinets in a CAD environment, then shows you renderings of the finished cabinets, lets you order all the hardware wholesale, produces cutlists, and ultimately creates files you can send to have the wood milled for you.
posts.key_insight: An early glimpse of Shapiro's longstanding interest in software that bridges digital design and physical fabrication — a thread that would eventually lead to Glowforge.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical advice for amateur woodworkers on sourcing cheap lumber directly from local sawyers rather than retail hardwood stores. He explains that prices can be less than half of retail, with the added benefits of matched boards from single trees and more interesting character wood. The post walks through the key consideration of moisture content, covering kiln-dried, air-dried, and wet wood scenarios. He warns that wet wood requires proper storage and carries risk of cracking and warping, and advises buyers to verify moisture claims with a meter rather than trusting the seller. The post concludes with a concrete example: 100 board feet of air-dried maple for $0.70/bf, cheaper than construction-grade 2x4s.
key_points: Local sawyers offer hardwood at less than half retail prices, with better board matching from single-tree sourcing
Moisture content is the critical variable when buying from sawyers: kiln-dried (6-8%) is ideal, air-dried (10-12%) is acceptable, wet wood is risky even at steep discounts
Air-drying requires one year per inch of thickness with proper stacking and stickering under cover
Buyers should verify moisture claims independently rather than trusting the sawyer's word
A sawyer who hasn't painted log ends to prevent splitting is a red flag for incompetence
money_quotes: Not an ED ad, this is a serious subject for amateur sawdust-makers.
Usually all your wood comes from one tree, so the boards match much better than what you get from the store.
It doesn't matter how long the log has been sitting around, since the wood only really dries when properly stacked and stickered.
If the sawyer hasn't painted the ends of his logs, run-don't-walk since he has no clue what he's doing.
The average sawyer is an enterprising businessman, and as such, may well be lying to you, especially about the moisture content.
I just bought 100 bf of airdried 8/4 maple for $0.70/bf, less than the cost of an equivalent volume of 2x4s.
posts.key_insight: Buying lumber directly from local sawyers can dramatically reduce costs for woodworkers, but requires understanding moisture content and drying methods to avoid expensive mistakes.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a brief cautionary tale about table saw safety after reading about a fatal accident where a splinter kicked back and killed a woodworker. He advocates for wearing a heavy leather shop apron as a potentially life-saving piece of safety equipment. He notes the practical bonus of always having a tape measure handy. The post ends with him convincing a friend to go apron shopping after telling the story to the friend's wife.
key_points: A full-length leather shop apron could prevent fatal injuries from table saw kickback
Storytelling about safety risks is an effective way to motivate others to take precautions
Practical shop safety gear can serve double duty — protection plus convenient tool storage
money_quotes: Nothing's for sure, but a heavy leather shop apron might have saved him.
Plus, you've always got your tape measure handy–beats the hell out of my current system of strategically placing tape measures every five feet around the shop.
Get a heavy apron–you'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Simple, inexpensive safety gear like a leather shop apron can be the difference between a close call and a fatal workshop accident.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a link to a Popular Woodworking nesting tables project that appeals to him as a weekend build. He notes his intent to use solid spalted maple boards he already has on hand, showing his preference for quality materials over the project's default specs. The post is a brief, casual entry reflecting his maker hobby interests.
key_points: Shares a woodworking project for nesting tables from Popular Woodworking magazine
Wryly notes that a 'weekend project' might actually be completable in a weekend
Plans to upgrade the material to solid spalted maple from his personal stock
money_quotes: A weekend project that you might even be able to finish in a weekend!
If I make it, it'll be out of solid maple, though… gotta do something with these 20″x6'6″ spalted maple boards I've got!
posts.key_insight: A brief snapshot of Shapiro's maker side, showing his hands-on woodworking hobby and preference for quality materials.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews his used Ridgid TP-1300 planer, praising it as his favorite power tool. He evaluates it on five criteria: accurate performance, idiot-proof design, graceful failure modes, tolerance of operator error, and the sheer joy of revealing beautiful wood grain. The post doubles as a meditation on what makes a great tool — one that works as advertised, anticipates user mistakes, and delivers magical results. He recommends readers try planing mystery rough-cut lumber, while noting his planer is from the pre-decline Ridgid era.
key_points: A great power tool performs as advertised, is designed for imperfect users, and fails gracefully rather than catastrophically
Graceful failure is critical — the planer protects the user by stopping rollers when overloaded rather than destroying the workpiece
The deepest joy in woodworking is the reveal: discovering spectacular beauty hidden beneath the rough exterior of raw lumber
Good tool design anticipates operator stupidity and makes it hard to produce bad results
money_quotes: It fails gracefully. This is critical for me, since I fail often, and so must my tools.

There is no immediate gratification in woodworking like taking something that looks like a the mutant offspring of a fuzzy caterpiller and a railroad tie and discovering it's flame maple with a burl inclusion.
It's truly incredible that you can reveal such spectacular beauty from beneath what is in fact the rotted carcass of a dead plant.
Power tools do work. Great power tools do VOODOO MAGIC.
It's really hard to feed the wood in in a way that makes the planer upset — at an angle, diagonally, it's all good.
It does good work despite operator stupidity.
posts.key_insight: The best tools are ones that fail gracefully, tolerate imperfect users, and transform raw material into something revelatory.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a woodworking lesson about sandpaper replacement frequency. He discovered that sandpaper loses its effectiveness after just five to ten minutes of use, and that he had been sanding with essentially dead paper for most of his sessions. By changing sandpaper frequently, sanding tasks become dramatically faster and produce better finishes. The post is part of a broader theme about not skimping on disposable shop supplies.
key_points: Sandpaper has only 5-10 minutes of effective cutting life per sheet, far less than most people assume
Using worn-out sandpaper leads to compensating with overly coarse grits, which causes gouges when fresh paper is applied
Changing sandpaper frequently allows use of finer grits and produces better finishes in far less time
Cheap PSA (sticky) sandpaper may be preferable to hook-and-loop because lower cost encourages more frequent replacement
money_quotes: Turns out that what I thought was the 'normal' part of sanding was actually me rubbing dead limp sandless paper repeatedly over wood.
Depending on the quality of your sandpaper, you have five to ten minutes of serious sanding each time you change the pad.
After that time, you may as well switch over to Charmin, because you're not sanding squat.
When you try doing it right–changing sandpaper every five to ten minutes–your sanding tasks will take roughly one four hundredth as long as they did before.
Buy a crateful of sandpaper, change it constantly, and learn to love sanding again.
posts.key_insight: The hidden cost of skimping on disposable supplies is not the money saved but the vastly greater time wasted working with degraded tools.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews eCabinet Systems' free cabinet design software after spending a week learning it. He finds it genuinely free with no strings attached and easy to install, but plagued by bugs, crashes, and poor error handling. The UI is a mix of Windows-standard and AutoCAD-compatible conventions with questionable design choices, though he notes it's learnable. Despite the rough edges, he's enthusiastic about using it to design bookcases, and mentions his background as a lead program manager for the Windows XP UI at Microsoft.
key_points: eCabinet Systems software is genuinely free with no hidden costs, though purchasing materials through it isn't the cheapest option
The software suffers from crashes, bugs, and poor error handling — their registration server was offline for three days with no user-facing error message
The UI makes questionable choices like using buttons instead of tabs and inconsistent naming, but remains learnable
The 350+ page learning guide is invaluable and estimated to take about 10 hours to work through
Shapiro deliberately downplays his UI expertise, revealing only at the end that he was lead program manager for the Windows XP interface at Microsoft
money_quotes: It really is free; there appear to be no strings attached whatsoever.
It crashes periodically (bad), but doesn't appear to corrupt the files when it goes down (good).
I couldn't register the software for three days because their server was offline, but it just hung instead of telling me that.
I suspect Thermwood needs to hire some good dedicated testers.
All that being said, it's not completely crazy and is quite learnable.
posts.key_insight: Even free, feature-rich software loses trust through poor error handling and UI inconsistencies — problems that dedicated testing and design attention could easily fix.

posts.summary: Shapiro shares his discovery of Freeplans.com, a service that emails free woodworking plans supported by advertising. He reports being pleasantly surprised that the first plan he received was high-quality, with clear instructions and pictures for building a cherry bookcase. He also explains his privacy-conscious approach of using a disposable 'tearoff' email address to detect if the service sells his information.
key_points: Freeplans.com delivers genuinely useful free woodworking plans funded by ads rather than data selling
Shapiro uses disposable email addresses as a practical technique to detect spam sources and protect privacy
Early web services could earn trust through simple, transparent business models
money_quotes: Freeplans.com actually appears not to be a rip-off.
I used a 'tearoff' email address, so I'll know if they sell my name.
posts.key_insight: Even in the early web era, Shapiro's maker curiosity and tech-savvy privacy habits were already evident in how he approached free online resources.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares inspiration images of Japanese step tansu chests from various furniture retailers, considering one for an upcoming woodworking or maker project. The brief post is essentially a mood board of tansu designs he finds appealing. He acknowledges his growing backlog of projects but embraces it, noting that the design phase is enjoyable in itself.
key_points: Shapiro curates reference images of step tansu chests from multiple Asian furniture retailers
The post reflects his hands-on maker mentality — always planning the next physical project
He openly acknowledges accumulating a backlog of projects but sees designing as its own reward
money_quotes: Yeah, I realize I'm accumulating quite a backlog of projects. That's OK, designing is half the fun.
posts.key_insight: For a dedicated maker, the design and inspiration phase is as rewarding as the build itself.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro provides a comprehensive buyer's guide to cordless power tools, written for a non-expert audience. He recommends which tools to buy cordless (drill is essential, reciprocating saw is useful, circular saw is questionable), ranks major brands by quality and price, and shares strategies for getting the best deals. The core advice is to stick with one brand for battery compatibility, with Porter-Cable and Ryobi as top picks at different price points. He warns against formerly reputable brands like Black & Decker, Craftsman, and Ridgid that have declined in quality.
key_points: A cordless drill/screwdriver is the one essential cordless tool; circular saws drain batteries too fast to justify going cordless for most users
Battery incompatibility between brands makes it important to commit to one brand ecosystem for all cordless tools
Ryobi offers the best value in budget tools—cheap enough to replace but durable enough for non-professional use
Several formerly respected brands (Black & Decker, Craftsman, Ridgid) have declined significantly in quality while not always lowering prices
Combo packs offer dramatically better value than buying individual tools and batteries separately
money_quotes: You can buy just about everything cordless these days, but that doesn't mean you should.
A contractor I know put it to me this way: 'I can buy a Milwaukee and it'll last me 10 years, or I can buy a Ryobi for a quarter of the price and have it last 3 years.'
What will last him 3 years of heavy-duty work will probably last you or me 10 years, so I'm a big fan of Ryobi.
Black and Decker is scraping the bottom of the barrel; they charge the least and, in my experience, offer the very worst quality tools.
Last in the 'how the mighty have fallen' category is Ridgid, who started out producing very high quality tools at medium-high prices.
They just outsourced all their production to China, sold the company, and reduced their warranty from lifetime to 2 years–but didn't lower their prices.
posts.key_insight: When buying cordless tools, commit to one brand ecosystem for battery compatibility, and for most homeowners, budget brands like Ryobi deliver professional-grade durability at a fraction of the cost.

posts.summary: Shapiro continues his review of eCabinet Systems software, documenting his experience working through its extensive 350+ page tutorial. While impressed by the system's flexibility for designing cabinets with sheet goods, he identifies a practical gap: the software doesn't adequately address how to finish exposed plywood edges. He notes ongoing crash issues but remains cautiously optimistic, withholding final judgment until he produces an actual cabinet with the tool.
key_points: eCabinet Systems offers impressive flexibility despite stability issues and a steep learning curve
The software has a blind spot around finishing details like exposed plywood edges — edgebanding, veneer, or solid wood edging
Real validation of design software comes from producing a physical result, not just mastering the interface
money_quotes: It does still have bad problems with crash-prone-ness, but saving frequently solves most of that.
What the heck do you do with all the exposed plywood edges?
The real proof is when I produce my first cabinet with it.
posts.key_insight: Design software is only as good as its ability to address real-world fabrication details — the gap between a nice digital model and a finished physical object is where tools prove their worth.

posts.summary: Shapiro describes his experience cutting splines — thin strips of contrasting wood used to strengthen picture frame corners. He walks through the practical challenges of cutting thin strips on a table saw safely, particularly the kickback danger of placing the thin piece between the blade and fence. After wasting wood on multiple failed attempts, he shares a workaround: cutting the spline groove but not all the way through, then finishing with a hand saw and chisel to avoid repeated fence adjustments.
key_points: Splines are contrasting wood inserts that strengthen mitered corners, especially important when wood may not be fully dried
The safe way to cut thin strips (skinny side out) makes precise measurement and repeatable cuts frustratingly difficult
His workaround of partial table saw cuts finished by hand tools trades efficiency for safety and repeatability
Self-deprecating humor about using primitive hand methods when a table saw is right there highlights the practical compromises of woodworking
money_quotes: Not the curve, but the slice o'contrasting beauty you stick in the corner of something to strengthen it.
The obvious way to do that is put the fence 1/8 of an inch away from the sawblade, and that's an excellent recipe for turning your spline into a projectile weapon.
Remember the rule, skinny side on the outside. Anything else results in kickback.
Then I use a sharpened chunk of obsidian tied to a twig with dried sinews to hew off the last bit, and repeat.
It still seams backwards when I have a perfectly good table saw sitting in front of me.
posts.key_insight: In woodworking, the safest and most repeatable approach often means abandoning the obvious power-tool method in favor of a hybrid technique that trades speed for precision and safety.

posts.summary: Shapiro recounts his experience with a cheap Ryobi bandsaw that proved useless for cutting curved pieces from thick lumber but turned out to be perfect for trimming splines on picture frames. The blade wandered too much for precision work on 8/4 stock but handled the delicate spline-trimming task cleanly. He shares a practical tip from a friend about starting flush-cut saw cuts from the loose end to avoid breaking spline corners. The post is a lighthearted reminder that the right tool for the job isn't always the best tool — sometimes a junky tool finds its niche.
key_points: A cheap bandsaw that fails at heavy cutting tasks can excel at lighter, more forgiving work like spline trimming
When trimming splines with a flush-cut saw, always start cutting from the loose hanging end to avoid breaking the corner
The post illustrates the maker principle of finding unexpected uses for imperfect tools rather than discarding them
money_quotes: The sawblade was wandering around the lumber like a drunken fratboy on mardi gras.
I ran the only-slightly-bloodied edge of that old picture frame along the junky bandsaw blade, and it was a match made in heaven.
The darn thing sheared off perfectly.
Be very careful to start cutting at the loose hanging end. If you don't, then you risk breaking off the tip of the corner of the spline as you finish the second cut.
I mentioned it in my post of a few days ago, not actually realizing that I did have a functional-albeit-crappy bandsaw in my basement at the time. Oh well, I learned.
posts.key_insight: An imperfect tool isn't a useless tool — matching the right task to the right (even crappy) equipment can yield surprisingly good results.

posts.summary: Shapiro documents ongoing frustrations with eCabinets software, a cabinet design tool he's been evaluating. He catalogs four major issues: frequent crashes tied to the render engine, poor performance from naive rebuild-everything architecture, alarming bugs that corrupt files, and missing features like closed box construction. Despite the litany of problems, he notes the vendor is responsive with fixes and concludes the benefits still outweigh the drawbacks.
key_points: The software crashes frequently due to render engine issues, but the lack of deterministic crash files makes bug reporting difficult
Performance suffers from a lazy architecture that rebuilds everything from scratch instead of tracking dirty state
An easily reproducible bug that corrupts files suggests inadequate testing methodology
The vendor's willingness to quickly hack out fixes partially compensates for shipping buggy software
money_quotes: Clearly, they're not marking dirty bits and only doing the necessary work; they're rebuilding the whole shmeer each time anything might have been changed.
This is unnerving because it's easy to reproduce, and makes me think they may not have a robust testing methadology. Or any testing methadology.
If you're going to ship buggy software, you better be prepared to fix the bugs fast.
You can't make a six-sided box. Kind of an odd limitation.
Generally, the software just feels slow and buggy. They do appear to be hard at work fixing this, and I hope they're successful.
posts.key_insight: Shipping buggy software is forgivable if you fix bugs fast, but easily reproducible corruption bugs suggest a lack of any real testing methodology.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his excitement about purchasing a Jet 12-inch bandsaw along with Timberwolf blades and an upgraded fence. He explains the key technical considerations for bandsaw blades—thickness for tracking straight lines versus cutting curves, and tooth set width for turning ease versus minimizing wood waste. The post is a brief maker's journal entry about tooling up for a planned Japanese-styled table with tricky curves.
key_points: Acquired a Jet 12-inch bandsaw with Timberwolf blades and upgraded fence for a Japanese table project
Blade thickness determines tracking: thicker blades cut straighter but handle curves poorly
Tooth set width affects turning ability and wood waste—wider set aids curves but wastes more material
Learning by doing: jumping into a challenging curved-cut project as a way to master the tool
money_quotes: I suppose there's no better way to learn than to try it out.
The secret seems to be in the blades.
A 'thick' blade tracks straighter, which is good if you're cutting straight lines but bad if you're cutting narrow curves.
posts.key_insight: An early glimpse of Shapiro's maker mindset—diving into unfamiliar tools and learning through ambitious hands-on projects—years before founding Glowforge.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro enthusiastically recommends the Death Stick EX-9 'Exhumer,' a cat's paw style nail puller tool. He describes how the tool works by digging prongs under a nail head and leveraging it out. The post is a brief, lighthearted product recommendation that highlights the tool's cool name and built-in bottle opener as bonus features.
key_points: The Exhumer is a cat's paw nail puller that uses prongs and leverage to extract nails from wood
The tool's branding and name are part of its appeal, even if comparable tools exist
Practical tools with personality (cool name, bottle opener) make better recommendations
money_quotes: Yup, I'm serious. I've got one, and you should too.
There are probably lots of other cat's paw nail pullers that are just as good, but none have such a cool name. Or a built-in bottle opener.
posts.key_insight: A brief, playful tool recommendation showing Shapiro's maker culture roots and appreciation for well-branded physical products.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro answers a reader's question about why her planer keeps tearing chunks from the corners of boards during final thickness passes. He offers two theories: moisture absorption at board ends weakening the wood, and the possibility of heavily figured wood causing tearout. He provides practical solutions including using sacrificial scrap lumber, checking blade sharpness, and avoiding thick passes. The post showcases Shapiro's self-deprecating humor, admitting to only four months of planer ownership while confidently diagnosing the problem.
key_points: Lumber absorbs moisture faster at the ends, potentially weakening those areas and making them prone to chunking during planing
Figured wood with undulating grain creates two cut points per pass, leading to tearout that worsens as boards get thinner
Attaching sacrificial scrap lumber to the back of boards can protect valuable wood from end-grain damage
Dull blades exacerbate tearout by 'whacking more and cutting less'
money_quotes: As someone who's owned a planer for nearly four months and planed literally dozens of board-feet of lumber, I feel fully qualified to answer your question.
By the time you're at the end of the board, you don't have half a tree of lumber behind you to hold the whole thing in place.
In figured wood the grain looks like misbehaving spaghetti; it actually undulates back and forth.
Your gorgeous piece of curly lumber starts resembling a lunar landscape after a (very noisy) pass through the machine.
Dull blades will cause/exacerbate both of these problems, since you're whacking more and cutting less.
Your questions are welcome, as long as you don't mind an incorrect answer.
posts.key_insight: Chunking at board ends during final planer passes likely results from moisture-weakened end grain or figured wood tearout, both solvable with sacrificial backing boards and sharp blades.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro introduces a multi-part blog series documenting how he built a custom coffee table with a slate inset. He shares the blueprints for the project under a Creative Commons license, allowing others to build from his plans but not modify or sell them commercially. The post is largely an introduction and table of contents for the series, with self-deprecating humor about his technical drawings. He notes the plans were created in TurboCAD v8 and explains his licensing rationale based on past experiences with people reselling free work.
key_points: Shapiro shares open blueprints for a handmade coffee table with slate inset, his first major furniture project
The plans are released under a Creative Commons license that permits reproduction but prohibits commercial use and modification
He justifies the restrictive licensing by citing experiences with people relabeling and reselling free work
The series spans four parts covering design, construction, assembly, and finishing
money_quotes: The profs who taught me proper GD&T will kick my ass when they see it.
Still, it was good enough to print out, scatter copies of around the shop, spill shellac on, and ultimately produce a table from.
I've seen too many people have their free work relabeled and resold for me to act otherwise.
posts.key_insight: Shapiro's early maker ethos is evident in openly sharing furniture blueprints while carefully protecting them with Creative Commons licensing — balancing generosity with hard-won lessons about free work being exploited.

posts.summary: This is Part II of a multi-part series where Dan Shapiro documents building a custom walnut and slate coffee table. The post walks through the woodworking process from selecting and preparing lumber through gluing up the tabletop. He details a clever technique using dado cuts to create perfectly square recesses for slate tile inlays that look hand-chiseled but are actually done quickly on a table saw. The writing blends practical woodworking instruction with humor and personality, revealing Shapiro's deep enthusiasm for the craft.
key_points: Walnut lumber grading is done on the best side, meaning the worst side may have more character than expected — a trap for the unwary buyer
The dado blade technique for creating slate tile recesses is an elegant shortcut that produces results indistinguishable from painstaking hand-chisel work
Unreinforced glue joints in wood are actually stronger than the wood itself, as demonstrated by his hammer test on cutoffs
Incremental glue-up (one board at a time) trades speed for precision and is recommended over gluing everything at once
money_quotes: Woodworkers call that side the 'bottom'.
It'll look like you carefully chiseled out the opening with handtools and got 12 perfectly square corners, but you actually did the whole thing in an hour with your table saw.
I whacked the seam with a hammer, and the wood broke away from the seam — as advertised, the (unreinforced) glue joint was stronger than the wood itself.
I'll be damned if I was going to let an opportunity to use my new biscuit joiner pass me by.
Or if you the swashbuckling, daring, buy-lots-of-lumber-because-you-screwed-it-up-the-first-time type, just glue the whole sucker up at once.
Mix yourself a pastry bag of the finest liquid walnut and start gooping it in every nook and cranny you can find.
posts.key_insight: Clever use of power tools and technique can produce results that look like painstaking handcraft — the smartest woodworking is knowing which shortcuts are invisible.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro details the final construction steps of a custom coffee table with inlaid slate tiles. The post covers leg construction from laminated 6/4 stock, attachment techniques using hidden screws beneath the slate, and slate tile preparation from inexpensive Home Depot tiles. The bulk of the article focuses on finishing with shellac, including dissolving flakes, pore-filling with pumice, and a simplified French polish technique. The post is written as a practical woodworking tutorial with humorous asides about bug excretions, potable alcohol, and using a red sock as a finishing pad.
key_points: Leg construction uses laminated 6/4 stock glued in two directions, with a 4/4 cap on the bottom to minimize exposed endgrain
Screws attaching legs to the tabletop are cleverly hidden beneath the slate tile inlays
Shellac finishing involves dissolving flakes in denatured alcohol, filling pores with pumice, then building up layers using a simplified French polish method
Inexpensive ungauged slate tiles from Home Depot are mounted with clear caulk piles that allow future replacement
money_quotes: Once this dries up, nothing short of a flaming jackhammer will cause your table distress.
Technically you can return the grinder to directly to coffee use, although the spouse might not appreciate you contaminating his/her coffee with bug excretions.
Ironically, the only toxic thing about shellac is the methyl alcohol deliberately added to de-tipple the otherwise potable ethyl alcohol.
If you have a source of 100% pure ethanol, you can use that instead of denatured alcohol for a great finish (and a hell of a party).
In a moment of bizarre experimentation, I decided to apply the shellac with a red wool sock wrapped in an old T-shirt. It worked, but my table smells like an old foot.
Jointing endgrain is one of the sketchiest jointing tasks.
posts.key_insight: Building quality furniture is accessible with basic materials and techniques, and the imperfect, personality-filled process is as much the point as the finished product.

posts.summary: This is the fourth and final installment in a series about building a custom slate-and-wood coffee table. The post is essentially a photo showcase of the completed project, presenting corner detail and isometric views of the finished piece. Shapiro invites readers who are inspired to try similar projects to share their results, and offers to provide more detailed instructions to anyone attempting the build. It's a brief, celebratory wrap-up to a multi-part DIY furniture project.
key_points: The series exceeded its planned 3-part scope, humorously acknowledged in the title
The final post focuses on showcasing the completed coffee table through photographs
Shapiro offers to help others replicate the project with additional instructions on request
money_quotes: Part IV in a 3-or-so part post describing how I made our coffee table.
And finally, the best part of all… the pictures of the final product.
If you're inspired by this project to try something similar, drop me a line and show me your results!
posts.key_insight: A hands-on DIY furniture project wraps up with pride in the finished product and an invitation for others to try building their own.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical tip about affordable plywood available at Home Depot. He describes maple/birch 4x8 sheets available for around $35, noting their limitations but emphasizing their value compared to other retail sources. He recommends the plywood for shop use, jigs, and cabinet backs, and notes the convenience of Home Depot's cutting service.
key_points: Home Depot carries maple/birch plywood sheets for ~$35, the cheapest retail option with two presentable sides
The plywood has limitations (internal voids, medium veneer, only 5 ply) but is practical for shop use and light cabinetry
Home Depot's cutting service adds convenience since cutting 4x8 panels is difficult in home shops
money_quotes: If you compare to just about any other retail source, this is the cheapest you can find plywood with two presentable sides.
Buy a sheet for your next project. You'll thank me for it.
I just bat my lashes and they don't charge me a thing.
posts.key_insight: Affordable maple/birch plywood at Home Depot offers hobbyist woodworkers a practical material with two presentable sides at a price point well below other retail sources.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recommends a niche woodworking website's 'Wood of the Month' feature from a trade magazine he discovered at a tool sale. He notes the magazine is narrowly targeted at high-volume professional woodworkers and mostly not relevant to him, but finds the wood profiles interesting. He also mentions the publisher offers free subscriptions to subtly different magazine variants for different woodworking specialties.
key_points: Recommends the 'Wood of the Month' section from iswonline, a niche woodworking trade publication
Found the magazine at the Grizzly Tent Sale, a woodworking tool event
Notes the publisher segments nearly identical content into slightly different trade magazine variants
money_quotes: A magazine that you probably haven't read produces a site that you probably haven't found.
They don't want to let you sign up for all of them, probably because they're all quite similar.
posts.key_insight: An early glimpse into Shapiro's maker culture interests, sharing a niche woodworking resource years before founding laser-cutter company Glowforge.

posts.summary: A very brief post from 2004 where Dan Shapiro promotes the Rechargeable Battery Recycling Corporation (RBRC) as a resource for recycling cordless power tool batteries. He encourages readers to recycle their batteries to prevent heavy metals from entering the environment, with a lighthearted nod to local wildlife benefiting from the effort.
key_points: Cordless power tool batteries contain heavy metals that can harm the environment if not properly disposed of
The Rechargeable Battery Recycling Corporation (RBRC) offers a recycling solution for these batteries
Small individual actions like battery recycling contribute to protecting local ecosystems
money_quotes: Your local forest, stream, creek, or spotted owl nest will thank me for it
posts.key_insight: Responsible disposal of power tool batteries through dedicated recycling programs helps keep heavy metals out of local ecosystems.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts a dinner at Palisade, a Seattle seafood restaurant he recommends for tourists. He describes the restaurant's panoramic waterfront views, live fish displays, and excellent seafood. The core of the post is his discovery that the restaurant's extensive woodwork is made entirely of Koa, an expensive Hawaiian hardwood he had coincidentally just read about. He jokes about wanting a fish-it-yourself restaurant and closes with affectionate asides about his mother-in-law and his own small stash of Koa wood.
key_points: Palisade is recommended as Seattle's premier tourist restaurant for its views, live marine displays, and fresh seafood
The restaurant is extensively decorated with Koa, a rare and expensive Hawaiian hardwood at $30/board foot
Shapiro's woodworking knowledge let him identify the wood, and a coincidental article he'd read confirmed his guess
The post is an early personal blog entry showing Shapiro's interests in craft, materials, and maker culture before his later tech career prominence
money_quotes: That's what I want, a fish-it-yourself restaurant.
According to our server, the Maitre d' stand alone is $10k worth of lumber.
Great seafood, but not quite perfect–a tiny bit dumbed-down for the masses. Perfect for tourists.
I've got a 2 bf bit of koa in my garage just waiting for a project…
My mother-in-law is a delightful person who I enjoy spending time with. No, I am not being sarcastic. No, she does not read this weblog. Yes, I am very, very lucky.
posts.key_insight: An early personal blog post revealing Shapiro's eye for craftsmanship and materials — interests that would later manifest in his founding of Glowforge.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his excitement about acquiring a Forrest Woodworker II table saw blade, obtained through a barter arrangement with a co-worker who used his workshop. He notes the blade's overwhelmingly positive reviews and plans to use it for his next project, a dining room table. He expresses curiosity about its claimed versatility with plywood despite having fewer teeth than typical sheet goods blades.
key_points: Acquired a highly-rated Forrest Woodworker II blade through a workshop-sharing barter arrangement
The blade has 63 uniformly positive reviews claiming it's the best general-purpose woodworking blade
Questions whether a single blade can truly handle both solid wood and plywood, noting sheet goods typically require 60+ tooth blades
money_quotes: According to the reviews (all 63 of them!) this is *the* best blade for just about any woodworking task.
I'm curious about it's claims to be great on plywood too–usually sheet goods blades are 60+ tooth
posts.key_insight: A short, excited post about acquiring a well-regarded woodworking blade, reflecting Shapiro's hands-on maker sensibility and willingness to barter shop access for quality tools.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts a trip to the Grizzly woodworking tool tent sale with a friend, describing the bargain-hunting culture around discounted industrial machinery. He explains that Grizzly imports Chinese tools at low prices with American motors and their own QA, and the tent sale is where they unload damaged or unsellable inventory at even steeper discounts. The post captures the frenzy of early-bird shoppers who camp out for the best deals, and shares a tactical tip about arriving at 10 AM to catch re-tagged items that first-round buyers abandoned. It's a lighthearted slice-of-life piece from Shapiro's woodworking hobby days, well before his later ventures in tech and AI.
key_points: Grizzly imports Chinese machinery with American motors and in-house QA, offering decent quality at low prices
The tent sale moves surplus, damaged, and demo inventory at steep discounts, with the best deals gone in 15 minutes
A tactical hack: arriving at 10 AM lets you get first pick of re-tagged items abandoned by early shoppers
The American-made tool market is muddied by imports even from premium brands like Delta and Jet
money_quotes: The tent sale is their chance to unload the planer that rusted on the way from Shanghai, the tablesaw that has minor bloodstains from the troublesome Safety-Shield demo, and everything else that can't be sold through normal channels.
Brave souls line up at 8 in the morning, even camping out the night before, and charge in first thing in a fit of scavenging fury.
They burst forth with literally tons of machinery, and the checkout/loading line can take an hour or more.
If someone tears off the tag from a machine but doesn't pay for it, after two hours, it gets re-tagged and you can buy it again.
As a rule of thumb, if you can't be there at 8:00, be there at 10:00 (or 3:00 when they'll haggle over what's left).
posts.key_insight: The best bargains at competitive sales often go not to the earliest arrivals but to those who understand the system's second-chance mechanics.

posts.summary: In this follow-up post, Shapiro recounts his friend's experience at a Grizzly woodworking tool tent sale, where apparent bargains require careful inspection. His friend bought a seemingly great deal on a longbed jointer marked down from $800 to $500, only to discover it was missing blades, a pulley, and had a cracked cast-iron base — adding $200 in parts back to the cost. The post serves as a cautionary tale about reading defect descriptions carefully and thoroughly inspecting discounted machinery before purchasing. Shapiro advises future tent sale shoppers to print parts diagrams ahead of time for proper checkout.
key_points: Grizzly tent sale items come with detailed defect lists from technicians, but buyers must still inspect thoroughly
A jointer marked down to $500 ended up costing closer to $700 after missing and broken parts were accounted for
Ambiguous defect descriptions like 'cracked base' can refer to critical components rather than cosmetic ones
Preparation — like printing parts diagrams in advance — is essential for evaluating discounted tools
money_quotes: But woe betide the one who doesn't inspect it adequately first, or who doesn't read the description carefully!
The 'cracked base' that the description mentioned referred not to the sheet-metal stand, but rather the actual cast-iron jointer base.
I came out gaining nothing but wisdom, but maybe I'll try for a deal on a drum sander next year.
If you do decide to hit the tent sale, prepare by printing out the parts diagrams of any machine you're interested in and do a thorough checkout before you buy.
I did pick up some tung oil and a pair of tweezers with a magnifying glass attached, but that was from their regular store, so I don't think it counts.
posts.key_insight: A bargain on used tools is only a bargain if you inspect thoroughly enough to understand what you're actually getting — ambiguous defect descriptions can hide deal-breaking problems.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his real-time impressions of receiving and testing a Forrester Woodworker II table saw blade. He documents the experience chronologically from unboxing through first cuts, noting the excessive grease on the blade, its impressively quiet operation, and its excellent performance cutting walnut. While the blade doesn't leave quite as mirror-smooth a finish as his previous 80-tooth Freud blade, he concludes it's a delightful tool that meets expectations even if it falls slightly short of the hype.
key_points: The Forrester WWII blade comes heavily greased, making for a messy unboxing experience
The blade runs noticeably quieter than other blades, with less rattle and scrape
With 40 teeth versus 80, the blade trades mirror-smooth finish for cutting power, leaving tiny scratches at 1-2mm spacing
For ripping thick hardwood like 5/4 walnut, the blade performs excellently and is a joy to use
money_quotes: Damn, those carbide tips are HUGE.
Guess these pants just moved from the 'office clothes' pile to the 'woodworking clothes' pile.
Oh, sweet walnut, parts like butter before my WWII.
It's delightful and a joy to use, although not quite as miraculous as I had expected.
posts.key_insight: The Forrester Woodworker II is an excellent ripping blade that trades finish quality for quiet operation and powerful cutting, making it a strong but not miraculous upgrade for serious woodworkers.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts a painful workshop injury sustained while building a dining room table. While tightening pipe clamps on a glue-up, a crescent wrench slipped and slammed his fingernail into a neighboring clamp. He had to finish clamping before the glue dried, then visited a doctor who laughed at his question about losing the nail. The post serves as a cautionary tale that hand tools can be just as dangerous as power tools.
key_points: A crescent wrench slip during a table glue-up caused a serious fingernail injury requiring a hand specialist
Time pressure from drying glue forced him to finish the job before seeking medical attention
Hand tools deserve the same respect and caution as power tools
money_quotes: When I asked the doctor if I was going to lose the nail, he laughed at me.
You need to be every bit as careful with hand tools as you do with power tools.
I can't believe I did all this damage to my hand with a crescent wrench!
The glue was drying, so I had to finish tightening the other six clamps before I could haul my butt to the doctor's office.
I spent the whole weekend taking Vicodin and playing Baldur's Gate (fortunately the ring finger isn't used on the xbox controller).
posts.key_insight: Hand tools deserve the same respect as power tools — complacency around seemingly safe equipment can still lead to serious injury.

posts.summary: Shapiro evaluates four wood joinery methods—plain glue, biscuits, dowels, and splines—for gluing up hardwood boards into flat tabletops. He grades each on strength, ease of use, cost, invisibility, and most importantly alignment. After extensive hands-on testing, he concludes that dowels with a high-quality jig (the Dowelmax) deliver the best results, particularly for alignment. The post is bookended by a lengthy, darkly comic account of a woodworking injury involving a lost fingernail and a cavalier hand surgeon.
key_points: Plain glue joints are strong enough but produce poor alignment, leading to excessive sanding and wavy surfaces
Biscuits offer good strength but their swelling effect can push boards out of alignment during glue-up, making them better suited for smaller projects like picture frames
Dowels with a precision jig provide dead-on alignment every time with virtually no effort, making them the clear winner for tabletop construction
The weakest part of a glue joint isn't the glue itself but the wood immediately adjacent to it, near the board's edge
money_quotes: Wood glue joints are typically stronger than the wood itself. So why bother strengthening the joint?
The point of a strong joint isn't actually the joint, but rather the areas near the joint.
You'll notice the wood fracture a few millimeters from the glue line. While it's nice to know your glue didn't fail, it's small compensation when your table is sitting on the floor in two pieces.
Cutting lumber is like trimming a turkey, and you sometimes get bits of bone in the meat and vice versa.
Rushing and woodworking not being my favorite combination, I'm saving biscuits for picture frames.
The Dowelmax rocked my world. Save the money; don't buy a biscuit joiner.
He lost it. I had no idea we'd be needing it again or I would have offered to hold on to it for him.
posts.key_insight: For gluing up hardwood tabletops, alignment matters more than joint strength, and a precision dowel jig outperforms biscuits and plain glue on the metric that actually counts.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he has switched his blog's RSS syndication to Feedburner's smartfeed system. He explains the change at escalating levels of technical detail, from 'nothing changed' for casual readers to redirect specifics for RSS power users. The post is a practical tech update wrapped in humor, advising readers to point their newsreaders at his canonical URL rather than the Feedburner URL directly, so he can revert easily if needed. It's a snapshot of mid-2000s blogging infrastructure concerns.
key_points: Shapiro migrated his blog syndication to Feedburner's smartfeed, which auto-converts between RSS, Atom, and other formats
He structures the explanation in escalating 'geekiness levels' so readers can stop when it stops being relevant to them
He advises readers to use his canonical URL rather than the Feedburner URL directly, preserving his ability to switch providers without breaking feeds
The move was partly motivated by wanting aggregated reader statistics to track his audience size
money_quotes: Read until it stops making sense or you stop caring.
I don't get Google ad revenue since you're not visiting my page, but that's OK, 'cause you're my BOY and I LOVE you.
It also lets me aggregate my site statistics so I can see how many readers I have on my feed, thereby feeding my monstrous, out-of-control ego.
Sometimes you just need to give your brain a break. No, I'm not still on vicodin.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted 2004 blog housekeeping post about RSS feed migration that captures the era's blogging culture and infrastructure concerns.

posts.summary: This is a very brief technical update post from 2004 where Dan Shapiro notes that Feedburner is troubleshooting missing newlines in his blog's new RSS feed. The post is essentially a status update to readers experiencing feed issues. It thanks readers for their patience and points them to subscription options. There is no substantive argument or analysis in this post.
key_points: Feedburner was troubleshooting missing newlines in the blog's RSS feed
The post serves as a brief status update to affected readers
money_quotes: The good folks at Feedburner are troubleshooting the missing newlines in the new feed.
posts.key_insight: This is a minor technical status update about RSS feed formatting issues, with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a deal on a Porter Cable random orbital sander found on his wife's Amazon deals blog. He references a previous post about his enthusiasm for the tool and recommends it to readers, while noting he hasn't comparison-shopped the price against other retailers.
key_points: Recommends a Porter Cable random orbital sander deal found via his wife's weekly Amazon deals blog
References his own prior post expressing deep enthusiasm for the sander
Adds an honest caveat that better prices might exist at other retailers like Home Depot
money_quotes: Today her posting includes a good price on a Porter Cable Sander of Infinite Wisdom and Joyous Wonder.
It meets all my ROS sander requirements and is well reviewed.
If you're in need, pick one up. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted product recommendation post reflecting Shapiro's early blogging about maker tools and woodworking enthusiasm.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a project by his friend Frank who built a 6-foot-long rotisserie out of two bicycles, a washing machine motor, and stainless steel. They used it to cook 140 pounds of lamb for Shapiro's wedding. The post is a brief personal share with a link to Frank's project page, notable mainly as an early example of Shapiro's interest in maker culture and creative engineering.
key_points: A DIY rotisserie was built from repurposed bicycles and a washing machine motor
The contraption successfully cooked 140 lbs of lamb for a wedding
The project reflects a maker ethos of creative reuse of everyday mechanical parts
money_quotes: Frank built a 6' long rotisserie out of a couple of bicycles, a washing machine motor, and some stainless steel.
We used it to cook 140 lbs of lamb for my wedding, which came out perfectly.
posts.key_insight: Creative engineering with repurposed everyday objects can solve surprisingly large-scale problems like catering a wedding.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro documents his quest to buy decent woodworking chisels after realizing his current set is worthless. After using a friend's mediocre-but-functional Irwin/Marples chisels, he's reminded that his own chisels are even worse—especially after his wife used them to chip cement. He sets a $60 budget and evaluates four options ranging from cheap sets with wine money left over to two premium Japanese chisels rated #1 by Fine Woodworking.
key_points: The realization that even mediocre tools dramatically outperform truly bad ones
Evaluating the classic budget tradeoff: more adequate tools vs. fewer excellent ones
Practical research sharing with links to chisel reviews and terminology guides
The domestic negotiation angle of tool purchasing as a married hobbyist
money_quotes: As soon as the blade hit the wood fibers I was brutally reminded that my chisels are absolute, unmitigated, crap.
Frankly, at this point I'd be better off with a butter knife.
They're not total crap, but they're not the chisels you're going to take to your grave by any means.
Get a set of 4 Blue Chip chisels and blow the extra $35 on a good bottle of wine.
Get two badass chisels that will last forever, and deal with only having 1/4 and 3/4 sizes.
posts.key_insight: When buying tools on a budget, the core decision is whether to get more adequate tools or fewer excellent ones—a tradeoff that applies well beyond woodworking.

posts.summary: Shapiro expresses frustration with a woodworking magazine's unhelpful router bit review that offered no specific data or rankings, only the vague conclusion that expensive bits cut better. He then shares a useful review from Fine Woodworker that includes actual comparative data. The post highlights that mid-priced Oldham Viper bits from Home Depot performed well, while some expensive brands ranked near the bottom. It's a brief, practical maker-community post from early in Shapiro's blogging history.
key_points: A major woodworking magazine published a router bit review with no rankings, no brand list, and no useful data — just the conclusion that expensive bits are better
Fine Woodworker published a data-driven review with an actual scorecard comparing brands
Budget-friendly Oldham Viper bits from Home Depot turned out to be a strong performer, while some expensive brands ranked poorly
money_quotes: More expensive bits cut better. Saywhat?
Just a looong section on methadology, and the conclusion that you should Fedex your visa card overnight to the good people at Carbide Anonymous and be thankful for it. Bull.
So when I stumbled on a non-moronic review of router bits yesterday I thought I should share.
It turns out that the yellow Oldham Viper bits they sell at the Depot are a solid deal (and, bonus, made in the USA).
posts.key_insight: Good product reviews require actual data and rankings, not just the lazy conclusion that you get what you pay for.

posts.summary: Shapiro recounts a brief trip to Seoul, South Korea, where he tried and failed to find a traditional woodworking store to buy chisels. He had hoped to find Japanese-style chisels at a lower price or discover distinctly Korean woodworking tools and techniques. Instead, he was repeatedly directed to small power-tool kiosks selling brands like Bosch. He closes by asking readers if anyone knows about Korean woodworking traditions.
key_points: Shapiro traveled to Seoul hoping to buy chisels and explore Korean woodworking traditions
He found no traditional woodworking stores, only modern power-tool kiosks
He crowdsources the question to his blog readers, an early example of using a blog as a community Q&A platform
