money_quotes: I might learn something about Korean woodworking and techniques; maybe even pick up a distinctly Korean tool and a few pieces of interesting wood to stash in the carry-on.
I kept getting directed to these tiny power-tool kiosks (I can report that Bosch is popular over there).
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted travel anecdote about the difficulty of finding traditional craft supplies in a modernized city, reflecting Shapiro's early interest in hands-on making.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro follows up on a previous butcher block woodworking project, reporting that the tung oil finish worked well and the block survived heavy use during a large dinner party. He provides five practical recommendations for anyone making their own butcher block, covering wood selection, end-grain construction, finishing techniques, sizing for planers, and safety. The post reflects his hands-on maker enthusiasm, presenting the project as an accessible and rewarding woodworking endeavor that makes great gifts.
key_points: Non-porous hardwoods like hard maple or white oak are best for butcher blocks; avoid toxic spalted maple
End-grain construction is significantly better for heavy cleaver use, confirmed through hands-on experimentation
Finish selection depends on wood porosity: salad bowl finish for open-pore woods, mineral oil for closed-pore
Sizing the block to fit your planer simplifies leveling, and oversized blocks can be cut into multiple gifts
The project is fast, fun, and can be built entirely from workshop scraps
money_quotes: Don't use spalted maple; the fungus that makes the pretty spalting is toxic.
My experimentation confirmed the conventional wisdom that endgrain is much better for projects involving extended use of a meat cleaver.
Don't drink while working with a meat cleaver.
This project is a no-brainer. It's fast, fun, useful, and you can build it entirely with scraps from past projects.
Remember that you can make it 12.5″ x reallyfrigginlong and cut it into multiple butcher blocks when you're done–they make great gifts.
posts.key_insight: Building an end-grain butcher block is a rewarding, accessible woodworking project that yields a durable, functional kitchen tool when you choose the right wood and finish.

posts.summary: A brief, lighthearted post from 2004 where Shapiro shares a link from his former Microsoft coworker Raymond Chen about an oversized Victorinox Swiss Army tool. The post is essentially a link-share with a humorous personal anecdote about Raymond's eccentric work attire.
key_points: Links to Raymond Chen's blog post about a comically large Victorinox multi-tool
Includes a quirky personal anecdote about a Microsoft coworker who wore a suit but no shoes
money_quotes: Raymond is the only person I knew at Microsoft who wore a suit to work every day. No shoes, though.
posts.key_insight: A throwaway link post notable only as an early-blog-era snapshot of Shapiro's Microsoft days and his eye for quirky personality details.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro comments on Black & Decker's acquisition of Pentair's tool group, which includes respected brands Delta and Porter-Cable. He speculates that the merger will result in a 'slide to the median' where the acquired brands may gain access to cutting-edge innovations but lose some quality as cost-trimming and parts-sharing take effect. He notes that B&D's own quality may improve from the closer association. He concludes there are no short-term impacts on tool buying decisions.
key_points: Black & Decker is acquiring Pentair's tool group including Delta, Porter-Cable, DeVilbiss, and Oldham
Corporate acquisitions tend to produce a 'slide to the median' where premium brands lose quality while budget brands gain it
Porter-Cable and Delta may benefit from B&D's faster adoption of innovations like laser guides
Quality erosion is likely as the acquirer trims costs through parts-sharing and efficiency measures
money_quotes: In my experience, a purchase like this often results in a 'slide to the median'.
Their quality may also sag a bit as they start trying to trim inefficiencies, parts sharing with B&D for example.
The B&D group may similarly improve quality as the groups get closer together.
posts.key_insight: When a mass-market company acquires premium brands, both tend to converge toward mediocrity — the acquired brands lose quality while gaining mainstream innovations.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro compares two methods for cleaning up wood glue squeeze-out after clamping: chipping hardened glue with a chisel versus wiping it while still wet. He explains that chipping risks damaging the wood surface, while naive wiping leaves a thin glue haze that ruins staining. The breakthrough insight, shared by a friend, is to use boiling water from an electric kettle, which dramatically improves the wipe method's effectiveness due to increased solubility at higher temperatures. He recommends this hot-water approach for hardwoods, with caveats about burn safety and timing.
key_points: Chipping hardened glue with a chisel tends to pull wood fibers along with it, requiring heavy sanding to fix
Cold-water wiping leaves a dilute glue haze that soaks into wood and creates visible blotches when staining
Boiling water dramatically improves glue cleanup because solubility increases with temperature
The hot-water method requires working quickly (within 15 minutes) and avoiding softwoods that absorb too much water
money_quotes: The catch, as always, is in the fundamental zen nature of the glue itself: it's supposed to stick to things.
When you pry it off, it has a tendancy to drag small chunks and splinters of your newly completed furniture along with it, kicking and screaming.
You don't discover this until you stain the wood and find giant glue-blotches all over everything.
Basically, the secret to success with the wipe method is to keep a cheapo electric kettle on hand and use actual boiling hot water to do the trick.
This approach has a tendency to result in first and second degree burns if you're not careful, so use hand protection.
posts.key_insight: Using boiling water instead of cold water transforms glue cleanup from a frustrating compromise into a reliable technique, thanks to the basic chemistry of temperature-dependent solubility.

posts.summary: Shapiro shares a practical woodworking tip from building his own dining room table. When his pipe clamps were 4 inches too short, he realized that since pipe clamps are literally made of pipe, he could extend them using standard pipe couplers. He walks through the technique, noting the tradeoff of a bump at the coupler joint that can cause bowing, and offers workarounds like alternating clamp sides or using multiple short segments to distribute the bumps evenly.
key_points: Pipe clamps can be extended using standard pipe couplers since they're made of ordinary pipe
The coupler creates a bump that can cause bowing during glue-ups, so clamps should be placed on alternating sides
Using multiple short pipe segments with frequent couplers creates an even secondary surface instead of one problematic bump
Pipes can also be cut down and re-threaded at hardware stores for more versatile shorter lengths
money_quotes: I was gluing up our dining room table and hit a snag–the longest pipe clamps I have were 4" too short.
Well, fortunately I remembered that since they're made of PIPES, I can do all the usual pipe-y stuff to them, including put couplers on them.
In any case, grab some couplers that fit your pipe clamp pipes. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Pipe clamps are just pipes, so standard plumbing fittings like couplers can extend them when they're too short for a project.

posts.summary: Shapiro describes a practical woodworking technique for cleaning up dadoes (grooves) that were cut with a dull blade, leaving wood shreds and undersized joints. He recommends using a cabinet scraper run back and forth to shear off material and widen the dado. The technique requires two passes per side but is fast and effective. He notes the dadoes came from an ecabinets product, not his own work.
key_points: A cabinet scraper is an effective tool for cleaning up poorly cut dadoes
The scraper's width makes it easy to keep at 90 degrees without rounding the shoulder
Two passes per side are needed since the scraper can't reach the full depth in one pass at the required flat angle
money_quotes: I took a cabinet scraper and ran it back and forth, shearing off a bit of the edge.
It's easy to keep at 90 degrees so you don't round over the shoulder, nice and wide so you can control it, and pretty quick.
All in all, it's a fast and effective way to widen your dado.
posts.key_insight: A cabinet scraper is a fast, controlled way to clean up and widen undersized dadoes without rounding the joint shoulders.

posts.summary: This is a very brief 2004 blog post sharing an Amazon coupon code for tools purchases. Shapiro mentions being away and having issues with an e-cabinets supplier that he's not yet ready to discuss publicly. He also notes progress on a dinner table project. The post is essentially a quick personal update with a deal link.
key_points: Amazon coupon code for $25 off $199+ tool purchases
Shapiro is dealing with frustrations from an e-cabinets supplier but withholding public criticism until all options are exhausted
A dinner table woodworking project is progressing well
money_quotes: I want to exhaust every last option before I skewer them publicly, so I'm staying mum for the moment.
posts.key_insight: A brief personal update from Shapiro's early blogging days showing his long-standing interest in maker projects and his measured approach to public criticism.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical advice on building a humidor as a woodworking project after ordering a large batch of cigars. He explains why humidors are ideal small projects that allow use of scrap wood and small quantities of expensive figured woods. The post focuses on sourcing Spanish cedar for the humidor lining, warning against substitutes and offering a cost-saving tip: getting free or cheap cedar cigar boxes from local cigar shops. He notes the irony of asking your local shop for help after buying cigars online.
key_points: Humidors are ideal woodworking projects because they require small amounts of wood, allowing use of scraps and expensive figured woods without high cost
Spanish cedar is essential for humidor lining — other cedar varieties will make cigars smell like a hamster cage
Local cigar shops will often sell or give away Spanish cedar cigar boxes, providing an affordable source of lining material
The post reflects a maker's mindset of resourcefulness, recycling materials, and finding clever workarounds for expensive supplies
money_quotes: NOT any other kind of cedar, or your cigars will smell like a hamster cage.
Try your local cigar shop (you know, the one you shafted by buying all your stogies online).
Enviro-conscious recycler cheapskate humidor, and nobody will be the wiser.
Humidors are great projects because you can build them from fairly small amounts of wood.
Which, if I calculate correctly, at my current rate of smoking them, is a roughly one-decade supply.
posts.key_insight: Resourceful makers can build quality humidors cheaply by recycling Spanish cedar from cigar boxes sourced from local shops.

posts.summary: Shapiro demonstrates that most combination squares are inaccurate by describing a simple flip test that reveals whether the tool is truly square. He then presents three solutions at different price points: an expensive precision square, a dedicated fixed square, or a cheap rafter square made from a single piece of aluminum with no moving parts to fail. The post implicitly advocates for the simplest, most reliable tool over complex or expensive alternatives.
key_points: Most combination squares fail a basic accuracy test because plastic components wear down over time
The simplest solution (a solid aluminum rafter square with no moving parts) is often the most reliable and cost-effective
Complexity and moving parts introduce failure modes that simpler designs avoid
money_quotes: Do the two lines overlap, or do they make a skinny "V"? Yeah, thought so.
It's just one solid piece of aluminum, no moving parts, nothing to mess up.
And it's cheap enough that you can buy a bunch to scatter all around the house.
posts.key_insight: The simplest tool with no moving parts often outperforms complex, expensive alternatives — reliability comes from eliminating failure modes, not adding features.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro explains why standard miter slots on tablesaws are frustratingly imprecise, causing inaccurate cuts. He shares a personal anecdote about making a picture frame that shrank to wallet-size after repeated corrections. He recommends either buying an expensive adjustable miter gauge or building a custom crosscut sled with runners machined to fit your specific table.
key_points: Stock miter gauges that come with tablesaws have significant wobble, making precise cuts difficult
Building a crosscut sled with custom-machined runners provides a perfect fit to your specific table
Planing a board to the right thickness then ripping strips creates hyper-accurate sled runners
money_quotes: Miter slots stink. No two ways about it, it's really hard to make a tool that works well in a miter slot.

I actually made a whole damn picture frame before I figured out why I had to recut each side eighteen times to get it right on 45 degrees… the resulting frame held a wallet-size picture, but that's besides the point.
posts.key_insight: Stock tablesaw miter gauges are too imprecise for accurate work; building a custom crosscut sled with runners machined to fit your specific table is the best solution.

posts.summary: This short post explains the origin of spalted wood's distinctive patterns — they come from fungal decay. Shapiro advises woodworkers that spalted wood must be harvested at the right moment before it loses structural integrity, and that the fungal spores remain on the wood after harvesting. He recommends either kiln-drying, oven-sterilizing at 130 degrees for eight hours, or sealing with shellac to kill or contain the spores. The post is a concise, practical tip for woodworkers buying or processing their own spalted lumber.
key_points: Spalted wood's beautiful patterns are actually the visible decay left by a specific fungus
Timing the harvest is critical — too early and the spalting isn't visible, too late and the wood loses structural integrity
Fungal spores remain on the wood and must be killed via kiln-drying, oven treatment (130°F for 8 hours), or sealed with shellac
Spalted wood is often weak along the spalt lines and should be reinforced with a frame or backing
money_quotes: It's the decay left behind by a specific type of fungus.
The wood must be harvested when the spalting is pronounced and visible, but wait too long and the wood loses its structural integrity.
The spores are still present on the wood.
Eight hours in a 130 degree oven should be enough to sterilize the board.
Toast your spalted lumber. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Spalted wood's beauty comes from fungal decay, and woodworkers must sterilize or seal it to prevent the spores from causing further damage.

posts.summary: A brief woodworking tip about using masking tape on inside corners before gluing to prevent messy squeeze-out. Shapiro recommends masking off hard-to-clean areas, letting the glue set for 30-45 minutes, then peeling off the tape for clean results. The post is a quick practical maker tip with no broader argument.
key_points: Masking tape on inside corners before gluing prevents hard-to-clean squeeze-out
Wait 30-45 minutes before peeling off the tape and excess glue
Plan for cleanup difficulty before assembly, not after
money_quotes: When gluing up something that will be hard to clean when complete, use masking tape to keep the squeezeout from messing up your project.
posts.key_insight: Prevent glue mess on hard-to-reach joints by masking off corners with tape before assembly.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro follows up on a previous router bit review post, reporting his experience with an Oldham Viper 3/4 straight bit. He used it on walnut and maple for trimming and rabbeting tasks. He found it cuts aggressively and produces a noticeably better finish than cheaper carbide bits. He recommends readers pick some up.
key_points: The Oldham Viper bit was recommended in a prior router bit review post
It performed well across multiple tasks including trimming walnut and cutting rabbets in maple
The bit produces a better finish than budget carbide alternatives
money_quotes: The bad boy makes sawdust like a woodchuck.
It gives a noticably better finish than the El Cheapo Grande carbide bits I have already, too.
Pick up a few. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Investing in a quality router bit like the Oldham Viper delivers noticeably better performance and finish than budget carbide alternatives.

posts.summary: A brief update noting that an anonymous commenter disputed a previous post about Ridgid tools outsourcing production and slashing warranties. The author updated the original article in response.
key_points: Ridgid allegedly cut their warranty and outsourced tool production overseas
An anonymous commenter disputed the original claims
The author updated the original article to reflect the new information
posts.key_insight: This is a brief housekeeping post with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro categorizes power tool brands into two tiers: 'Good and Expensive' (G&E) and 'Cheap and Crappy' (C&C). G&E brands like DeWalt, Milwaukee, and Porter Cable reliably deliver quality at their normal price points, but can disappoint when they slap their name on cheap products. C&C brands are defined not by being universally bad, but by their inconsistency—some products are excellent while others are junk. The key takeaway is that G&E brands let you buy with confidence, while C&C brands require careful research before purchasing.
key_points: Premium tool brands (DeWalt, Milwaukee, Porter Cable) are reliably good at their normal price points, but cheap products from these brands should not be trusted on name alone
Budget brands are defined by variability, not universal poor quality—Skil makes excellent circular saws despite being a 'cheap' brand
Consumer reviews of budget tools are unreliable because buyers are often too euphoric about the deal or lack experience to judge quality honestly
The real distinction between tiers is research burden: premium brands let you buy blind, budget brands require homework
money_quotes: I divide tool brands into two categories. These are, roughly, 'cheap and crappy' and 'good and expensive'.
If it ain't expensive, you shouldn't count on it being good either, even if it's from a 'Good and Expensive' brand.
The one constant thing about C&C brands is their variability.

Many people are so euphoric about getting a whole set of 18v tools for $150 that they don't evaluate the quality of the things honestly, or they just don't have the experience to know how mediocre they really are.
Skil makes some top-of-the-line circular saws (they do call them 'Skil Saws' for a reason).
posts.key_insight: Premium tool brands buy you the freedom to skip research, while budget brands demand careful per-product evaluation because their defining trait is inconsistency, not universal poor quality.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical tip from his woodworking experience: microfiber towels work excellently for applying shellac and other finishes. After struggling with lint and pilling from various cloth materials while finishing a coffee table, he discovered microfiber towels at Costco. He tested them on a humidor with great results, noting only that colored microfiber can leach dye into alcohol-based finishes.
key_points: Common household fabrics like T-shirts and socks leave lint and pilling when used to apply shellac
Microfiber towels, made from tightly-woven polyester or nylon, are lint-free and ideal for applying finishes
Colored microfiber cloths can leach dye into alcohol-soluble finishes, so white cloths are preferable
money_quotes: I tried old T-shirts, socks, just about everything, but kept having problems with lint and pilling, which would show up in the finish.
I recently was wandering the aisles at Costco, with that typical look of fear, determination, and opportunity that one gets at massive consumer warehouses.
The dye used to color the microfiber is slightly alcohol-soluble.
Get some microfiber cloths. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Microfiber towels are a superior alternative to common household fabrics for applying shellac and other finishes, as they eliminate lint and pilling problems.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces adding Amazon product recommendations to his blog sidebar, joking that he's recommending expensive, breakable tools to earn commissions. He admits the recommendations are actually items from his own workshop. The post is a brief, humorous update typical of early blogging culture, complete with self-deprecating jokes about his follow-through on updates. He also teases upcoming merchandise through CafePress.
key_points: Shapiro added Amazon affiliate product recommendations to his blog sidebar
He jokes about the commission scheme before admitting the items are genuinely from his own shop
Self-aware humor about his tendency to never update things he promises to maintain
Teases branded merchandise as a future project
money_quotes: I took the most expensive, yet ineffective items from Amazon's tool store, and recommended them to you.
That way you'll buy them, I'll get a five percent commision, and then they'll break, and you'll buy more! I'll be rich in no time!
I'll try to keep it updated, which is Dan-speak for 'I will never touch it again'.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted early blog post showcasing Shapiro's self-deprecating humor and maker sensibility as he experiments with blog monetization.

posts.summary: This is an ultra-short post from 2004 where Shapiro shares a link to a product on Amazon that he considers an unnecessary but desirable power tool. The post is essentially a one-liner with an affiliate link, characteristic of early blogging's casual, link-sharing style.
key_points: Highlights the universal consumer impulse to want tools we don't actually need
Early example of affiliate-link blogging style common in the mid-2000s
money_quotes: You don't need it, but you know you want it anyway.
posts.key_insight: The appeal of a good tool has nothing to do with whether you actually need it.

posts.summary: Shapiro recounts a woodworking mistake where he tried to sand down a too-thick humidor part using 400-grit sandpaper instead of low-grit. Despite producing enormous quantities of sawdust that gave the illusion of progress, he spent an hour making almost no real headway. The high grit number means more cutting surfaces producing many tiny particles—volume of sawdust without meaningful material removal. It's a simple lesson about using the right tool for the job and not confusing the appearance of productivity with actual progress.
key_points: High-grit sandpaper produces lots of fine sawdust that creates an illusion of rapid progress
Visible output (sawdust) is not the same as meaningful progress (wood removal)
Using the wrong tool can waste enormous time while feeling productive
The right approach is to match your tools to the scale of the task
money_quotes: I was generating HUGE quantities of sawdust, so I figured I must be making progress.
Higher grit means more cutting surfaces means more particles of sawdust… they're just really small particles.
It looks like you're hogging off a ton of wood, but of course you're not making much of a dent at all.
I made a stupid mistake recently, so of course I am coming to you to share.
An hour (and a sore arm) later I realized my mistake.
posts.key_insight: Visible busyness and output can be deeply misleading—generating huge quantities of sawdust doesn't mean you're actually removing wood, and the appearance of productivity is not the same as real progress.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a brief tip about micro mesh abrasives, a specialty product designed for polishing violins, as a solution for finding super-high-grit sandpaper. The post is a quick maker-culture recommendation inviting readers to try the product and report back.
key_points: Super-high-grit sandpaper is difficult to source through normal channels
Micro mesh abrasives from violin supply companies offer a potential alternative
The post invites community feedback before committing to a purchase
money_quotes: It's hard to find super-high-grit sandpaper.
posts.key_insight: Niche specialty suppliers in unexpected fields like violin-making can be sources for hard-to-find maker materials.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro enthusiastically recommends a deal on Two Cherries chisels, a well-regarded woodworking tool brand. He directs readers to compare regular pricing against a deeply discounted set of four chisels available for under $60. The post is a brief, no-frills buying recommendation rooted in his personal woodworking hobby. It references a prior post where he researched chisel options in detail.
key_points: Two Cherries chisels are a quality woodworking tool worth investing in
The recommended set offers significant savings compared to individual or gift set pricing
The only tradeoff is unpolished backs, which doesn't affect sharpness
money_quotes: Drop everything and buy these chisels, now.
You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: When you find a great deal on quality tools, act fast and share it with fellow makers.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro responds to a reader's question about choosing the right clear finish for a custom domino game table. The reader wants to embed pictures and memorabilia under a clear coat, similar to bar tops, and needs a durable, non-yellowing surface that can withstand domino play and spilled drinks. Shapiro evaluates several finish options from his woodworking experience, including shellac, epoxy, water-based varnishes, and Behlen's Rock Hard Tabletop Varnish. He ultimately recommends using a thick sheet of glass fused to the table with epoxy underneath, combining maximum durability with clarity.
key_points: Shellac is too soft for game table use, yellows over time, and is damaged by alcohol spills
Bar-top epoxy is tough and repairable but may still dent and scuff under heavy use
Water-based varnishes lack the hardness and build thickness needed for this application
Traditional varnishes like Behlen's Rock Hard are extremely durable but have a yellow-orange tint that affects embedded items
A thick glass sheet over epoxy provides the best combination of durability, clarity, and impact resistance
money_quotes: Shellac is too soft. You might manage it if you use natural (not blonde) shellac that's freshly mixed, but even then, I think you'd dent it with your dominoes.
I have experimented with exactly the goop you link to above, and it's tough stuff. You'll probably dent it and scuff it, but not break it.
It's also not difficult to refinish if it gets too beat up.
You won't have much effect on a 3/8 sheet of glass when you slam a domino into it, if it's well supported underneath with something conformant.
If it were me, I'd get a sheet of thick glass, and use the epoxy stuff underneath it to fuse it to the table & buffer it underneath.
posts.key_insight: For maximum durability on a game table with embedded memorabilia, a thick glass top fused with epoxy outperforms any standalone finish by combining clarity, impact resistance, and protection.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro continues his series on building custom humidors, focusing on design principles. He explains the box-within-a-box construction where a pretty exterior surrounds a Spanish cedar interior, emphasizing the importance of a proper lip seal between lid and box. The post covers practical sizing considerations including length, height, width, and wall thickness, with specific measurements for accommodating various cigar sizes. He notes that humidors shouldn't be perfectly airtight since cigars release ammonia as they age. The post balances technical woodworking advice with humor, including a recommendation to use the golden ratio for aesthetic proportions.
key_points: A humidor is fundamentally a box within a box: a moisture-proof exterior surrounding a Spanish cedar interior with an overlapping lip seal
Perfect airtightness is actually undesirable because aging cigars release ammonia that needs to escape
Sizing should be generous since the wood cost difference is minimal, with specific guidance on length, height, and width dimensions
The golden ratio (1.618) should be applied to exterior dimensions for aesthetics, accounting for wall thickness
Wall thickness should be at least 1/2 inch exterior wood and 1/8 inch Spanish cedar minimum
money_quotes: Basically, a humidor is a box within a box. The exterior box is something pretty, structurally sound, and moisture-proof.

You don't actually want the thing hard-sealed, by the way; sure, it'd hold its humidity better, but cigars release a tiny bit of ammonia as they age, so a little air exchange is necessary or your cigars will pick up that distinctive eau de micturation.
Remember that no matter how big you make this, it's still not very much wood, so you might as well make it big.
If in doubt, use the golden rule, and make it as wide as someone else would want it to be. Er, use the golden ratio, and make it 1.61803399 times as wide as it is long.
This makes his humidor excellent for storing cigars, and for protecting fragile objects from a direct nuclear strike.
posts.key_insight: Good humidor design balances structural precision with practical forgiveness — the seal should be snug but not airtight, and the dimensions generous rather than tight.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a lighthearted Halloween post about carving a political jack-o-lantern using his woodworking chisels. He playfully hedges on the political nature of the pumpkin by offering two interpretations depending on the viewer's political leanings. The post is a brief, humorous personal entry from 2004 with no substantive argument or thesis.
key_points: Shapiro repurposed his new woodworking chisels to carve a political jack-o-lantern
He frames the carved pumpkin as either an homage or a scary Halloween creation depending on the viewer's politics
The post links to a guide on advanced pumpkin carving techniques
money_quotes: If you're a supporter of the current administration, please regard this as an homage to our president.
If not, please regard this as the scariest jack-o-lantern that I was capable of carving.
posts.key_insight: A playful Halloween post about pumpkin carving with political overtones, offering no substantive business or technology insight.

posts.summary: In this third installment of his humidor-building series, Shapiro explains the critical woodworking challenge of designing for humidity differentials. Since a humidor's interior maintains 70% relative humidity while the exterior faces much lower room humidity, the box experiences constant stress that demands careful joinery choices. He recommends strong glued joints (dovetails or fingerjoints) for the sides but slotted, unglued raised panels for the top and bottom to allow wood movement. He also shares his specific material choices and finishing tips, emphasizing that finishing must happen before assembly to preserve the panel's ability to move.
key_points: The humidity differential between a humidor's interior (70% RH) and exterior (~50% RH) creates constant stress on the wood, making joinery the central design challenge
Sides need strong glued joints like dovetails, while tops and bottoms should float in slots to accommodate wood movement
Finishing must be done before assembly—otherwise the finish locks raised panels in place, defeating their purpose
Material choices included walnut scraps, AAA curly maple for the top, and spalted maple for the bottom, keeping costs down by using small quantities of premium wood
money_quotes: Humidity and wood are a treacherous combination.
There is going to be incredible stress on the parts of your box, and you have to design for parts of the box to move.

The key thing to remember is that you have to finish the top and bottom before you assemble it. Otherwise, you'll waste all your time with the raised-panel stuff, because the finish will lock everything in place, preventing movement.
posts.key_insight: Designing a humidor is fundamentally an exercise in engineering for constant wood movement caused by the humidity differential between interior and exterior.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a woodworking tip about using mineral oil for wet sanding, which produces smoother finishes and dramatically extends sandpaper life. The post is structured as a humorous how-to guide, with the central comedic bit being the awkwardness of buying mineral oil (sold as a laxative) at the grocery store. He explains the technique: apply mineral oil liberally to the surface, then sand with 320 grit for results far superior to dry sanding. The post also touches on his broader sanding workflow, including pore-filling with pumice.
key_points: Mineral oil wet sanding produces smoother surfaces than dry sanding and makes sandpaper last dramatically longer
The cheapest mineral oil comes from the pharmacy/grocery aisle, where it's sold as a laxative — cheaper than the industrial woodworking version
The technique works best as a final step (320 grit) but can be used from the start if you're not filling pores with pumice
Apply enough mineral oil that your hand 'skates' along the surface before sanding
money_quotes: I haven't shared the ultimate secret to fabulous finishes, not to mention saving a bundle on sandpaper: mineral oil wet sanding.
If you happen to have the simultaneous personality defects of being easily embarrased and constantly feeling like you have to explain yourself to total strangers, this may be a bit tricky.

It's funny how it's cheaper to buy the medicinal stuff than it is to buy the industrial stuff. You'd think that if you were actually going to swallow it as a laxitive, they'd have to charge more for it.
I mean the stuff is really cheap. Too bad I'm not constipated. I'm not, you know. Totally regular. Every morning, like clockwork.
Not only with the surface be far smoother than you could have done dry, but you'll be able to do an entire tabletop on just one sheet of sandpaper.
By some mineral oil, and keep your mouth shut when you do. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Mineral oil from the pharmacy aisle is the cheapest and most effective wet-sanding lubricant for woodworking, producing superior finishes while making sandpaper last far longer.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his research on table saws, providing practical buying advice for woodworkers shopping under $1,500. He outlines three key criteria: cabinet-style construction over contractor-style, left-tilt blade orientation, and solid cast-iron wings. After evaluating three saws meeting these criteria, he recommends the Grizzly G1023SL as the clear winner based on features, reviews, and price.
key_points: Cabinet saws are superior to contractor-style saws due to heavier trunnions, reduced vibration, and better dust collection
Left-tilt saws are safer because right-tilt blades force cutoffs into the fence, increasing kickback risk
Cast-iron wings are essential for precision work since stamped steel flexes and adds instability
The Grizzly G1023SL offers the best combination of features, quality, and value at $895
The Jet Xacta cabinet saw suffers from significant quality control and shipping problems despite being the most expensive option
money_quotes: Get a cabinet saw, not a contractor-style saw. Heavier trunnions mean less vibration.
Get a left-tilt saw. Most people put the fence on the right, and a right-tilt blade forces the cutoff down and into the fence. This makes kickback more likely, which makes everything more dangerous.
Stamped steel flexes and can't be used for precision work.
There also seems to be severe quality problems–a good third of reviewers reported that they had to return parts, usually several times over.
The Grizzly G1023SL is the big winner. Everything I was looking for, and outstanding reviews across the board.
posts.key_insight: When buying a table saw, prioritize cabinet construction, left-tilt orientation, and cast-iron wings — and the best value meeting all three criteria is often not the most expensive option.

posts.summary: A brief housekeeping post announcing that Dan Shapiro's blog is now available at the more memorable URL www.nothingseveredyet.com. No substantive content or argument is presented.
key_points: Blog moved to a more memorable URL (nothingseveredyet.com)
posts.key_insight: This is a trivial URL-change announcement with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: A brief, humorous post written the day after the 2004 U.S. presidential election offering a practical tip: WD-40 or kerosene can remove bumper stickers. The implicit joke is that post-election, people on the losing side will want to scrape off their political bumper stickers. The entire post is just two sentences, making it one of the most concise political commentaries possible.
key_points: WD-40 (mostly kerosene) effectively removes bumper stickers
The timing—one day after the 2004 election—is the entire joke
Practical maker advice doubles as dry political humor
money_quotes: WD-40 (or straight kerosene, which is what WD-40 is mostly composed of) takes off bumper stickers.
It's handy stuff to have around the shop–you'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best political commentary is a practical tip delivered with perfect timing.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his personal method for applying a shellac finish to wood, specifically walnut table legs. The post is a detailed step-by-step guide covering sanding, pore-filling with pumice, and building up layers of shellac to achieve a smooth, glossy finish. He approaches the process with a casual, experimental attitude, treating shellac preparation like a cooking project. The technique involves packing pumice into wood pores with shellac, cleaning up with denatured alcohol, then progressively sanding with mineral oil before applying final coats.
key_points: Pore-filling with pumice mixed into shellac is the key technique for achieving a smooth finish on open-grained woods like walnut and red oak
The process involves multiple stages: initial shellac coat, pumice packing, alcohol cleanup, mineral oil sanding, and final shellac layers
Progressive sanding from 220 up to 2000 grit with mineral oil removes surface shellac while preserving the pumice-filled pores
The author takes a deliberately casual, experimental approach to shellac preparation rather than precise measurement
money_quotes: If I mess 'em up, who cares–they're just going to get kicked anyway.
I treat shellac prep like a cooking project–that is to say cavalierly.
Don't sweat the nasty pumice-swirls you're leaving behind. The goal here is to pack pumice into every pore of the wood so the finish is smooth.
The important thing is that the pores are packed with pumice mixed with shellac, and your surface can get glassy smooth.
Using your Sander of Deepest Delights, lightly sand to 220.
posts.key_insight: A high-quality shellac finish on open-grained wood comes from packing the pores with pumice and shellac before building up surface coats, and the process rewards a relaxed, experimental attitude over perfectionism.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he's selling his trusty table saw on eBay after upgrading to a new one. He notes the current bid is surprisingly low compared to identical saws listed elsewhere. He sweetens the deal for blog readers by offering free spalted maple lumber to the winning bidder, with the condition they share photos of what they make and keep all fingers intact.
key_points: Shapiro is upgrading his table saw and selling the old one, which he credits with keeping him injury-free
The eBay listing is significantly underpriced compared to identical saws, presenting a deal for readers
He offers a reader-exclusive bonus of spalted maple lumber worth around $60 to the winning bidder
The post reflects his hands-on maker identity and community-building through woodworking
money_quotes: The single instrument that is most responsible for me Not Severing Anything Yet.
The bid is now $50, which is somewhat insane considering that an identical saw is already at $250 with a couple of days still to go.
The only requirement is that you have to send me pictures of whatever you make to post, and you can't cut your finger off while doing it.
Buy my saw, or at least look at the pretty pictures. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted woodworking post that showcases Shapiro's maker roots and community-minded approach to his craft long before his tech career milestones.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro provides a practical guide to food-safe wood finishes for woodworkers who need their projects to be safe for contact with food or mouths. He ranks several finishing options from most to least durable, including shellac, salad bowl finish, pure tung oil, walnut oil, mineral oil, and other plant oils. The post notes important caveats like the difference between pure tung oil and commercial 'tung oil finish' products that contain heavy metals. He encourages woodworkers to turn scrap wood into functional items like butcher blocks, building blocks, and salad bowls using these safe alternatives.
key_points: Most commercial wood finishes are toxic when wet and potentially unsafe even when dry, so food-contact projects require special alternatives
Pure tung oil provides the toughest penetrating finish but requires patience with drying time and periodic re-wiping
Mineral oil is the cheapest and most common butcher block finish but never hardens and needs frequent reapplication
Commercial 'tung oil finish' products often contain heavy metal drying agents and should not be confused with pure tung oil
money_quotes: Ever had a project that looked so good, you just wanted to lick it?
While most finishes are toxic, they become much less toxic when they dry. Of course, that's what they said about lead paint.
Insert 'tongue oil' joke here.
Pure tung oil comes from a nut, though, and you can drink it if you want (hint: you don't).
Don't use baby oil, since it has fragrances added and you probably don't want your butcher block smelling like a baby's derriere.
posts.key_insight: Woodworkers making food-contact items should choose from a specific set of food-safe finishes ranked by durability, from shellac down to mineral oil, rather than relying on potentially toxic commercial products.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro describes building a simple jig for cutting perfect 45-degree miter joints for picture frames. After struggling with a more complex crosscut sled approach, he realized he could achieve the same result with just two pieces of scrap wood, one measurement, and two screws. The jig works by clamping material against a hardwood stick mounted at 45 degrees on plywood, which rides against the table saw fence. The post exemplifies his maker philosophy of favoring simplicity over complexity.
key_points: A good jig should be easy to make, use cheap materials, require minimal precision cuts, and actually work
Complex solutions (crosscut sleds with runners) often introduce more problems than they solve
The simple two-piece jig replaces finicky runner systems by using the existing table saw fence as a guide
The entire jig requires only two screws, one angle measurement, and about ten minutes to build
money_quotes: What makes a perfect jig? Well, here are my criteria: 1) Easy to make. If it ain't, I won't.
Then it hit me, like a red sock to a baseball. I was making this way too complicated.
I could do it with two pieces of wood and exactly one measurement, in about ten minutes.
Basically you drive one screw, line up 45, then drive a second, and you're DONE.
That's right, you use the fence–no need for runners and all the pain that entails.
Slap one together out of some scrap lumber and see for yourself. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: The best workshop solutions come from simplifying the problem rather than engineering a more complex tool.

posts.summary: Shapiro shares his experience working with cocobolo, an exotic hardwood with remarkable and unusual properties. The wood changes color dramatically — from red-orange to bright yellow after sanding, then back again when exposed to moisture. While beautiful and extremely durable, cocobolo produces allergenic sawdust that acts as a sensitizer, making repeated exposure increasingly dangerous. The post serves as both an enthusiastic recommendation and a practical safety warning for woodworkers considering this material.
key_points: Cocobolo undergoes dramatic color shifts: red-orange to post-it-note yellow after sanding, reverting when exposed to water or oil
The sawdust is a sensitizer allergen similar to poison ivy, meaning reactions worsen with repeated exposure even if initial contact seems safe
The wood's natural oils make it waterproof and self-finishing but require mineral spirits before gluing
Cocobolo is extremely hard and durable, used for knife handles and even flooring, and can be polished to a high sheen without added finish
money_quotes: The sawdust is orange and should be treated like radioactive waste.
It smelled good enough that I wanted to lean closer and catch a closer whiff, but that seemed unwise since the tablesaw was still running.
I don't mean 'it gets slightly more yellow', I mean post-it note, bug-lamp yellow.
Even if you don't get nailed by it the first few times you're around it, the fifth or tenth or whatever time you'll find yourself instantly overwhelmed by just a speck.
You could probably make an outdoor deck out of it if you really had money to burn.
Pick up some cocobolo, a full face respirator, a new dust collector, and a hermetically sealed bunny suit. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Cocobolo is a stunning and practically indestructible exotic wood whose remarkable properties come paired with equally remarkable hazards, demanding serious safety precautions.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical tip for calibrating a Biesmeyer-style rip fence on a table saw. After initial setup difficulties, he discovered that the far end of the fence wasn't staying true when reclamped. The fix was tightening the pads so the fence just barely slides over the guide rail, resulting in truer, squarer cuts.
key_points: Biesmeyer fences only clamp on one end, making alignment of the far end critical
The setup guide's instructions alone weren't sufficient for consistent accuracy
Tightening the pads for a snug slide over the guide rail solved the drift problem
money_quotes: The trick is to tighten the pads down so the biesmeyer fence just barely slides over the guide rail.
If you do this, the whole fence runs truer, stays squarer, and generally makes you happier.
True up your bies. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: A Biesmeyer rip fence runs truer when its pads are tightened so it just barely slides over the guide rail, compensating for the single-clamp design.

posts.summary: A brief promotional post sharing a 15% off coupon code for Rockler.com, a woodworking supply retailer. Shapiro highlights a few product categories including an amusingly expensive musical jewelry box kit, finishing products, and sanding supplies. The post is essentially a quick tip for fellow woodworkers and makers.
key_points: Shares a specific coupon code (V5108) for 15% off at Rockler woodworking supplies
Highlights the absurdity of a high-end musical jewelry box kit while pointing to practical supplies
Demonstrates Shapiro's long-standing interest in maker culture and woodworking, years before founding Glowforge
money_quotes: Don't miss this if you're planning on building the world's most expensive musical jewelery box.
posts.key_insight: An early glimpse into Shapiro's maker culture roots, sharing woodworking deals with his audience long before his laser-cutter company days.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recommends OSHA's free online woodworking safety resources as a practical guide for hobbyist woodworkers. He points out that since taxpayers already funded these materials, they might as well use them. The post specifically highlights OSHA's woodworking etool as an accessible, machine-by-machine safety guide. He contrasts the readable etool with OSHA's notoriously dense full regulatory codes.
key_points: OSHA's woodworking etool provides free, taxpayer-funded safety information organized by machine type
The etool is written in accessible language unlike OSHA's full regulatory codes
Real production woodworking injury data informs the safety recommendations
money_quotes: Why spend your own hard-earned dollars twice… your friendly federal government has spent the money already!
These are the guys whose job it is to monitor real, prodction woodworkers, figure out what's chopping off their fingers, and determine what to do with it.
It will tell you on a machine-by-machine basis what's likely to chop your arm off (and what to do about it).
The etool is 'OSHA for dummies', where 'dummies' is defined as 'people who speak english instead of beauracro-speak'.
The full codes are there too, if you're having trouble falling asleep.
posts.key_insight: Taxpayer-funded OSHA woodworking safety resources are an underused, accessible free alternative to paid safety courses for hobbyist woodworkers.

posts.summary: Shapiro offers practical tablesaw safety advice through his characteristic humor. He divides woodworkers into two categories — 'safety nuts' who use proper guards and pushsticks, and those called 'stubby' (implying they've lost fingers). The post focuses on a commonly overlooked danger: moving a slightly angled tablesaw fence to the opposite side of the blade, which reverses the tilt and causes dangerous kickback. It's a short, punchy safety reminder from his early blogging days about maker culture.
key_points: Woodworkers who ignore safety equipment risk serious injury, humorously categorized as 'stubby'
A tablesaw fence angled slightly away from the blade becomes dangerous when moved to the opposite side, reversing the tilt toward the blade
Kickback — lumber launched back at the operator — is caused by wood binding between blade and fence
Standing to the side of the saw rather than directly behind it is a critical but often forgotten safety measure
money_quotes: There are two kinds of woodworkers. The first kind is often called the safety nut. The second kind is often called stubby.

If you do, it will be leaning TOWARDS the blade, which absolutely guarantees that the piece will bind between the blade and the fence, and inevitably cause both burning of the piece and rapid projectile motion of your lumber towards your belly button, aka kickback.
posts.key_insight: Safety shortcuts in woodworking — and by extension any hands-on craft — have consequences that range from ruined work to serious injury, making attention to seemingly minor details like fence alignment essential.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro examines SawStop, a safety technology that can stop a tablesaw blade instantly upon detecting contact with skin. Despite his enthusiasm for the invention, he notes several practical barriers: no major saw manufacturer has agreed to install the system, forcing SawStop to sell their own custom saws. The technology is expensive, adding significant cost to already pricey equipment, and false alarms can be costly since the braking process destroys both the brake cartridge and the blade. Shapiro concludes he loves the idea but recommends caution until major manufacturers adopt it.
key_points: SawStop's blade-stopping technology can detect finger contact and halt the blade instantly, but no major manufacturer has adopted it
The company has been forced to build and sell their own saws rather than licensing the technology to established brands
Cost is a significant barrier: specialty saws are expensive and false triggers destroy both the $60 brake cartridge and $100+ blade
The technology represents a compelling safety innovation stuck in a market adoption gap
money_quotes: If their finely tuned electrical system detects that the blade has contacted a finger–or a Jenny O hotdog–it brings the whole thing to a screeching halt.
Sawstop is involved in a fascinating battle to get this technology to you, but so far, they haven't got a single saw manufacturer to install their system on their product line.
It's a new saw from a new company, though, and that's a bit of a concern from a reliability standpoint.
At $60 for a new brake cartridge, and $100+ for a replacement blade, you'll be pretty ticked off if it false-triggers for any reason.
I love this idea. I want to keep my weblog name; I've grown fond of it. But until the big guys start putting it on their saws, I've got to recommend caution.
posts.key_insight: Even brilliant safety innovations face an uphill battle when incumbent manufacturers won't adopt them, forcing inventors to become manufacturers themselves at a cost and reliability disadvantage.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro addresses the conflict of interest inherent in product reviews by woodworking writers who receive free tools from vendors. Rather than pretending the conflict doesn't exist, he devises a transparent policy: accept free tools, give one away to readers, write honest reviews but simply skip reviewing tools that are bad. He frames this as a mutually beneficial 'sellout' arrangement where both writer and readers benefit from free equipment while maintaining a baseline of honesty.
key_points: Woodworking writers do reviews primarily because they get to keep the free products, creating an inherent conflict of interest
Shapiro's policy: accept free tools but give one away to readers via giveaway, only covering shipping costs
He won't write negative reviews of free tools — he'll simply not publish the review at all
For tools he paid for himself, he'll review without restraint, positive or negative
The vendor gets no editorial control over the review content
money_quotes: You may think that's because there's a high demand among readers; that our discerning eye can reveal the finer points of differentiation between identical-seeming products. Well, you'd be wrong. That's not why woodworking writers get to write reviews.
It's because they get to keep the free stuff when they're done.
So I was all ready to proceed with my career as a sellout woodworking journalist loaded with free stuff, when I hit upon a brilliant fourth option: sell out, but make you sell out with me.
If I say it's great, it's great. I won't even leave out the bad parts. I just won't write reviews of crappy tools.
If I paid for it, the gloves are off.
posts.key_insight: Transparency about conflicts of interest — and sharing the spoils with your audience — is a more honest approach than pretending reviewer objectivity exists.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews a $25 leather shop apron from woodworker.com, rating it 5/5 thumbs up. He argues that a leather apron is essential safety equipment for woodworkers because shop debris can fly at high speed and impale skin. The apron meets his requirements for coverage, leather construction, double-stitched seams, handy pockets, and easy on/off use. Despite some rough workmanship and lighter leather than a farrier's apron, he considers it a no-brainer purchase at the price point. The review is part of his 'NSY Free Tools' series, and includes a giveaway.
key_points: A heavy leather apron provides critical torso protection from high-speed debris kicked back by shop tools like saws, planers, and jointers
The reviewed apron hits a sweet spot of $25 pricing with solid construction including double-stitched seams and metal rivets
Pockets loaded with everyday tools (pencils, tape measure, box knife) serve as a behavioral nudge to actually wear the apron
Leather significantly outperforms canvas or denim for stopping sharp, fast-moving objects, even if this apron's leather is lighter than industrial-grade
money_quotes: From time to time, various objects in your shop may decide to jump up in the air and move in a straight line at very high speed.
Now, this should be an easy question. Who's skin would you rather be impaled: yours, or a dead cow's?
Since I need one of those three things every ten minutes I'm in the shop, this guarantees that I won't be in the shop for more than ten minutes before I remember to put on my apron.
Some of the cut lines are less than straight, perhaps revealing its origins in the bovine protection business, or possibly just the result of some hasty scissorwork.
This is a great apron; very handy from a usefulness standpoint, and a kick-me-in-the-head nobrainer from a safety standpoint.
posts.key_insight: Smart workshop safety gear doesn't need to be expensive — a $25 leather apron with useful pockets creates both physical protection and a behavioral incentive to actually wear it.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro evangelizes calipers as an essential but overlooked tool for woodworkers, explaining their three measurement capabilities: thickness (pinching), width (interior measurement), and depth (via a sliding protrusion). He argues that while calipers are standard equipment for machinists and engineers, many woodworkers miss out on their hyper-accurate measuring abilities. The post reviews different caliper types—digital, fractional dial, and decimal dial—recommending decimal dial for experienced users and fractional dial for beginners. He advises against slide and vernier calipers as unnecessarily difficult when better alternatives are cheap.
key_points: Calipers measure in three ways most people don't realize: thickness by pinching, width via reverse jaws, and depth via a sliding protrusion
Digital calipers are easy to read but jitter on soft materials like wood; decimal dial calipers offer 10x the accuracy of fractional dial models
Slide and vernier calipers are needlessly difficult when affordable digital and dial alternatives exist
The tool is standard for machinists and engineers but surprisingly unknown among woodworkers
money_quotes: These little metal instruments of hyperaccurate joyous delight are an absolute staple for machinists; they wouldn't be caught dead without one.
My hair, for example, is four mils thick. You would know how thick your hair was too if you had a caliper.
Stay away from slide calipers and vernier calipers, unless your goal is to exercise your eyeballs and improve your slide-rule math skills, respectively.
There's just no reason to abuse yourself like that when the alternatives are so cheap.
Be sure not to push too hard, as you can compress the wood fibers and make it look bigger than it really is.
You might want one of each–a fractional caliper for standard measurements, and a digital or decimal dial one for when you're trying to shave a hairsbreadth (literally) off a tenon.
posts.key_insight: Calipers are a cheap, versatile precision measuring tool that every woodworker should own but most don't know about.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he's bringing guest writers to his blog to provide more diverse opinions and perspectives. The brief post sets expectations that he may or may not editorially intervene with guest content. He teases the first guest post from Kevin Smith with tool reviews.
key_points: Expanding the blog beyond a single voice by inviting guest contributors
Setting a deliberately hands-off editorial stance — he may edit, mock, or defer to guests unpredictably
The self-deprecating humor suggests a casual, community-oriented blogging philosophy
money_quotes: Some of them might even know what they're talking about.
I may or may not edit, introduce, censor, mock, defer to, or disagree with them.
You're on your own, dear reader.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted announcement of guest writers that reveals Shapiro's informal, community-first approach to blogging.

posts.summary: This is a guest post by Kevin, a woodworker and engineer, reviewing three favorite workshop tools. He advocates for the Porter Cable variable tooth saw blade for its remarkably smooth cuts on a modest table saw, a Husky portable air compressor for its quiet operation and surprising capability despite small size, and the Dremel High Speed Rotary Saw Kit as the definitively superior Dremel model. The post is a practical, no-nonsense set of recommendations from someone who values getting maximum performance from affordable, compact tools rather than buying the biggest equipment available.
key_points: A quality blade matters more than saw size — a Porter Cable variable tooth blade transformed a modest 10-inch Craftsman table saw's ability to cut oak
The Husky portable compressor proves that sacrificing raw power for portability, quiet operation, and small footprint is a worthwhile trade-off for home workshops
The Dremel High Speed Rotary Saw Kit is strictly superior to the standard model for only ~$10 more, coming with flex shaft, plunge router kit, and more horsepower
money_quotes: It's horsepower and blade quality.
When the wood is passed through you almost don't hear it cutting. The wood just disappears, it's almost freaky.
For $35 who cares about longevity, if it wears out, buy a new one.
It's as far from industrial strength as you can get. But that's what's so great.
If you aren't willing to sacrifice shop space, you aren't willing to have your ears ringing and you aren't willing to shell out more than $200 then this is the perfect compressor.
Heck, if you are willing to spend $200 I recommend you buy 2.
posts.key_insight: Smart tool choices that prioritize the right attributes — blade quality over saw size, portability over raw power, value over brand prestige — can deliver outsized results in a home workshop.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a deal on Marples Blue Chip chisels at Costco, where a 6-chisel set with a mallet is available for $29.97—significantly cheaper than Home Depot or Amazon prices. He compares it to a previous chisel deal he posted about and recommends readers pick up a set even if just for backup use on rough materials like MDF.
key_points: Costco offers a 6-chisel set with mallet for $29.97, roughly half the Amazon price
The deal is compared to a previous Two Cherries chisel deal that doubled in price shortly after being posted
Even if not used as primary chisels, the set is worth buying as backup tools for rough work
money_quotes: Costco has the 6 chisel set WITH a mallet for $29.97.
Even if you don't buy them as your main chisels, though, at this price you should pick up a set as your crappy backup chisels for when you're hacking away at MDF and such.
Swing by Costco and pick up a pair–you'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Good chisel deals are worth jumping on quickly, whether for primary use or as expendable backup tools for rough work.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a brief recommendation for Woodworker.com's technical FAQ, highlighting a specific tip about darkening walnut sapwood with vinegar. The post is a short maker-culture link share from early in his blogging career, pointing readers to a practical woodworking resource.
key_points: Woodworker.com's technical FAQ is recommended as excellent reading for woodworkers
Vinegar can be used to darken walnut sapwood, making otherwise unusable boards viable
The tip addresses a practical material recovery technique — salvaging wood from the scrap pile
money_quotes: Who knew that you could darken walnut sapwood with vinegar?
It's still weaker than heartwood, but that tidbit may allow you to use a board that would otherwise go in the scrap pile.
posts.key_insight: Practical woodworking knowledge like chemical treatments for sapwood can save materials that would otherwise be wasted.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a simple, affordable wood finishing technique using dewaxed shellac, spray lacquer, and rottenstone polishing. The method produces a tough, scratch-resistant, clear finish that works well even on difficult woods like cocobolo. The entire process can be completed in a single evening with materials costing under $20 from a hardware store. He demonstrates the technique on a portable humidor he built.
key_points: A durable, clear wood finish can be achieved with three materials: dewaxed shellac, spray lacquer, and rottenstone
The shellac base coat seals wood, improves adhesion, and can add color tone depending on the type used
Multiple light-to-medium coats of lacquer build up thickness, with final polishing done using rottenstone rubbed with water
The technique is especially useful for difficult woods like cocobolo that resist other finishes
money_quotes: You can slap this on in a night.
It's tough, scratch-resistant, alcohol and water resistant, and it goes on great over trouble woods like cocobolo.
You will wind up with a gorgeous, lustrous, clear, hard, beautiful, superlative coating.
Try some rotten polishing. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: A professional-quality wood finish is achievable in one evening with under $20 in materials using a simple shellac-lacquer-rottenstone technique.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro briefly welcomes readers of Woodshop News magazine, which ran an article about his woodworking blog 'Nothing Severed Yet.' The post is a short greeting that highlights a couple of interesting tidbits from the magazine issue, including wood prices and a kitchen design software package. It serves primarily as a cross-promotion between the magazine's audience and his blog.
key_points: Woodshop News featured Shapiro's woodworking blog 'Nothing Severed Yet' in its January 2005 issue
The post highlights niche woodworking industry details like Koa wood pricing and kitchen design software
Shapiro was actively involved in maker/woodworking culture well before his later tech ventures
money_quotes: How else will you find out that Koa's up to $60/bf, or that KitchenDraw has a $4,000 kitchen design package that you can rent for $4/hour?
posts.key_insight: This early post reveals Shapiro's longstanding roots in maker culture, years before founding Glowforge or becoming known in the tech industry.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro discusses Milwaukee Tools' announcement of a new 28-volt lithium-ion battery powered cordless tool line. He explains the technical reason the industry hadn't adopted li-ion batteries sooner: while they're lighter and pack more capacity, nickel-cadmium batteries deliver more power instantaneously, which matters for high-draw tools like saws. He expresses cautious optimism about the new tools while lamenting that Milwaukee has been acquired by TTI, a Hong Kong company that also owns Ryobi, marking another American toolmaker moving overseas.
key_points: Milwaukee Tools introduced a 28V lithium-ion cordless tool line, a significant shift from nickel-cadmium batteries
Li-ion batteries are lighter with more capacity, but ni-cad batteries historically delivered superior instantaneous power for high-current applications like saws
The key open question is whether Milwaukee solved the high current draw limitation of li-ion technology
Milwaukee's acquisition by TTI (a Hong Kong company that owns Ryobi) represents the continuing trend of US toolmakers moving overseas
money_quotes: li-ion batteries have more power, but ni-cads have always been able to deliver more power *at once*.
If you're running a saw, it's all about dumping as much power into the motor as you can.
Here's hoping they got it right, because I'd love to shed a few pounds off my cordless drill.
It's sad to see yet another US toolmaker heading overseas.
posts.key_insight: The shift from ni-cad to lithium-ion in power tools wasn't just a matter of adopting better battery chemistry—it required solving the fundamental engineering challenge of instantaneous power delivery for high-current applications.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical woodworking technique for bookmatching lumber using a table saw instead of a bandsaw. The post provides a step-by-step guide for splitting figured wood in half and reassembling it to create symmetrical, decorative patterns. He details the blade height and fence calculations, safety precautions against kickback, and finishing steps to reveal the wood's figure. It's a concise maker tutorial from early in Shapiro's blogging history, well before his AI and tech writing.
key_points: A table saw can substitute for a bandsaw when resawing small pieces of figured wood for bookmatching
The technique requires cutting from both sides to avoid cutting all the way through, which would risk dangerous kickback
The final separation is done by hand with a handsaw and chisel, not the table saw
Mineral spirits or rubbing alcohol can be used to preview the wood's figure before finishing
money_quotes: When you get lots of lumber, you'll sometimes find small areas with really cool figure.
During step 4, be sure that the blade isn't cutting all the way through. If it is, the risk of kickback is very high.
Be sure to have the cut sides facing up, as the bookmatch is closest where the wood was next to the blade.
Douse it in mineral spirits or rubbing alcohol to bring out the figure, and call over someone to admire it!
posts.key_insight: You can achieve a decent bookmatch without a bandsaw by making opposing table saw cuts that stop just short of center, then finishing the split by hand.

posts.summary: A guest author reviews the Grip-Tite magnetic feather board, a woodworking safety device invented by hobbyist Jerry Jaksha. The device uses a strong magnet to quickly attach to cast iron tool surfaces, holding wood securely against blades without complex clamping setups. The reviewer argues that its ease of use removes the temptation to skip safety precautions, especially on table saws and jointers. Despite initial sticker shock at $40, hands-on experience at a woodworking class converted the author into an enthusiast who now uses them for nearly every operation.
key_points: The Grip-Tite replaces traditional feather boards with a magnet-based system that takes seconds to set up instead of minutes
Ease of use is the key safety innovation — when safety devices are convenient, woodworkers actually use them consistently
The product was invented by a hobbyist woodworker who went through the difficult process of turning an idea into a real product
Hands-on demonstration overcame the author's price resistance, illustrating how trial experience drives adoption
money_quotes: For most woodworkers that means that they use fewer feather boards then they really should, or they cheat and end up with hands and fingers too close to hungry blades.
With a few Grip-Tites around I'm never tempted to skimp on safety for even the smallest operations.

The Grip-Tite magnetic feather board is the invention of Jerry Jaksha, a hobbyist woodworker like you and me who was willing to go through the hell that it takes to turn a great idea into a real product.
After spending a couple of days using the Grip-Tite on most setups, I was sold.
Almost nothing goes through my table saw or jointer with out one any more.
posts.key_insight: The best safety devices succeed not through superior protection but through superior convenience — when safety is effortless, people actually use it.

posts.summary: This short 2005 post is a reader-contributed correction about lithium-ion battery technology for power tools. Kevin, who built an electric VW Rabbit, corrects Dan's earlier claims about battery chemistry in Milwaukee tools. The discussion covers Li-Ion discharge rates, efficiency, and cost barriers. The post highlights that while Li-Ion batteries offer impressive power-to-weight ratios, their high cost ($145 for a single battery) remained the key obstacle to adoption in consumer power tools at the time.
key_points: Li-Ion batteries discharge at rates measured in 'C', with top-of-line cells reaching 10-15C while maintaining 80% efficiency
A 22.2V Li-Ion battery can deliver 555 watts (nearly 1 HP) while weighing just over half a pound
The primary barrier to Li-Ion adoption in power tools was cost, not performance
Most power tools at the time used Ni-MH or Ni-Cad, not Li-Ion
money_quotes: Since he's built an electric rabbit (VW, not bunny), I'm inclined to believe him.
A 1C discharge means the battery is empty in 1 hour. At 2C, empty in 30 min.
That's 555 Watts or almost 1 HP. That's one serious battery operated tool.
And the battery only weights a bit over 1/2 a pound. The problem is the battery costs $145.
posts.key_insight: In 2005, lithium-ion battery technology already had the power and weight advantages to revolutionize cordless tools, but cost was the remaining barrier to widespread adoption.

posts.summary: A guest post by Brian on Dan Shapiro's blog about a practical workshop improvement. After his wife threatened to stop doing laundry due to sawdust in the baskets, Brian built a wall to separate his garage workshop from the laundry area. He shares his first organizational find: magnetic toolbars from Lee Valley for storing chisels safely and accessibly. Dan adds a budget alternative suggestion from Ikea.
key_points: Domestic compromise drove a workshop renovation—separating the shop from the laundry area
Magnetic toolbars solve the tradeoff between keeping chisels accessible and protecting them from damage
Lee Valley's version is solidly built with protective coating; Ikea offers a budget alternative at six dollars
money_quotes: Recently my wife has been threatening to stop doing the laundry if she continued to find sawdust filling her laundry baskets.
Figuring that I had a pretty good thing going and better act quick, I used part of my Christmas vacation to add a new wall.
I hate to have chisels hidden away in a case or drawer out of easy reach.
I cringe at sound of my beloved chisels as they are knocked onto the unforgiving concrete in my garage.
Lee Valley — one of the most dangerous sites you'll ever visit!
posts.key_insight: Small, inexpensive organizational tools like magnetic bars can resolve the tension between keeping workshop tools accessible and keeping them safe.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro documents his experiments with Behlen's Rockhard Table Top Varnish as he prepares to finish a walnut dining room table. He tests three different finishing schedules on walnut cutoffs, comparing varnish alone, shellac-then-varnish, and mineral oil-then-shellac-then-varnish combinations. He finds that pre-treating with mineral oil before shellac and varnish produces the deepest, richest color because the low-viscosity oil penetrates the wood grain fully. The post is a hands-on maker's log, complete with observations about the varnish's thick consistency, strong yellow tint, and potent fumes.
key_points: Rockhard varnish is extremely thick, deeply yellow, and has powerful fumes — requiring well-ventilated application
Three finishing schedules were tested: varnish only, shellac + varnish, and mineral oil + shellac + varnish, with the oil-first approach winning for depth of color
Mineral oil penetrates walnut deeply to darken it, while shellac seals the color in and varnish builds a hard protective top coat
Remaining challenges include color-matching to a coffee table's rose tint, reducing yellowness with contrasting tint, and pore-filling for a smooth surface
money_quotes: I could feel braincells committing harakiri as soon as I opened the thing.
We're talking maple syrup here, although more Aunt Jemima than Grade B.
Not 'may slightly age and impart a tinge of yellow to your lovely antique' yellow, but yellow yellow.
This stuff is tough as a goat and it builds a thick coating fast.
I might play with getting rid of some of the yellow by adding a tiny bit of contrasting tint. This might result in quite the disaster, but that's never stopped me before.
On the right, the low-viscosity mineral oil penetrated fully, darkening the wood. Then the shellac sealed it, and finally the rockhard built up a deep finish. That's going to be the winner.
posts.key_insight: The best finish for walnut combines mineral oil for deep penetration and color, shellac to seal it in, and hard varnish on top for durability — each layer serving a distinct purpose.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro humorously rediscovers the public library through his wife's recommendation, framing it as a Netflix-like service. He then reviews 'Understanding Wood Finishing' by Bob Flexner, praising it as a practical and comprehensive guide to wood finishing techniques. The book covers topics like sanding grits, finish identification, and step-by-step instructions for different woods. His only criticism is the lack of brand-specific recommendations, which he attributes to the book's original 1993 publication date.
key_points: Shapiro uses an extended comedic bit to reintroduce the concept of a public library as a superior alternative to Netflix
He recommends 'Understanding Wood Finishing' by Bob Flexner as an excellent practical guide for woodworkers
The book's strength is its practical detail — sanding grits, finish comparisons, mystery finish identification, and step-by-step instructions with photos
The main weakness is the absence of brand-name recommendations, though the age of the book makes this forgivable
money_quotes: A few weeks ago, my wife told me about this outstanding new service. It's like Netflix, except: They let you have 10 items at a time instead of 3
They have a pretty lousy selection of DVDs, but their selection of books is outstanding
It's free!
So I went to check out this "Library". It was incredible!
What exactly is a gel finish, and when should you use it? (hint: not for any of the reasons listed on the can)
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted book recommendation post that showcases Shapiro's comedic voice and his hands-on maker interests in woodworking.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his research and practical tips on dyeing a walnut dining room table, explaining the differences between stains and dyes. He covers water-soluble vs. alcohol-soluble dyes, NGR dyes, and techniques for controlling color concentration. The post concludes with a self-deprecating lesson learned about applying water-based dye before mineral oil.
key_points: Dyes soak into wood fibers and preserve grain better than stains, which act more like dilute paint
Water-soluble dyes are more lightfast but raise the grain; alcohol-soluble dyes fade faster but don't raise grain; NGR dyes offer the best of both worlds
Dye can be heavily diluted and applied in coats for precise color control, and can be washed out with solvent if over-applied
Always apply water-based dye before oil finishes—a lesson learned the hard way
money_quotes: Stains are basically dilute paint. Dyes actually soak into the fibers, so they don't mask the grain as much as stains do.
You can dilute the heck out of dye, and then apply coats of it until it reaches the right color concentration.
In theory, you can make any dye color with red, yellow, blue, and black. In practice, it's a heck of a lot easier to start with a color that's close to what you want.
This may seem obvious (it sure does in retrospect), but put your water-based dye on first, BEFORE you put on the mineral oil. Oops.
