posts.key_insight: Wood dyes offer superior grain visibility and color control compared to stains, but proper application order and solvent choice are critical to getting good results.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces the winner of a free woodworking apron from his NSY Free Tool Review series. Richard S. from Williamsport, TN received the apron, which Shapiro humorously notes should protect him from flying splinters. The post also notes that future free tool giveaways may be delayed because manufacturers have been reluctant to provide tools for reviews.
key_points: Richard S. from Williamsport, TN won a woodworking apron from the NSY Free Tool Review giveaway
Manufacturers have been reluctant to provide free tools for review giveaways, slowing the series
Shapiro ran a tool review blog focused on practical woodworking gear
money_quotes: He got the apron a few weeks ago and, presumably, has not been had any flying splinters poke holes in his abdomen since then.
Many manufacturers have been reluctant to part with a tool for giveaways, so it may be a bit before we get to do another 'free tool' review.
I'll keep fighting the good fight for you, though!
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted giveaway announcement from Shapiro's early blogging days documenting his woodworking and maker interests.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a deal on a Sjoberg workbench found by a reader named Brian. The post highlights a $299 shipped price from Woodworker's Source, compared to $499+ at Rockler. It includes a correction noting the bench is the JPB-4 model rather than the DUO, with fixed vises. The post is a straightforward product recommendation from an early blogging era.
key_points: A Sjoberg JPB-4 workbench is available for $299 shipped, significantly below the $500+ typical price
The post corrects an initial misidentification of the bench model, showing community feedback in action
Shapiro recommends having freight held at the terminal to avoid shipping damage
money_quotes: The second thing I did was rush over to share it with our wonderful readers. First thing I did was to make sure I got mine at this price.
No that wasn't a typo the $299 gets it shipped to your door!!
posts.key_insight: An early blog post sharing a woodworking deal that reflects Shapiro's long-standing maker culture roots, well before his later ventures in tech and laser cutting.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a reader comment following up on his earlier review of the SawStop table saw, a safety-focused power tool. The reader, Dave Wright, reports owning the saw and points to early reviews from woodworkers who purchased it themselves. Wright notes that while expensive, the SawStop's premium is comparable to upgrading trim levels on an SUV — a matter of priorities rather than unreasonable cost. The post serves as a brief community update on an emerging product.
key_points: Early real-world reviews of the SawStop table saw were beginning to surface from woodworkers who bought it with their own money
The SawStop's higher price is framed as comparable to paying for a premium vehicle trim — a reasonable upgrade for those who prioritize safety
The post highlights community engagement and reader expertise as valuable follow-up to product commentary
money_quotes: It's expensive, but you'll pay about the same to get the Eddie Bauer version of an SUV instead of the regular one. For most people it's just a matter of priorities.
posts.key_insight: The SawStop table saw's safety premium is comparable to a vehicle trim upgrade, making it a matter of personal priorities rather than prohibitive cost.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro highlights a chisel review by David Wright that he considers the best he's ever read. The post serves as a brief recommendation, linking to Wright's PDF comparison of high-end chisels. Shapiro notes that Wright's review significantly influenced his own earlier writing about chisel shopping. Wright adds a postscript confirming his original opinions held up over extended use, with Two Cherries and Stanley 750 remaining his favorites.
key_points: Shapiro endorses David Wright's comprehensive high-end chisel review as exceptionally well-done
Wright's long-term use confirmed his original review findings, lending credibility to first-impression tool evaluations
The post reflects Shapiro's engagement with the woodworking community and hands-on maker culture
money_quotes: In fact, it was the article that had the greatest impact on me when I wrote this piece about chisel shopping.
Opinions formed when writing that article have generally been proven true over extended use as well.
The Sorby and Henry Taylor still dull quickly though it's easy to put a nice edge on them.
posts.key_insight: A thoughtful initial tool review, when done rigorously, tends to hold up over years of real-world use.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces the addition of an email subscription option for his blog Nothing Severed Yet (NSY). He walks readers through the simple signup process, jokes about the email service provider being Nigerian, and notes he also switched providers from Bloglet to Feedblitz due to reliability issues. The post is a lighthearted, self-deprecating announcement typical of mid-2000s blogging culture.
key_points: Email subscriptions added to Nothing Severed Yet blog by popular request (one person asked)
The service was initially powered by Bloglet but switched to Feedblitz due to reliability problems
RSS feeds are also available as an alternative for tech-savvy readers
The post reflects early blogging infrastructure challenges around content distribution
money_quotes: You can use this to get the latest NSY articles sent directly to your inbox, bypassing that pesky "web" thing completely.
The whole service is run by the good folks at Bloglet, a small Nigerian company that has lots of experience managing email lists. Ha ha, just kidding.
I switched the service from Bloglet to Feedblitz, since Bloglet was being flakey as all getout.
It seems like I unsubscribe to things more often than I subscribe to them.
* Somebody asked. ** If you're that guy who asked.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted early-blogging-era announcement about adding email subscriptions, notable mainly as a time capsule of 2005-era content distribution challenges and the informal voice of personal blogging.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro's blog hosts a guest review by contributor Kevin of the Ridgid 4-gallon mini shop vac, purchased for $70 at Home Depot. The review evaluates the vac on four criteria: price, size, noise level, and fine dust filtration, finding it satisfactory on all counts. The main complaint is the non-standard 1 7/8" hose diameter that is incompatible with other shop vac accessories. Overall, the reviewer considers it a good value for a compact, portable shop vac that performs well for woodworking tasks like sanding built-in bookshelves.
key_points: The Ridgid 4-gallon wet/dry vac scores well on price ($70), compactness, and fine dust filtration when connected to an orbital sander
Noise levels are acceptable — quieter than the sander itself, though not truly quiet
The non-standard 1 7/8" hose diameter is a significant design flaw with no adapter provided, incompatible with standard shop vac accessories
The small capacity isn't a practical disadvantage since filter clogging, not tank fullness, determines when you need to stop and clean
money_quotes: I could buy Tide for $14, but the Albertsons house brand is pretty tempting at $7. Sometimes I've found a good deal, sometimes you get what you pay for.
The hose is 1 7/8" diameter. Somebody should get fired for this. Seriously, fired.
Rigid still uses the standard sizes on all their vacs except for on this one.
My girlfriend has assured me that size doesn't matter.
I've always needed to empty my shopvac when the filter gets clogged, not when the vac gets full.
posts.key_insight: The Ridgid mini shop vac is a solid budget choice for woodworkers who value portability and fine dust filtration, despite a baffling non-standard hose size.

posts.summary: Shapiro provides a technical correction to his earlier woodworking advice about finishing with Behlen's Rockhard Table Top Varnish. He discovered that his recommended finishing schedule of mineral oil, shellac, then varnish doesn't work because the varnish fails to adhere properly to the shellac. He offers three corrective steps: skip mineral oil or use a hardening oil, scuff the shellac before varnishing, and don't thin the first coat over shellac. He self-deprecatingly admits he should have just followed the instructions on the can.
key_points: Residual liquid mineral oil reduces surface tension and causes varnish to flow out unevenly
Shellac needs to be scuffed with sandpaper before applying varnish to promote adhesion
When wood is already sealed by shellac, the first coat of varnish should not be thinned
Following manufacturer instructions is often better than improvising finishing schedules
money_quotes: Or you could just skip the whole shellac thing and follow the instructions on the can, instead of being difficult like me.
Fabulous finishers frequently finish firewood first.
As you may have gathered, I am not a fabulous finisher. But the Rockhard is a pretty fabulous finish.
posts.key_insight: When experimenting with wood finishing techniques, sometimes the best approach is to follow the manufacturer's instructions rather than improvising a complex multi-step schedule.

posts.summary: This is a guest review by Brian Todoroff on the Nothing Severed Yet blog, evaluating the Jack Rabbit Deluxe Set, a tool designed to eliminate the tedious process of switching between drill bits and driver bits when drilling, countersinking, and driving screws. The review was prompted by a pantry-building project that required 40 screws and caused hand fatigue from constant bit switching. The reviewer praises the tool's machining quality, thoughtful design, and ease of use, noting it reduces the drill-to-drive switch to under a second. The only criticism is a depth stop shape that can leave slight crescent marks on wood surfaces. The tool earns a 4 out of 5 rating.
key_points: The Jack Rabbit tool eliminates the need to swap bits by allowing sub-second switching between drilling/countersinking and driving modes
The tool uses a collet system that accepts standard twist drill bits, making replacements easy and ensuring perfect centering
Build quality and machining are notably high, with thoughtful design touches like storage that keeps the last-used bit installed
The only drawback is the depth stop's contoured shape, which can leave slight crescent depressions in wood if not careful
money_quotes: I felt like someone took pride in the quality of the tool, not just how cheaply they could make it.
The magnetizer was so well made that I spend a minute or two just turning it over in my hand and generally fondling it.
The switch from drill to drive and back takes less than a second and is done with two fingers while the drill remains ready in your other hand.
This is one of those tools that inspires confidence because it just feels right.
Hmmm… there goes the reason for a second drill. Guess I'll have to think up a new excuse to buy another tool…
posts.key_insight: A well-designed specialty tool that solves a specific workflow friction point can eliminate the perceived need for additional equipment entirely.

posts.summary: Shapiro reviews free poker chip box plans from Rockler, using them as a springboard to rant about woodworking plan design philosophy. He argues that most published woodworking plans are needlessly complex, optimized for commercial manufacturing constraints (cheap lumber, expensive labor) rather than hobbyist workshops where the opposite is true. He advocates for simple designs using quality solid wood over complex joinery holding together plywood. The Rockler box plan earns his praise for using a clever shortcut (pre-made walnut molding) to achieve an impressive look without unnecessary complexity.
key_points: Most woodworking plans are designed around commercial manufacturing constraints (cheap materials, complex labor) that don't apply to hobbyists
Hobbyists should spend more on quality solid wood and less time on unnecessarily complex joinery
Good design means simple construction with snazzy results, not elaborate complexity that makes pieces look more commercial
The Rockler poker chip box plan is praised for using pre-made walnut molding to get an impressive profile without difficult work
money_quotes: Why do woodworking plans always go out of their way to make things difficult?
I look at plans in magazines, and they're five times as difficult as anything I've ever built, and they look half as good.
They look like something you'd buy at a store, where labor is done by giant robots or tiny children, but lumber is expensive.
If I'm planning to spend six months building a coffee table, I don't mind spending $200 on some beautiful 5/4 FAS walnut boards.
Your story is going to be a lot less convincing if they notice the place where you dropped your beer stein on the corner and dented the poplar veneer.
Simple designs, snazzy result, lots of solid wood.
posts.key_insight: Good woodworking design for hobbyists means investing in quality materials and simple construction rather than mimicking commercial manufacturing's complex joinery on cheap substrates.

posts.summary: This 2005 blog post is a guest review by professional cabinetmaker Mike Ewanchuk of Finger Gloves, a product designed for hand-staining wood. The review compares Finger Gloves against standard disposable latex gloves during a real staining project. Mike finds that Finger Gloves dramatically outperform latex in durability, grip, and reusability, requiring zero changes during a full coat versus six pairs of latex gloves. The post is part of Dan Shapiro's 'free tool review' series on his woodworking blog Nothing Severed Yet.
key_points: Finger Gloves lasted an entire staining session without tearing, compared to six pairs of disposable latex gloves for the same project
The product offers better grip and dexterity than latex gloves thanks to textured nubs on the fingertips
Despite higher per-unit cost, the reusability and durability make Finger Gloves more economical than disposable latex
The gloves have crossover utility beyond staining, such as rose trimming for thorn protection
money_quotes: I firmly maintain that these facts are unrelated, because admitting otherwise would mean having to clean my shop.
By the time the box was done, I had gone through six pairs of latex gloves.
I was able to cover the entire box without changing gloves once.
You will almost forget you are wearing them.
His shop is so clean you could eat off the floor (and his one year old daughter occasionally does).
posts.key_insight: For hand-staining work, reusable finger-specific gloves can be more durable, practical, and cost-effective than disposable latex gloves.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief post from 2005 where Dan Shapiro shares a link to pinktoolbelts.com, a website selling pink-colored tools. The post's title humorously references his blog's inclusive stance while highlighting a product that challenges gendered assumptions about tools and maker culture. At only 28 words of actual content, it serves as a quick link-share rather than a substantive article.
key_points: Shares a link to pinktoolbelts.com as a novelty find
The humorous title signals inclusivity in maker/DIY culture regardless of gender or style preferences
Reflects early-2000s blog culture of short link-sharing posts
money_quotes: The web site name pretty much says it all.
posts.key_insight: Maker culture and tools shouldn't be constrained by gendered expectations — a point made with minimal words and maximum humor.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a practical woodworking tip about wood movement — the fact that wood expands and contracts with humidity changes throughout the year. He explains that wood moves more across the grain than with it, which affects joinery and finishing decisions. He recommends an online tool called the 'Shrinkulator' for calculating dimensional changes in wood based on humidity and wood type.
key_points: Wood dimensions change seasonally, with wide pieces shifting by up to 1/8 inch due to humidity
Wood moves more across the grain than with it, making cross-grain joinery risky
Both sides of wood should be finished identically to prevent warping
The 'Shrinkulator' tool calculates expected dimensional changes based on humidity and wood species
money_quotes: One of the things most beginning woodworkers don't realize is that wood moves through out the year.
Table tops and other wide pieces can easily change size by 1/8th inch and back through out the year.
Wood also moves much more across the grain than with it, which is why you have to be careful when you join two pieces of wood with the grain running different directions.
posts.key_insight: Wood is a living material that moves seasonally with humidity, and understanding this movement is essential for building furniture that lasts.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a returning deal on a high-quality solid wood workbench from Woodworker's Source, available at half price. The post is a brief product recommendation pointing readers to a $600 workbench selling for just under $300, complete with bench dog holes and clamps.
key_points: A $600 solid wood workbench is available for under $300 from Woodworker's Source
The deal is a return of a previously shared bargain found by Brian
The workbench includes bench dog holes and clamps
money_quotes: Woodworker's Source is selling a $600 solid wood workbench, complete with bench dog holes and clamps, for just under $300.
posts.key_insight: A brief deal alert highlighting a quality woodworking workbench at half price, reflecting Shapiro's early maker culture interests.

posts.summary: Shapiro compares the Dowelmax doweling jig against a biscuit joiner in a head-to-head woodworking joint strength test. He glued up two joints in a walnut board—one with dowels, one with biscuits—and after a week of curing, smashed the board against a pipe. The biscuit joint broke on the first impact while the dowel joint held. He also notes the Dowelmax produced a perfectly flush joint with minimal effort, while the biscuit joint was off by 1/16th of an inch. His verdict is unequivocal: the Dowelmax is worth its higher price tag and outperforms cheaper alternatives in accuracy, ease of use, speed, and strength.
key_points: The Dowelmax doweling jig produces more accurate joints than a biscuit joiner with less effort—perfectly flush alignment versus 1/16th inch offset
In a destructive strength test, the biscuit joint failed on the first impact while the Dowelmax dowel joint survived
Despite costing more than cheap doweling jigs or comparable biscuit joiners, the Dowelmax ranks near the top in usefulness-per-dollar
The review approach is empirical and hands-on: same board, same wood species, same curing time, direct comparison
money_quotes: In terms of usefullness-per-dollar, this sucker is near the top of my list.
It absolutely blows away the competition for accuracy, ease of use, speed, and strength.
I wasn't working particularly hard at aligning either joint, but the Dowelmax one came out perfectly flush anyway, while the biscuit was about 1/16th off.
I grabbed the board firmly by both edges and slammed it down on a pipe in the center to distribute the force equally to both joints.
Sell your biscuit joiner for scrap metal and hawk the biscuits on street corners.
posts.key_insight: A rigorous head-to-head test shows the Dowelmax doweling jig decisively beats biscuit joiners in both alignment accuracy and joint strength, justifying its higher price.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro discloses that his site has become a Rockler affiliate after Rockler noticed he'd been linking to their content. He humorously explains that any purchases through his affiliate links will support the site, then jokes that the money actually goes toward buying lunch. The post is a brief, tongue-in-cheek transparency notice about monetization.
key_points: Rockler reached out to formalize an affiliate relationship after noticing existing links to their content
Shapiro discloses the affiliate arrangement to readers as an ethical transparency measure
He undercuts the seriousness of the disclosure with self-deprecating humor about using the money for lunch
money_quotes: Rockler just sent me a very nice email asking if we'd like to become a Rockler affiliate, since evidently we've been pilfering their affiliate content for a while (without really knowing that's what we were doing).
Here at NSY we hew to a high ethical standard, plus it's obvious from looking at the web links.
Just kidding, I use it to buy lunch.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted early-blog-era disclosure of an affiliate monetization arrangement, notable mainly for its casual transparency and self-deprecating humor.

posts.summary: This early blog post reflects on the invisible craftsmanship behind well-designed tools. Shapiro (via contributing editor Brian Todoroff) argues that good designers agonize over countless small details that most users never consciously notice. The post uses a woodworking depth stop as a case study, featuring designer Brian Giffin's explanation of why he made specific material and geometry choices. The conclusion is an appreciation for the unseen engineering effort that makes everyday tools work seamlessly.
key_points: The best tools become invisible through good design — users never think about why they work so well
Good designers obsess over details like handle length, switch placement, and material choice, spending far more thought on each aspect than users ever will
Brian Giffin's depth stop design illustrates thoughtful engineering: convex shape for angled drilling, brass for heat dissipation, and chip relief for consistent depth
The 80% solution philosophy — imperfect but meaningfully better than nothing — reflects practical design thinking
money_quotes: In some dark corner that you tell no one about is also that colossal waste of money. Perhaps it has long since been forcefully ejected from your sawdust kingdom, but the memory of the wonderful project it ruined still haunts you.
How did they know to add that little curve to give your thumb just enough clearance? How did they come up with the million other details that make everything come together so smoothly?
In most cases they put infinitely more thought into each little aspect of the design then most of us ever put into using the tool.
I made it convex so that it contacts the inner part first, even when slightly angled.
It's not a perfect solution but 80% seemed better than nothing.
posts.key_insight: The best product design is invisible — great tools work so well because their designers obsessed over details that users will never consciously notice.

posts.summary: A brief reminder post announcing the deadline for a free Finger Gloves giveaway. Readers are directed to sign up by April 1st for a chance to win. The post contains no substantive argument or analysis.
key_points: Announces April 1st deadline for a free Finger Gloves giveaway
Links to a previous product review post
Encourages readers to subscribe for future updates
posts.key_insight: A short promotional reminder with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: This guest post by Brian Todoroff reviews the experience of ordering lumber online rather than buying from local suppliers. Living in the San Francisco Bay Area where wood prices are high and selection is poor, Brian discovered that mail-order wood could beat local prices even after shipping while offering broader selection. He reviews two suppliers — New England Specialty Lumber Supply (a small operation with great prices and personal service) and Woodworker's Source (a larger operation with excellent selection and quality). The post covers practical considerations like rough vs. surfaced lumber, shipping weight thresholds, and quality expectations.
key_points: Mail-order lumber can beat local Bay Area prices even after shipping costs, with broader wood selection
Shipping has distinct cost tiers: UPS/FedEx for under 70 lbs, truck freight above 200 lbs, with a costly no-man's-land in between
Surfaced (S2S) lumber saves planing time but arrives thinner and may warp during shipping, creating thickness inconsistencies
Small dealers offer personal service and competitive prices while larger operations provide better selection and faster turnaround
money_quotes: It's great to poke around the stacks and discover that one spectacular piece with the burl inclusion and a bit of crossgrain ripple that just screams 'jewelry box!' in a voice that only you can hear.
It always seemed like a silly and expensive thing to try to ship a good chunk of a tree cross-country, sight unseen, in hopes of getting something workable.
From 70 lbs to 200 lbs is a shipping no-man's-land where the best deal varies for different orders.
If you're lucky enough to have one, be sure to support them as there is no better friend when you need to find those matched boards for a table top.
Once I started looking into it, however, I found that I could easily beat the local prices–even after shipping–and have a much broader range of wood to pick from.
posts.key_insight: Mail-order lumber is a viable and often superior alternative to local suppliers, offering better prices and selection despite the counterintuitive economics of shipping heavy wood cross-country.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro describes his search for a black wood alternative for a contemporary wall case design. After considering temperamental ebony and chemically treated walnut, he discovers that India ink provides an excellent solution for blackening wood. He experiments with it on birch plywood and finds it produces an impressively deep black finish with minimal effort, preserving grain texture and holding up well under top coating. The post concludes with his satisfaction at finding a practical, safe, and effective ebony substitute.
key_points: Ebony is difficult to work with and prone to splitting, making alternatives worth exploring
India ink (super fine carbon in solvent and binder) is safe, permanent, and produces the deepest possible black
A tiny amount of India ink covers a large area and dries quickly with no bleeding or loss of wood grain detail
The ink holds up well under oil/varnish top coating, making it practical for finished furniture pieces
money_quotes: Ebony is notoriously temperamental when it comes to working it and has a nasty reputation for suddenly splitting long after you believe all danger is past.
Another bonus is that carbon, being an element, simply won't fade and is as black as black gets.
Probably take a few coats to get real black… a bit on the tip of the brush… let's see what happens… Dang! That's black!
It has a nice texture that doesn't look painted and the grain looks unchanged.
For now though, I do believe I've found my ebony substitute.
posts.key_insight: India ink offers woodworkers a safe, permanent, and remarkably effective alternative to ebony, producing a deep black finish that preserves wood grain and accepts top coats without bleeding.

posts.summary: A brief post recommending a sale on clamps from Penn State Industries, noting they are significantly cheaper than comparable Jorgensen clamps on Amazon. Shapiro vouches for PSI based on past positive ordering experiences. The post is written in a playful tone under the byline of 'Nothing Severed Yet,' a woodworking publication.
key_points: Penn State Industries clamps are less than half the price of Jorgensen clamps on Amazon
Shapiro has had consistently positive experiences ordering from PSI
The post reflects Shapiro's hands-on maker and woodworking hobby
money_quotes: Jorgergengerhansonson (or however you spell it) clamps on Amazon are more than twice as much.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted woodworking tip reflecting Shapiro's maker roots long before his tech career took center stage.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his visit to The Woodworking Shows, contrasting it favorably with the tech trade shows he attends for work. Unlike typical industry conventions filled with inane pitches and badge-swiping salespeople, the woodworking show features inventors personally demonstrating their tools and inviting hands-on participation. The show also offers substantive hour-long workshops that go beyond product promotion to teach genuine techniques. Shapiro's highlight was meeting the inventor of his Incra router jig and learning a trick to cut finger joints four times faster.
key_points: Tech trade shows are painful exercises in dodging sales pitches and junk mail, while woodworking shows offer genuine hands-on engagement with inventors
The woodworking show bridges commerce and education effectively, with sponsored presenters teaching broadly useful techniques rather than just shilling products
Direct access to tool inventors creates uniquely valuable learning opportunities that can dramatically improve craft skills
The show features an eclectic range of tools from laser woodworking to digital precision gauges to thumb-proof hammers
money_quotes: Generally they are a pain. The goal is to find something that passes for being interesting on the show floor, while spending as little time as possible listening to inane sales pitches for things you don't care about and wouldn't take for free let alone pay money for.
It's a game where the losers are deluged by junkmail forever.
But not sell you on the idea of something, actually sell you something!
While the presenters are technically sponsored by various companies, they do a lot more than shill products for their corporate masters.
He showed me a trick that will literally cut my finger-joint-making time by 4–that is, you can cut all four boards at once.
posts.key_insight: The best trade shows succeed by replacing abstract sales pitches with hands-on demonstrations and genuine knowledge sharing between makers.

posts.summary: This is a guest book review by contributing editor Brian Todoroff reviewing Taunton Press's 'The New Best of Fine Woodworking' book series. The series collects selected articles from Fine Woodworking Magazine over the past decade, organized by topic. The reviewer finds that the topical grouping adds value even for existing magazine subscribers. Despite minor editing errors, the books are recommended for both new and experienced woodworkers seeking consolidated reference material on specific techniques.
key_points: The book series compiles a decade of Fine Woodworking articles organized by specific topics like finishing, router techniques, and shop layout
Grouping articles by subject adds value beyond the original magazines by enabling focused exploration of a single topic
Minor editing errors exist but don't interfere with comprehension, and outdated pricing/brands are helpfully marked
The series serves both beginners seeking foundational knowledge and experienced woodworkers looking for alternative approaches
money_quotes: Taunton has always managed to bring a deep sense of quality to everything they produce.
Having all these articles grouped together by subject made it a joy to explore a topic.
By combining tidbits from different articles, I was able to pick up several tips and details to make me feel comfortable with trying a new finish.
For the old dog looking to learn a new trick, it's great to be able to thumb through one volume with a variety of alternatives and viewpoints on a topic.
I've never been disappointed with Taunton's offerings.
posts.key_insight: Curating and organizing existing content by topic creates new value beyond the sum of its parts, making reference material more accessible and actionable for practitioners at all levels.

posts.summary: A brief post from 2005 where Shapiro promotes a colleague's blog documenting a kitchen remodel. The post humorously describes the remodel process as falling between 'the second mortgage and the nervous breakdown.' There is no substantive argument or analysis — it is a casual link-sharing post typical of early blogging culture.
key_points: Shapiro highlights a contributing editor's new blog about a kitchen remodel
The post captures the casual, link-sharing style of mid-2000s blogging
money_quotes: He's still at stage 2, which comes after the second mortgage and before the nervous breakdown.
posts.key_insight: A lightweight early-blog-era link post with no substantive argument — notable only as an artifact of mid-2000s blogging culture.

posts.summary: This is a very brief 2005 blog post announcing the winner of a Jack Rabbit Deluxe tool set giveaway. Rob Chatlin won the set as part of a tool review series on Dan Shapiro's blog. The post includes a short testimonial from the winner praising the tool's ease of use. It's a minor maker-culture post from Shapiro's early blogging days, focused on physical tools rather than software or AI.
key_points: Rob Chatlin won a Jack Rabbit Deluxe Set from a blog giveaway tied to a tool review
The winner praised the tool's speed for drilling pilot holes and ease of switching to screwdriver mode
Shapiro's early blog featured regular free tool reviews and giveaways for readers
money_quotes: Drilling the pilot holes and countersinks is fast once the adjustments are made for the given situation, and switching to the screw driver couldn't be easier.
posts.key_insight: A brief giveaway announcement from Shapiro's early blogging era, reflecting his longstanding interest in maker tools and community engagement.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares detailed plans for building a portable humidor made from walnut and cocobolo wood. The post provides an 11-page PDF with diagrams, photos, and step-by-step instructions. He also experiments with open-source sharing by releasing all source materials—including the publisher file, full-resolution photos, and 3D model files—under a Creative Commons license. He encourages others to remix, translate, or repurpose the materials however they see fit.
key_points: Provides a complete 11-page PDF plan for building a portable humidor from walnut and cocobolo
Releases all source materials (publisher files, photos, 3D models) for others to remix under Creative Commons
Curates a detailed list of woodworking resources covering techniques like bookmatching, lacquer finishing, and working with cocobolo
Embraces early open-source/maker culture ethos by sharing not just finished plans but editable source files
money_quotes: I'm also trying something a little different. I've included the source materials that I used to make this.
If you want, you "remix" this any way you choose–translate it to ancient greek, fix my typos, or use the pictures as a background for an adult-oriented woodworking site.
I have no idea why anyone would want to do this, but there you have it.
One more thing–if you do make one, post a comment or send me a picture! I'll thank you for it.
posts.key_insight: Sharing not just finished creative work but all source materials under open licenses invites community participation and remix—an early expression of the maker culture ethos.

posts.summary: This early blog post is a practical guide to choosing quality finishing brushes for woodworking projects. Todoroff (a contributing editor posted on Shapiro's blog) explains the three key features of a good brush: a fluid reservoir, long bent bristles, and flagged ends. The post argues that investing in a quality brush is essential because it's the last tool to touch a finished project. It also covers the practical distinction between natural and synthetic bristles, recommending natural for oil-based finishes and synthetic for water-based ones.
key_points: Three features define a quality brush: a fluid reservoir (gap in the middle), long bent bristles (one fiber folded in half), and flagged (split) ends for smooth finish delivery
Most brushing failures come from rushing successive coats or using cheap brushes, not from the technique itself
Natural bristles (animal hair) work best for oil and solvent-based finishes, while synthetic bristles are better for water-based finishes because natural bristles absorb water and swell
A good brush is a long-term investment that makes finishing easier and produces better results
money_quotes: Don't let all the FUD fool you; brushing is a great way to apply many types of finish, and it's not a lot more work than most other techniques.
Where most people get into trouble when brushing is either rushing successive coats or using a cheap brush.
A brush can last for many years if you take good care of it.
The bristles are one long fiber bent in half at the top–not a whole bunch of short fibers stuck together.
Like a professional swimmer's hair, your brush should have a severe case of split ends.
Remember that the brush is the last thing to touch the valuable project you worked so hard and so long on, so don't skimp at the end.
posts.key_insight: The quality of your finishing brush—defined by its fluid reservoir, bent bristles, and flagged ends—is the most important factor in achieving a good brushed finish on woodworking projects.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro delivers a passionate rant against brushing finishes in woodworking, detailing every frustrating step from mixing to cleaning to recoating. He walks through the problems with getting the right consistency, maintaining expensive brushes, dealing with bubbles, and the technique required to avoid drips and runs. His alternative recommendation is to use wipe-on or spray-on finishes with cheaper, disposable applicators. The post is a practical maker's guide disguised as a comedic complaint.
key_points: Brush finishes require fussy preparation including diluting, separate containers, and avoiding contamination
Quality brushes are expensive and nearly impossible to clean well enough to reuse more than a few times
The actual brushing technique is difficult, requiring careful loading, application, and leveling to avoid drips and bubbles
Wipe-on and spray-on finishes with disposable applicators solve most of these problems at the cost of needing more coats
money_quotes: I hate brushing finishes. I don't dislike it, or prefer not to do it. I flat out despise it.
Brushing poses all sorts of problems, or shall I say, 'technique-enhancing opportunities'.
Usually, but not always, the bubbles rise to the surface of the finish and pop, so you don't get a finish that looks like frozen ginger-ale. Usually.
Repeat this a few cycles (usually one in my case) and you'll discover that you actually didn't wash it well enough and it's now ruined, at which point you may proceed directly back to step 1.
Each recoat requires a re-stir, a re-clean, and a whole lot more re-annoyingness.
Consider tossing your brushes. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: The frustrations of brush-on finishes in woodworking can be entirely sidestepped by switching to wipe-on or spray-on alternatives with disposable applicators.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a lighthearted promotion for novelty t-shirts sold through NothingSeveredYet.com, a woodworking humor site. The shirts commemorate close calls with power tools and serve as tongue-in-cheek reminders about shop safety. The post notes positive reception at a woodworking show and highlights the availability of various sizes including infant sizes for future woodworkers.
key_points: Promotes novelty t-shirts for woodworkers who've had close calls with tools
The shirts were test-marketed at the Santa Clara woodworking show with positive reception
NothingSeveredYet.com uses humor to address workshop safety awareness
Available in multiple colors, styles, and even infant sizes
money_quotes: Most of us have done something really stupid at one time or another and been glad to come out of it with all our digits.
Commemorate that, err… 'moment of clarity' and remind yourself to keep it a once in a lifetime experience.
Infant sizes are even available for the budding woodworker.
posts.key_insight: A playful promotion for woodworking safety humor merchandise, reflecting the maker community's self-deprecating attitude toward shop mishaps.

posts.summary: A brief post sharing enthusiasm for Tetris-piece-shaped modular shelving being sold online. Shapiro expresses interest in making his own version as a DIY project, appreciating the clever design that lets buyers assemble the shelves in any configuration they want.
key_points: Tetris-shaped modular shelving offers customizable assembly in any configuration
Shapiro's maker instinct is to build his own version rather than buy it
money_quotes: I want to knock this off for my next project.
Assemble them however you want while trying not to get that crazy russian tetris music stuck in your head.
posts.key_insight: A maker's first instinct upon seeing a clever product is to figure out how to build it themselves.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical advice for reducing tablesaw noise in a home workshop. He describes his own journey from a loud Grizzly contractor saw that annoyed neighbors to a quiet cabinet saw that lets him work while his wife sleeps. He offers three tiers of solutions: upgrading to a cabinet saw with its enclosed base and heavier construction, aligning your existing saw to reduce vibration, and adding accessories like link belts, zero-clearance inserts, and premium blades. The post is a concise, opinionated guide from an enthusiastic woodworker.
key_points: Cabinet saws are significantly quieter than contractor saws due to enclosed bases, housed motors, and heavier steel construction that damps vibration
Proper saw alignment reduces both noise and vibration while also improving cut quality
Aftermarket accessories like link belts, zero-clearance inserts, and premium blades can each meaningfully reduce noise
money_quotes: My old Grizzly contractor saw was so loud that I had neighbors complain when I fired it up. Admittedly it was one in the morning. But still.
I can rip 8/4 maple while my wife sleeps soundly in the bedroom upstairs.
Honey, I want you to sleep better at night, so…
In addition to giving you smoother, straighter, cleaner cuts, tuning up your tablesaw will reduce vibration during the cut, drastically reducing noise.
Quit the quivering, vamoose the vibration, and reduce the roar.
posts.key_insight: A quieter tablesaw comes from addressing noise at every level — the machine itself, its alignment, and its accessories — turning a shop disruption into a non-issue.

posts.summary: A guest post by Brian Todoroff reviewing PowerTwist Plus V-Belts for woodworking machinery. The post marvels at how these segmented, link-based belts produce smoother and quieter operation than traditional smooth belts, defying intuitive engineering logic. The belts are modular, allowing users to create any length by adding or removing segments, and can be swapped between machines. Todoroff recommends them as an easy, cost-effective upgrade for any belt-driven workshop machinery.
key_points: PowerTwist Plus V-Belts produce smoother operation despite being made of interlocking segments rather than a continuous smooth belt
The modular link design eliminates the need to find exact belt lengths and allows quick swaps between machines
The counterintuitive engineering makes them work better than conventional belts for vibration reduction
They are a high-value, easy upgrade for belt-driven woodworking machinery
money_quotes: Being an arrogant engineer by day, I pride myself on understanding how things really work, but these beauties make no sense whatsoever.
You'd think that for a smooth running belt you'd want, well… a smooth belt.
In a twist of logic worthy of Douglas Adams, these belts are about as far from smooth as possible.
Best I can figure someone sold their soul to make these work.
Don't try to make sense of them, just enjoy 'em.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the best engineering solutions are counterintuitive — a segmented, rough-looking belt can outperform a smooth one for vibration reduction in workshop machinery.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a resource he discovered that compiles dozens of DIY poker table building projects from woodworkers across the web. He notes the variety of designs and sizes available, covering different card games, and highlights that most include instructions and all have pictures. The post is a brief, enthusiastic recommendation for fellow woodworkers interested in building their own poker tables, with Shapiro humorously admitting he may be the last woodworker who hasn't built one yet.
key_points: A comprehensive collection of DIY poker table designs exists online, aggregated from dozens of woodworkers
The designs span multiple card games and come with instructions and photos
All designs are shared free of charge, embodying the open maker community spirit
money_quotes: Evidently I'm not the only woodworker who has contemplated building a poker table… actually, judging by this site, I may be the only one who hasn't done it yet.
Oh, and did I mention the designs are all free of charge?
posts.key_insight: The maker community freely shares detailed build plans, making ambitious woodworking projects accessible to anyone willing to try.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro explains the Incra positioning system for woodworking, demystifying how it differs from traditional dovetail jigs. He breaks down its three operating modes: micro-positioning for thousandth-of-an-inch adjustments, free sliding for large movements, and the key 'lock in' mode that snaps to the nearest 1/32 inch. The post argues that this snap-to-position mechanism enables perfect repeatability for dovetail joints. He concludes with a strong endorsement, noting that every woodworker he knows has adopted the system.
key_points: The Incra system is a router fence with a positioning system, not a traditional dovetail jig
The three operating modes (micro-positioning, free sliding, lock in) enable precise, repeatable cuts
The lock-in mode's snap-to-nearest-1/32-inch feature is the key innovation enabling perfect repeatability
The system comes with step-by-step templates that produce flawless joints even without full understanding of the mechanism
money_quotes: There's two things to understand about the Incra system. One is that it is a router fence, not a jig.

You can make a cut, slide it just about 1″ away, and it will snap to exactly 1″ away. Then you can move it back to your first cut, and it will snap to exactly where your first cut was. Over and over again, perfect repeatability.
It's simple and brilliant.
They provide step-by-step instructions that will give you flawless joints every time, even if you don't understand how you got them.
Literally every woodworker I know uses an Incra now, many of them after trying mine out.
posts.key_insight: The Incra positioning system's genius lies in its snap-to-position lock-in mode, which transforms imprecise manual movements into perfectly repeatable precision cuts.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares practical lessons learned from a week of cutting dovetail joints, a traditional woodworking technique. The post catalogs ten specific tips covering material preparation, tool calibration, gluing technique, clamping strategy, and salvaging mistakes. Written in a casual, numbered-list format, it reflects hands-on trial and error at the workbench. The overall message is that dovetails require precision at every step, but mistakes are recoverable with the right tricks.
key_points: Precision in material preparation (jointing and planing everything square) is essential for dovetail joints to fit properly
Cutting speed matters differently for pins vs tails — fast for pins, slow for tails to avoid splintering
Glue placement is critical: avoid end grain on pin boards, use extra in tail slots where the router bit removes excess material
Mistakes are recoverable — cracked accent pieces can be invisibly repaired, and liquid wood filler hides imperfections
money_quotes: I've been making dovetails for the past week, which means I've screwed up a lot of dovetails.
If it ain't square, the joints don't fly.
Cut the pins fast; they'll come out OK. Cut the tails slow, especially as the bit leaves the wood.
Think twice before you apply glue.
Once the thing is together, pack all your mistakes with liquid wood. No one will ever notice.
posts.key_insight: Dovetail joinery demands precision in preparation and patience in execution, but nearly every mistake can be salvaged with clever gluing, clamping, and filling techniques.

posts.summary: Shapiro shares a practical woodworking tip about filling holes in walnut burl using clear epoxy. He walks through the iterative problem-solving process: epoxy drains through holes that penetrate the board, taping the back traps air bubbles, and the solution is a pinhole in the tape that gets sealed once epoxy starts dripping. It's a short, hands-on maker post from early in his blogging history.
key_points: Clear epoxy can fill burl holes but drains through if the hole penetrates the board
Taping the back traps air and creates bubbles; a pinhole solves both drainage and air problems
Epoxy fumes are deceptively dangerous because they're nearly odorless but toxic
money_quotes: It turns out that if you have a hole that goes all the way through the board, epoxy (even the thick stuff) will drain right through to the other side.
It turns out that if you do that, there's nowhere for the air trapped in the hole to go, and you wind up with a big bubble at the bottom of the hole.
Cover the bottom with tape, but make a pinhole. When epoxy starts to drip out, cover the pinhole with a second piece of tape.
posts.key_insight: Iterative problem-solving in hands-on making often means discovering that each fix introduces a new constraint, and the best solutions address multiple problems elegantly.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recommends Woodweb's forums as an online marketplace for finding lumber deals, from reclaimed antique timbers to exotic woods like snakewood at steep discounts. He notes that savings on rare wood can effectively pay for new tools. He advises caution due to occasional scams, suggesting buyers ask for references and buy locally when possible to avoid shipping costs and verify quality in person.
key_points: Woodweb's forums offer a wide range of lumber from reclaimed timbers to exotic species at below-market prices
Snakewood was available at roughly 1/5 the typical market price, illustrating the potential savings
Buyers should exercise caution by checking references and preferring local sellers to inspect goods firsthand
money_quotes: if I buy four bf of snakewood, I've just saved enough to buy a new drill press. Brilliant!
It's important to be careful since there are a few ripoff stories floating around.
posts.key_insight: Online lumber forums can offer exotic woods at a fraction of retail price, but buyers should verify sellers and inspect goods before purchasing.

posts.summary: A very brief post where Shapiro shares a humorous complaint letter written to Continental Airlines about seat 29E. He acknowledges it's completely off-topic from his blog's usual woodworking focus but feels compelled to share it because it's so entertaining. The post is essentially a link-share with minimal commentary, reflecting the casual, personal blogging style common in 2005.
key_points: Shapiro shares a viral complaint letter about Continental Airlines seat 29E
He acknowledges the post is entirely off-topic from his blog's woodworking focus
The post reflects early blogging culture where personal blogs freely mixed topics
money_quotes: Sometimes something comes along that's just so odd, I have to share it with everyone, even though there isn't even the faintest connection between woodworking and the subject at hand.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted off-topic post sharing a humorous airline complaint letter, notable mainly as an artifact of early blogging culture.

posts.summary: A brief link-blog post from 2005 where Shapiro shares plans he found (via Boing Boing) for making baby wipes using a table saw. The post is essentially a one-liner celebrating the absurd ingenuity of the project. It reflects Shapiro's early blogging style and his long-standing appreciation for creative maker projects.
key_points: Highlights a DIY project that repurposes a table saw to cut baby wipes
Curated via Boing Boing, reflecting the early-2000s link-blogging culture
Demonstrates Shapiro's affinity for inventive, unconventional maker projects
money_quotes: What can I say… it's plans to make baby wipes with your table saw. How cool is that.
posts.key_insight: Even the most mundane household problems can inspire creative maker solutions with unexpected tools.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews the DowelMax doweling jig, a $230 woodworking tool he considers worth every penny despite its high price. He argues it eliminates the most painful phase of woodworking—alignment and glueup—by making it virtually impossible to drill inaccurate dowel holes. He demonstrates its precision by drilling 90 holes across six boards with perfect alignment, and its strength by showing dowel joints outlasting biscuit joints in destructive testing. The review is structured as a product endorsement grounded in hands-on shop experience.
key_points: The DowelMax is engineered to prevent user error through machined metal construction, registration marks, and symmetric design
Alignment and glueup is the most stressful phase of woodworking because it reveals all prior mistakes with minutes before glue sets
Shapiro drilled 300+ dowel holes with only one error (his fault), compared to 2 failures in just 8 holes using traditional dowel points
Destructive testing showed four dowels vastly outperforming a single biscuit joint, with wood splitting before the dowels failed
money_quotes: The people who invented it thought of every tomfool way you could bollox up your woodworking projects with their product, and engineered around it.
Alignment and glueup is the most heartrending, miserable, desolate time in a woodworker's life.
It's where all the mistakes of 1) become evident, with approximately four minutes left until the glue sets up.
I drilled over 300 dowel holes with the dowelmax on one project, and I had exactly 1 of them come out wrong.
The wood literally split in half the long way down the center; the dowels were fine.
You've probably got a couple of tools in your shop already that cost more than this one and will get used less.
posts.key_insight: A precision tool that eliminates the most error-prone phase of woodworking—alignment and glueup—is worth a premium price because it transforms an anxiety-ridden process into a reliable one.

posts.summary: Shapiro humorously critiques the publishing industry's trend of titling introductory books with insulting names (like 'for Dummies' or 'for Idiots'), contrasting them with older, straightforwardly titled reference books. He then reviews 'Basic Woodworking' by Sunset Books, a concise, unpretentious reference guide that covers essential woodworking topics in 1-5 pages each. He praises the book's efficiency and utility as a first-check reference for practical woodworking questions. The post is a lighthearted book recommendation wrapped in sharp commentary on marketing trends.
key_points: The publishing industry discovered that self-deprecating titles ('for Dummies', 'for Idiots') outsell straightforward introductory titles, pushing older earnest reference books out of print
Sunset Books' 'Basic Woodworking' is an exceptional reference that distills essential information into 1-5 pages per topic with clear illustrations
The best reference material is concise and practical rather than comprehensive — covering the 'most absolute, essential information' on each subject
Good introductory resources don't need to condescend to their audience to be accessible
money_quotes: That was before the ingenious discovery by the book industry that people prefered to buy introductory reading material from authors who considered the buyer to be 'a person of profound mental retardation having a mental age below three years.'
I'm currently raising capitol for a national chain of beauty salons to be called 'Gilding the Pig: Beautician Services for Disfigured Cheapskates with No Self-Esteem'.
It devotes 1-5 pages to each major thing you might consider doing with your dead tree, but each of those pages covers the most absolute, essential information on the subject.
Whenever I need a reminder about something or a piece of basic-but-new-to-me information, this is the first source I check.
I strongly suggest you go get one before some other reader with the cranial capacity of a toddler beats you to it.
posts.key_insight: The best reference books are concise and respect their readers' intelligence rather than padding content or condescending through clever branding.

posts.summary: This is an early blog post from Dan Shapiro's site featuring a guest review by Brian Todoroff of the Bosch RA1165 undertable router base. The review praises the product as an affordable alternative to expensive router lifts, allowing above-table height adjustment for under $60. The reviewer notes minor issues with locating the drill hole for the adjustment screw and occasional router tilting when the latch is released. Overall, the product is recommended as a great value for hobbyist woodworkers who don't need a full professional router lift.
key_points: The Bosch RA1165 solves the problem of having to reach under a router table for height adjustments, offering above-table wrench access at under $60
Before this product, woodworkers had to either spend hundreds on a router lift or awkwardly adjust their router from underneath the table
Minor design issues include the lack of a drill hole template and occasional router tilting when the latch is released
The product is ideal for hobbyist woodworkers but professionals would still benefit from a dedicated router lift
money_quotes: It seems that sticking a router under a hole in a table and using it from the top should be a simple bit of engineering, but until recently it eluded most router manufactures.
Every time I looked at the fixed base I just knew that it should be a simple modification to find a longer screw for the adjustment and then drill one hole.
My wife will happily tell you that I come up with far more projects than I could possible ever get done.
Luckily someone from Bosch was hiding in the rafters and took my grand idea back to the factory.
If I were running a full time shop routing day in and day out I'd still by a nice router lift, but for something that gets used once a week it's perfect.
posts.key_insight: A guest woodworking product review from Dan Shapiro's early blogging days, showcasing his longstanding connection to maker culture and hands-on engineering.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro reviews Bosch's new BLUECORE battery system for cordless tools, which offers 50% longer battery life and 30-minute charging through embedded heat rods and a microchip that communicates with the charger. He highlights the backward compatibility as the standout feature, noting that existing Bosch cordless tool owners can upgrade their batteries and chargers without replacing their entire tool collection. The post praises Bosch for not abandoning existing customers when introducing new technology.
key_points: Bosch's new battery packs use embedded heat rods to dissipate internal heat, extending battery lifetime by 50%
A microchip inside the battery communicates with the charger to optimize charging speed and report battery health
The new packs are backward compatible with existing Bosch cordless tools, and the new charger also improves charging speed for older battery packs
Shapiro praises Bosch's customer-friendly approach of not forcing a full tool ecosystem replacement to access new technology
money_quotes: Heat is the enemy of any battery pack.
Instead of squeezing you to buy a whole new set of tools to get these advantages you can just replace your existing packs as they wear out.
It's great to see a company that doesn't abandon their existing customers when a new technology comes down the pipe.
The new charger will even charge your old batteries faster, so even the old packs will have you back to work sooner.
I've gotta give Bosch kudos for offering a new system that upgrades both their old and new tools.
posts.key_insight: The best technology upgrades are ones that enhance what customers already own rather than forcing them to start over.

posts.summary: A brief housekeeping post where Dan Shapiro apologizes for not posting on his woodworking blog. He explains that his day job has been too busy and he just returned from a vacation in Vietnam. He promises to resume posting but warns updates may remain sporadic.
key_points: Shapiro's blog at this time was focused on woodworking, not technology
He signals that 'real life' work takes priority over his hobby blogging
This is a placeholder post with no substantive content
money_quotes: My non-woodworking real life has been too busy to spend much time in the shop.
posts.key_insight: A brief housekeeping note from Shapiro's early woodworking blog era, notable only as a snapshot of his pre-tech-CEO blogging life.

posts.summary: Shapiro recounts spending two weeks stripping paint from exposed beams in his living room and arriving at a hard-won conclusion: it's not worth it. Despite trying every method—liquid strippers, gel strippers, sanding, scraping, steel wool—he found that removing all five layers of paint without damaging the wood is essentially impossible. He humorously proposes a three-step alternative: throw out the painted wood, buy unfinished wood, and apply finish.
key_points: Five layers of paint each require different stripping methods, making complete removal impractical
A belt sander removes 98% of paint but the remaining 2% in grooves and pores is permanent
Sometimes starting fresh is more practical than trying to restore what exists
The end result of extensive stripping effort looks like 'reclaimed wood' at best
money_quotes: There is no way to remove *all* of the paint without also removing a serious portion of the wood.
So the only tool that indiscriminately removes paint is the belt sander.
The remaining 2% is there to stay.
After applying the new finish the wood looks as if it's a bit moldy.
If you want any other look then follow my simple 3 step process.
posts.key_insight: Sometimes the most practical solution to a restoration problem is to stop restoring and start replacing.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces his return to blogging after a months-long absence caused by secretly founding a new company, Ontela Inc., while maintaining his day job. He explains he couldn't discuss the venture publicly while still employed elsewhere. As of the post date, he's transitioning to work at Ontela full-time, which he hopes will free up small amounts of time for his woodworking hobby and blog.
key_points: Shapiro founded Ontela Inc. with a friend while still employed at his day job, consuming all his spare time
He kept the venture secret from blog readers because discussing a side company while employed is considered poor form
He's transitioning to full-time work at the new startup, expecting slightly more personal time with one job instead of two
money_quotes: Several months ago I founded a company with a friend.
I will be working my butt off at just one job instead of two, leaving dozens of minutes a week for woodworking and blogging and personal hygene.
posts.key_insight: An early glimpse of Dan Shapiro's entrepreneurial trajectory, as he leaves his day job to go full-time on his startup Ontela—a move that would become a recurring pattern in his career.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a link to Lars Liden's walk-in closet renovation project, which included building an outdoor hutch for a hot water heater. While noting it's more home improvement than woodworking, Shapiro highlights the detailed photo documentation. He adds a practical tip about using shop-made shelf pin hole jigs for projects with movable shelves, linking to two resources.
key_points: Lars Liden's closet renovation included building an outdoor hutch for the hot water heater
The project is well-documented with detailed photos on Flickr
Shop-made shelf pin hole jigs save significant effort when building movable shelves
money_quotes: It's more a home-improvement than a woodworking project, but it's still worthy of note because he has detailed pictures of the whole thing.
If you build a project with movable shelves, instead of painstakingly locating each shelf pin hole, use a shop-made shelf pin hole jig.
You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: A shop-made shelf pin hole jig is a simple tool that eliminates tedious measurement work when building movable shelves.

posts.summary: This is an extremely brief follow-up post where Shapiro shares a Flickr link he accidentally omitted from a previous post about a walk-in closet project. The post is essentially a correction/addendum with no substantive content beyond the link itself.
key_points: Follow-up post correcting an omission from a previous 'walk-in closet' post
Links to a Flickr photo set documenting what appears to be a closet project
posts.key_insight: This is a trivial correction post with no substantive content to analyze.

posts.summary: Shapiro reports from CES 2006 on a trend in speaker manufacturing: overseas companies discovered that applying beautiful wood veneer over acoustically-dead MDF produces gorgeous, great-sounding, affordable floorstanding speakers. However, some manufacturers took this too far by building speakers from solid wood, which is both more expensive and acoustically inferior to MDF since wood fibers resonate and distort sound. The post serves as both a CES trend report and a practical lesson for woodworkers.
key_points: Cheap overseas manufacturers discovered veneer over MDF as the sweet spot for attractive, affordable, great-sounding speakers
Solid wood speakers look premium but are acoustically worse than MDF because wood fibers resonate and distort sound
Manufacturers and consumers alike may not understand that the more expensive material produces worse results
The veneer-over-MDF approach is a case where the cheaper solution is genuinely superior on every axis
money_quotes: If veneer is cool, well, why not make the speakers out of solid wood?
Solid wood is actually worse for speakers than MDF, because the wood fibers resonate and mess up the acoustics.
So why replace cheap, good MDF with expensive, worse solid wood? Probably because consumers won't know any better (possibly because the manufacturers don't know any better!).
Veneer over MDF. Cheaper, more beautiful, and sounds better.
posts.key_insight: The most expensive material isn't always the best — veneer over MDF beats solid wood for speakers on cost, aesthetics, and acoustics, but manufacturers chase perceived premium over actual performance.

posts.summary: Shapiro follows up on his coffee table project that embeds slate tiles into wood. He describes the finishing process using Minwax Polycrylic, noting the care needed to avoid bubbles on rough slate surfaces. He recounts a mistake of waxing the tiles, which caused buildup in crevices that made them look dirty. After removing the wax manually, he concludes the tiles work great as drink coasters and recommends the technique for future projects.
key_points: Rough natural slate tiles need careful brushing with polyurethane to avoid bubbles in the finish
Waxing sealed slate tiles was a mistake — wax accumulated in crevices and couldn't be chemically removed without risking the surrounding shellac
Despite the finishing misstep, embedded slate tiles proved durable and functional as built-in coasters
Sometimes the simplest fix (elbow grease) is the only safe option when chemical solvents would cause collateral damage
money_quotes: Unfortunately, I then decided to get clever.
The wax built up in the crevices and was very noticable. It made the things look dirty.
Unfortunately, anything that would have dissolved the wax would also dissolve the nearby shellac finish and I didn't want to take any chances.
I used good old elbow grease to get rid of the wax.
They don't ring when you put drinks on them (their main purpose in life).
Try out some tiles on your next project. You'll thank me for it.
posts.key_insight: Embedded slate tiles in wood furniture work beautifully as built-in coasters, but resist the urge to over-finish them — getting clever with extra coatings can create more problems than it solves.

posts.summary: This brief post shares a reader's tip for removing glue from woodworking glueups. Jacques Richer suggests using a cabinet scraper as a superior alternative to chiseling or wiping. The technique involves waiting about 90 minutes for the glue to reach a semi-solid state, then scraping it off cleanly. It's presented as a practical update to an earlier article on the same topic.
key_points: A cabinet scraper offers a cleaner alternative to chiseling or wiping for glue removal
Timing matters: waiting ~90 minutes lets glue reach an ideal semi-solid state for scraping
The method avoids both the chipping of chisel removal and the mess of wiping
money_quotes: Scrape with a cabinet scraper – wiping it fairly often. It doesn't chip like using a chisel, and it doesn't take nearly as long or make nearly as much mess as wiping.
posts.key_insight: A cabinet scraper applied to semi-cured glue offers the cleanest and most efficient method for removing squeeze-out from woodworking glueups.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he's reviving his blog after leaving Photobucket to pursue a new business idea. The blog was previously dedicated to woodworking under the name 'Nothing Severed Yet,' but he signals a shift toward more varied content. The old woodworking posts remain archived. It's a brief, transitional post marking the start of a new chapter.
key_points: Shapiro left Photobucket and is 'gainfully unemployed,' signaling a pivot to entrepreneurship
The blog is transitioning from a niche woodworking blog to a broader platform
Old woodworking content is preserved, showing respect for past work while moving forward
money_quotes: What with being gainfully unemployed and all, I've decided to revisit my blog.
Those of you with long memories or penchants for manly crafts may remember this URL as the home of 'Nothing Severed Yet', my woodworking blog.
posts.key_insight: A lighthearted reintroduction post marking Shapiro's transition from corporate life to independent entrepreneurship, pivoting his blog from woodworking to broader topics.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro argues that startups should embrace polarization in recruiting rather than trying to appeal to everyone. Using the example of founding Ontela, he explains how they deliberately chose to locate on Seattle's Westside despite most tech talent being on the Eastside near Microsoft. This decision alienated over half their potential candidates but made the remaining minority enthusiastic about the opportunity. The strategy proved instrumental in making early hires, including a senior developer who joined largely because the shorter commute meant more time with his kids. The core lesson is that startups should leverage their uniqueness as a feature, not a bug.
key_points: Startups compete against big companies by targeting a passionate minority rather than appealing to the broadest possible talent pool
Ontela deliberately chose Seattle's Westside location despite most tech talent living on the Eastside near Microsoft, turning geography into a recruiting advantage
Polarizing decisions filter for candidates who are genuinely excited rather than merely willing, improving cultural fit and enthusiasm
The first hire was a senior developer who took a big pay cut primarily because the location meant seeing his kids more — a motivation big companies couldn't match
money_quotes: Those companies need to attract armies. You only need to hire one.
It's vastly better to polarize the market, alienate half your potential work force, and buck conventional wisdom than it is to play to the middle.
We don't have to hire most of the technology talent in Seattle. We just have to get a few awesome people.
There's a simple heuristic here that most companies use: the company's world headquarters is the piece of commercial real estate closest to the CEO's house.
Come on over! We have great healthcare benefits! And you can get your soul back if you need it later, promise!
He figured he'd get home sooner to see his kids if he didn't have to battle the bridge.
posts.key_insight: Startups win talent not by competing head-to-head with big companies for the broad market, but by making polarizing choices that deeply resonate with a passionate few.

posts.summary: Shapiro describes a family member's Gmail account being hacked and used to send a scam email requesting money. Unlike typical spam, this attack was more sophisticated: the hacker forwarded future emails to themselves, deleted contacts, and most tellingly, included 'B'H' — a Jewish religious abbreviation — in the scam message. Shapiro reasons that since Jews represent only 0.2% of the world population, including this marker suggests the hackers specifically targeted Jewish victims. He speculates the attackers may have screened for Jewish last names when selecting accounts to compromise.
key_points: The hack was unusually malicious — beyond sending spam, the attacker forwarded all future emails to themselves and deleted the victim's contacts
The scam email included 'B"H', a distinctly Jewish abbreviation, which would be nonsensical in a generic phishing attack but effective for building trust with Jewish recipients
The statistical improbability of including a Jewish marker in a random scam (Jews are 0.2% of world population) suggests deliberate ethnic targeting
Attackers may be selecting targets by screening for Jewish last names in public username lists before attempting to brute-force passwords
money_quotes: B'H? This is not something you see in your typical Nigerian con.
2% of the US population is Jewish. 0.2% of the world population is Jewish. This is a seriously weird thing to put in an email designed to part people with their money.

Unless, that is, you know in advance that those people are Jewish. Then it's a pretty cunning way to get the recipient to overlook the fact that your grammar is terrible, your story is hard to believe, and you can't really corroborate your story with any details.
Instead of shilling sketchy blue Bob Dole enhancers, this was an actual thief.
How do you decide which usernames to attack? Perhaps you start by screening for Jewish last names…
posts.key_insight: A Jewish religious marker in a phishing scam reveals that hackers may be ethnically profiling their targets to craft more convincing social engineering attacks.

posts.summary: Shapiro contrasts two VCs telling nationality jokes at conferences — one (Tom Huseby) who brought the room together with laughter and built relationships, and another who offended people and killed the mood. He uses this to argue that most business advice is bad not because it's wrong, but because it's context-dependent: what works brilliantly for one leader's personality and relationship style can backfire catastrophically for another. The core lesson is that leaders must honestly assess which behaviors suit their own style rather than blindly copying what works for someone else.
key_points: Most business advice fails not because it's inherently wrong, but because its effectiveness depends entirely on who's delivering it and their existing relationships
The same behavior (telling pointed jokes) can build deep connections or deeply offend depending on the leader's persona and social capital
Leaders need honest self-awareness about which tactics suit their style — knowing when you're 'the Chairman' versus 'the boor'
Context-dependent leadership means that advice from one type of leader will lead another to 'ruin and despair'
money_quotes: What you have to realize in business is that in some things you're the Chairman, and in some you're the boor.
Most of this advice is very, very bad.
It's bad not because it doesn't work well, but because it works well depending entirely on where you stand.
Neither one is right, and advice from one will lead the other to ruin and despair.
I know from experience that when I try to skip over the details with my team, I look and feel like Richard Nixon.
That's why I've taken to qualifying any advice I receive… is this good advice for a Chairman, or for a boor? And which one am I today?
posts.key_insight: Business advice is only as good as its fit with your own leadership style — the critical skill is honestly knowing which tactics suit your persona and which will make you look foolish.

posts.summary: Shapiro argues that most company culture statements are meaningless platitudes—generic aspirations like 'hire the best' and 'value teamwork' that every company claims. Real culture, he contends, is defined not by what makes a company great but by what makes it different. He identifies four components of authentic culture: polarizing decisions (refusing to compromise between opposing imperatives), excesses (taking good things to extremes), quirks (weird distinctions integral to the employee experience), and dysfunctions (the mirror-image costs of cultural choices). The framework implies that if your culture statement could belong to any company, it's not actually describing your culture.
key_points: Company culture is what makes you different, not what makes you great—generic aspirations shared by every company are not culture
Real culture emerges from polarizing decisions where a company refuses to compromise between two valid but opposing imperatives
Excesses—taking indubitably good things to their extreme—create distinctive culture in the margins where people ask 'isn't it a bit much?'
Dysfunctions are often the direct causal result of cultural strengths, like backstabbing arising from rewarding individual achievement
Quirks are the safe, harmless points of weird distinction that most companies still manage to screw up
money_quotes: Company Culture is a very serious matter, put together after much employee feedback and deliberation, and carefully designed to capture the key things that make your company great. It's also a load of well-mixed fertilizer.
The Rule of Company Culture: It's what makes your company different, not what makes it great.
If you find yourself saying 'we can do it all', that's great! And you're right, sort of. Your attempts at balance are admirable and may be successful, but do not constitute a corporate culture.
That only comes from taking a stand on one end of the see-saw.
Corporate culture is what occurs in the margins when someone asks – 'Well, I know that's good, but isn't it a bit much?'
A dysfunction is the mirror image of an excess – not enough of something that's important.
Backstabbing and rumor-mongering may be the price you pay for rewarding individual initiative and achievement.
posts.key_insight: Authentic company culture is defined by the polarizing trade-offs a company refuses to compromise on, not by the universally admirable values every company claims.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares his simple decision tree for deciding who to follow on Twitter.
