After his account grew unexpectedly when a talk about RC airplanes got picked up by Boing Boing, he developed a quick algorithm to manage incoming follows. The criteria prioritize signal over noise: he checks if someone is a friend or has a good follower-to-following ratio, then evaluates tweet frequency and interestingness.
key_points: Unexpected virality from a niche talk (RC airplanes on Boing Boing) can rapidly grow a social media following
A simple decision tree can systematize the social media follow-back decision
Quality signals matter: follower/following ratio, tweet frequency, and percentage of interesting tweets
Private exchanges between users don't count as 'interesting' content for follow decisions
money_quotes: If you're a friend, or your followers outnumber your follows, I'll look at your page.
If you don't tweet too often and at least a third of your tweets are interesting (and private exchanges with someone do not qualify), then I follow you.
posts.key_insight: A simple heuristic based on signal-to-noise ratio — checking follower ratios, tweet frequency, and content quality — is enough to manage a growing Twitter following.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro demystifies seemingly irrational VC behavior by tracing it back to fund economics. He explains that VCs invest other people's money from 10-year funds, earning 2% annual management fees plus 20% of profits, which creates incentive structures that drive counterintuitive decisions. The math of needing 3.6x returns just to match the S&P 500 explains why VCs chase 10x outcomes, push founders to take more capital than needed, block reasonable exits, and herd into the same investment sectors. Shapiro argues that once you understand how VCs get paid, their behavior becomes entirely predictable rather than insane.
key_points: VCs take their biggest risk when closing a fund; everything after is risk mitigation with other people's money
The 2-20 fee structure (2% annual management fee, 20% carry) incentivizes raising larger funds even though smaller funds empirically outperform
A 10x return target isn't greed — after accounting for failures, management fees, and compounding, it's what's needed just to match the S&P 500
VCs block seemingly great exits because returns are calculated against reserved capital, not just invested capital
Herd investing reduces thesis risk and provides political cover with LPs when investments fail
money_quotes: It's crazy but true: if you know how a VC gets paid, you can pretty much read their mind.
Modern venture capital is one shining moment of derring-do taking followed by 10 years of risk mitigation. It's sort of like breaking a mirror.
Folks who put their own dimes on the line are known as angel investors, not VCs. Venture Capital is strictly a game of other people's property.

When you hear that VCs aim for a 10x return, it's not greed – it's because if a third of their companies fail and a third just barely get them their money back, a 10x return on the winners puts them in the same place as the S&P 500!
If you took the leap to invest in caffeinated soap all by yourself, good luck explaining that to your LPs.
There's simply no way a person can surprise you if you know how to read their mind.
Golf games may be in danger of abbreviation.
posts.key_insight: VC behavior that appears irrational to entrepreneurs is entirely predictable once you understand fund economics: the 2-20 fee structure, 10-year fund timelines, and the math required to beat the S&P 500.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro provides a practical guide to quickly evaluating a patent by skipping most of its content. He argues that titles, drawings, abstracts, and specifications are largely misleading or incomprehensible, and that the only legally enforceable part worth reading is the independent claims. The post walks through each section of a patent, explaining why it can be safely ignored, with humor and real examples. He notes that even the patent office itself reads patents this way—claims first, then drawings, then specification only if needed.
key_points: Patent titles are deliberately general and describe the field being improved, not the actual invention
Abstracts can be deliberately misleading and have no bearing on enforceability—only the claims matter legally
Independent claims are the only part with actual legal enforceability and should be the sole focus of a quick read
Dependent claims (which refine parent claims) can be skipped since infringing a narrower claim means infringing the broader parent
The patent office itself reads patents the same way: independent claims first, then drawings, then specification only for clarification
money_quotes: Patent drawings are mostly similar to high school notebook doodles except that they cost $5,000.
Patent abstracts are at best meandering and hard to read, and at worst deliberately misleading.
The claims are the only part of the patent that have any actual legal enforceability.
It takes an average of nearly four years for a patent to be examined, so by the time anything interesting happens you've forgotten all about it.
The detailed description will send you catapulting in to catalepsy, while simultaneously not separating what's actually novel and invented from the stuff that everyone knows already.
Here is the the guy who patented the Tool.
posts.key_insight: The only legally enforceable part of a patent is its independent claims, so skip everything else when you need a quick read.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro discovered a job candidate's secret Twitter account simply by using tools like Gist and Rapportive that link email addresses to social media profiles. While reviewing an otherwise outstanding student resume, he found the candidate's hidden Twitter account through an email-based API lookup — not through googling. The post serves as a cautionary tale about online privacy: your email address is a skeleton key to your digital identity. Shapiro warns that anyone who thinks their anonymous social media accounts are truly separate from their professional identity is mistaken if they used the same email address.
key_points: Tools like Gist and Rapportive can link email addresses to social media accounts, making 'secret' profiles easily discoverable
Resumes should include an online footprint — GitHub for developers, clever side projects for MBAs, social media presence for marketers
Email addresses function as universal identifiers across platforms, connecting identities people assume are separate
Privacy through obscurity fails when APIs and aggregation tools can trivially connect the dots
money_quotes: If you're building a secret Twitter account, be sure to use a secret email address for it too, OK?
This category of tool is, quite simply, synapse augmentation. If you have it, you're a little sharper, a little more able to connect the dots.
Did you know you can look up someone's user account on Facebook, Linkedin, Twitter, and elsewhere by email address?
I didn't google this guy (and if I did I wouldn't have found him, since his name wasn't anywhere on his twitter account).

For a developer, point me to your github or stackoverflow account. For an MBA, let's see a clever website you banged out that's delivering a couple hundred bucks a month of adwords to your bank account.
posts.key_insight: Your email address is the thread that ties all your online identities together, and readily available tools can pull that thread with a single click.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro briefly announces a guest post he wrote about the role of a startup CTO, hosted on Scott Porad's blog. He also casually reveals he has started a new company called Sparkbuy, which is in stealth mode but launching soon. The post is essentially a short teaser directing readers elsewhere for the substantive content, while dropping a company announcement almost as an afterthought.
key_points: Shapiro wrote a guest post exploring the true meaning of the startup CTO role
He announces Sparkbuy, a new company in stealth mode about to launch
The casual, almost offhand announcement of a new company reflects startup culture's bias toward action over ceremony
money_quotes: I started a new company, Sparkbuy, which I think I forgot to mention.
posts.key_insight: The post is a brief redirect to a guest article on the startup CTO role, notable mainly for its offhand announcement of a new stealth-mode company.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro breaks down the real value of startup stock options, arguing that most employees dramatically overestimate what their equity is worth. He explains how liquidation preferences, participation rights, and dilution from funding rounds significantly erode the value of common shares compared to preferred investor shares. The post provides a practical heuristic formula for estimating option value that accounts for company failure risk, exit expectations, capital raised, and investor-friendly terms. Through a worked example, he shows that 0.1% of a company expecting a $250-500mm exit might only be worth $35k after adjustments. His motivation is transparency: he believes informed candidates will recognize genuinely good equity offers.
key_points: Startup employees typically take a ~33% pay cut, and the equity meant to compensate them is usually worth far less than presented
Preferred stock structures (liquidation preferences, participation rights, protective provisions) dramatically devalue common shares held by employees
The IRS rule of thumb valued common stock at one-tenth of preferred stock, meaning the headline 'company valuation' is misleading for option holders
Shapiro provides a concrete heuristic formula: take the low exit estimate halved, subtract total capital raised (doubled) times a penalty factor derived from preference terms and board composition
90% of startups fail, so if you don't deeply believe in the company, assume your options are worthless
money_quotes: A great startup with a dozen or so people will typically pay its employees about a third less than a big company.
The existence of preferred, 'investor' shares significantly devalues regular, common shares.
I don't want them misled with a number that's too big, or there's hell to pay when they realize they've been had.
90% of new companies fail, so if you don't, then you should assume your stock is worthless, and stop here. Also, you should go work elsewhere, because life's too short.
He's so good at closing candidates that before he does it, he takes on voluntary challenges. It's like he's working on an xbox achievement.
At most startups, the equity's worth a lot less than you might imagine.
I love startups. I love it when people get rich from startups. I want you to join a startup, and I want it to shower you with riches beyond your wildest dreams.
posts.key_insight: Startup stock options are worth far less than headline valuations suggest once you account for liquidation preferences, dilution, and the base rate of startup failure, and employees should demand transparency to evaluate equity offers realistically.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces the public launch of Sparkbuy, a product he had been working on for six months. The post is a brief announcement directing readers to the site and press coverage from the New York Times, Wall Street Journal's All Things D, and GeekWire. Sparkbuy was positioned as a 'Kayak for gadgets' — a comparison search engine for consumer electronics. The post is essentially a launch day placeholder with links rather than a substantive discussion.
key_points: Sparkbuy launched publicly as a comparison search engine for consumer electronics like laptops and TVs
The product was described in press as 'a Kayak for gadgets', positioning it as an aggregator in the electronics shopping space
The launch garnered coverage from major outlets including the New York Times and Wall Street Journal
money_quotes: Check it out at www.sparkbuy.com and see what I've been working on for the past 6 months or so.
posts.key_insight: Shapiro launched Sparkbuy as a comparison search engine for consumer electronics, attracting major press coverage by positioning it as 'a Kayak for gadgets.'

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro argues that equal (50/50) cofounder equity splits are always wrong because they represent avoidance of hard conversations rather than a thoughtful business decision. He presents a formula for calculating equity splits based on factors like who initiated the startup, who brought the idea, prior execution, CEO role, full-time commitment, reputation, and cash investment. He contends that unequal splits force cofounders to confront difficult questions about relative contributions early, which is healthy for the company. He directly rebuts Joel Spolsky's advocacy for equal splits, arguing it confuses 'easy' with 'fair' and doesn't reflect market realities. The core message is that cofounders who can't resolve equity disagreements should discover that incompatibility before taking on investors and employees.
key_points: Founders are defined by working without salary at a stage when the company can't pay anyone — if you draw a salary on day one, you're not a founder
The equity formula accounts for initiation (5%), idea (5%), prior execution (5-25%), CEO role (5%), full-time commitment (200%), reputation (50-500%+), and cash investment
A 50/50 split signals conflict avoidance, not agreement — cofounders need to resolve hard questions before commitments to investors and employees
Reputation and investability can be the single largest factor, with 'super-preneurs' whose involvement guarantees funding commanding outsized equity
Joel Spolsky's equal-split approach confuses 'easy' with 'fair' and over-allocates to salaried employees at the expense of founders who worked without pay
money_quotes: The question of equity brings out the most fundamental differences, perceptions, and values in an aspiring startup. In fact the equity question, more than any other, may strangle a young company before it can even get started. And that's a damn good thing.
The rule is this: if you're working for a company that's so young it can't pay you, you're a founder. If you are drawing a salary on your first day at work, you're not.
It's a lie to say that ideas are worthless and execution is everything… but it's not too far from the truth, either.
If you don't trust your CEO with the majority of shares, you're founding the company with the wrong person.
50/50 isn't a business decision, it's a compromise.
The most common cause of startup death is founders who can't resolve their differences. Nobody hears about it; they just pack up and go home before the company ever had a chance.
If you find that the only way you can get a decision made is by compromising – then you need to stop now, before the price of failure climbs higher.
It confuses 'easy' with 'fair'. If you've had two successful exits already while I'm doing my first startup, it's easy but not fair to split it 50/50.
posts.key_insight: A 50/50 equity split is conflict avoidance masquerading as fairness — cofounders must confront uncomfortable differences in contribution and risk early, because the inability to resolve those questions is itself the strongest signal the partnership will fail.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro writes an open letter to TechCrunch founder Mike Arrington, asking him to stop investing in startups because it creates conflicts of interest with his journalism. Shapiro argues that economic incentives shape behavior in subtle, unmeasurable ways, and that Arrington's dual role as investor and reporter will inevitably compromise TechCrunch's editorial independence. He frames journalistic independence as a product feature that shouldn't be sacrificed. The letter is notably polite and brief, positioning itself as customer feedback rather than a moral indictment.
key_points: Economic incentives shape behaviors in subtle and unmeasurable ways, even when people try to be fair
Conflicts of interest in journalism don't manifest as obvious bias — they create subtle distortions like over-covering or under-covering portfolio companies
Journalistic independence should be treated as a product feature, not just an ethical principle
The letter frames the argument as pragmatic product feedback rather than moral criticism
money_quotes: I'm a big believer that economic incentives shape behaviors in subtle and unmeasurable ways.
I think your decision to make investments in startups is going to make TechCrunch a worse product.
Are you going to be a little more likely to ignore competitors to your companies? Cover them, even when they're not newsworthy, to show you're not biased?
Feel compelled to pull or throw punches, either to support or prove you're independent of the companies you deal with?
Journalistic independence is a great feature. Please don't cut it.
I'm not making demands or threatening to leave. Just making a request, from one product guy to another.
posts.key_insight: Journalistic independence is best understood not as an abstract ethical ideal but as a core product feature — and conflicts of interest degrade it in ways that are subtle but inevitable.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro explains the multi-faceted reasoning behind selling his startup Sparkbuy to Google just six months after incorporation. The deal provided life-changing personal wealth, great returns for angel investors on a time-adjusted basis, and placed the team at their top-choice acquirer. Shapiro was drawn to pursuing Sparkbuy's product vision at Google scale while also welcoming the stability of a steady paycheck after eight years of startup life. He candidly admits that while raising a large round was the best strategic move for the company, he personally didn't want to do it again. The sale freed him to eventually swing for the fences on future ventures without financial anxiety.
key_points: Angel investors evaluate exits on time-adjusted returns, making a quick six-month exit with strong returns potentially better than a longer play
The financial windfall solved three lifelong goals: children's college funds, retirement savings, and a cushion to never work a job he doesn't love
Shapiro recognized a misalignment between what was strategically best for the company (raising a large round) and what he personally wanted to do
Most successful startups end in acquisition, so choosing your acquirer wisely matters more than resisting acquisition entirely
The biggest unsolved risk—profitably acquiring customers—was still ahead, meaning the company's value had significant uncertainty
money_quotes: Most startups fail – but of those that are successful, most are acquired.
If I was going to go to work for The Man, I was more excited about The Man being Google than anyone else.
It's just hard to find reliable, accurate, unbiased, quantitative information about products.
Health insurance reform is key to promoting entrepreneurialism and small businesses in this country!
What I thought was best for the company wasn't what I, selfishly, wanted to do.
When I start my next company, I can swing for the fences.
The biggest challenge for any consumer startup is how to profitably acquire customers.
I've been called a sellout, and people have told me that I epitomize what's wrong with entrepreneurs outside the valley. It's all cool.
posts.key_insight: Selling a startup can be the right decision when the founder's personal goals diverge from the company's optimal strategic path, especially when the deal simultaneously rewards investors, provides life-changing financial security, and frees the founder to take bigger risks next time.

posts.summary: Shapiro introduces a framework for categorizing startups into two extreme types: 'Twitter Startups' where the solution is obvious but nobody understands why anyone would want it, and 'Hoverboard Startups' where the problem is obvious but the solution seems impossibly hard. He argues that founders who don't recognize which category they fall into will botch their investor pitches. Twitter Startups should lead with traction and customer validation, while Hoverboard Startups need to demonstrate their technical breakthrough immediately. Most startups fall somewhere in between, but recognizing your type determines how you should structure your pitch.
key_points: A 'Hoverboard Startup' has a screamingly obvious problem but a prohibitively hard solution — investors only care whether you've actually cracked the technical challenge
A 'Twitter Startup' has a straightforward solution but no apparent problem — investors only care whether real users actually want it

Misidentifying your startup type leads to pitching the wrong things: Hoverboard founders waste time on market size when investors want a demo, Twitter founders waste time on tech when investors want traction
Real-world examples ground the framework: AirBnB and Instagram were Twitter Startups, Mint and EnerG2 were Hoverboard Startups, and failed examples like CueCat and Steorn show both types can fail
money_quotes: A Hoverboard Startup is one where the problem is screamingly obvious, but the solution is prohibitively hard.
A Twitter Startup is one where the solution is straightforward, but it doesn't appear to actually solve any problem.
You seriously don't care about his viral strategy or exit strategy, you just want to know if he's invented the secret of levitation.
If you're one of these and don't know it, you're going to screw up your pitch.

We also get rid of school and work affiliations, skip all the new stuff like location and checkins, and pretty much kill all the privacy settings so everything is just public by default. Won't that be AMAZING?
If you're a Hoverboard? Well, you'd better be levitating by the third slide, or your investor is going to be out of there.
posts.key_insight: Founders must recognize whether their startup's core risk is demand (Twitter) or feasibility (Hoverboard), then structure their entire pitch around answering that single question.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his frustrating experience trying to get mobile data working on his Android phone while traveling in the UK. What should be a simple process of buying a prepaid SIM card turns into a 33-step odyssey involving carrier unlock codes, incompatible credit cards, unhelpful phone trees, fraud alerts from American Express, and repeated reboots. The post uses an escalating numbered list format to comedic effect, with each step revealing a new absurd obstacle. The conclusion—raiding the minibar—is the only rational response to a system designed to defeat its users.
key_points: International mobile data setup involves a cascade of hidden requirements that no single party discloses upfront
Systems designed for local users create compounding friction for international travelers at every step—from postal codes to credit card validation
The Catch-22 of needing a working balance to call tech support about your non-working balance perfectly illustrates bad service design
Even proactive measures like calling your credit card company in advance fail to prevent fraud alerts from disrupting the process
money_quotes: Pick up a SIM card from the local mobile store, wireless stand, fast food chain, gas station, vending machine, cabbage shop, or hobo.
Enter SIM unlock code. Carefully. 5 or so wrong answers and your phone is a useless brick.
Other alternatives: travel without data, fly home.

Provide a witty retort when your clever operator with a perfect Pakistani British accent observes that your post code is the same as that of Buckingham Palace. Stand firm in your assertion. Share a chuckle.
Your internet stops working when you have 0-balance, a fact no one mentioned, and you couldn't find out because if you have 0-balance you can't call tech support.

Get urgent email from amex saying that they declined your ten pound charge just in case you're not you, despite the fact that you called them yesterday and told them you'd be getting a phone in England so please turn off the fraud alerts.
Raid minibar. Go to bed. (this step has not yet been tested)
posts.key_insight: The gap between how simple a process should be and how broken it actually is reveals systemic failures in designing for real users rather than ideal ones.

posts.summary: Shapiro discusses the awkward situation of being asked for a business plan by investors, referencing Brad Feld and Jason Mendelson's book 'Venture Deals' which acknowledges the document is largely obsolete but warns founders to be prepared for the request. Drawing from his experience pitching 70+ VCs and 100+ angels across multiple funding rounds at Ontela, Shapiro notes that no VC ever asked for a business plan, but several angels did during the seed round. His solution: send the same materials you send everyone else—an executive summary and slide deck—without apology or acknowledgment. He notes that investors who ask for business plans are probably not the right fit for high-growth tech startups, as none of the angels who requested one ultimately invested.
key_points: Business plans are effectively obsolete in venture financing—top VCs haven't read one in decades, preferring demos and live interactions
When asked for a business plan, simply send your standard pitch materials without apology or mention of the business plan request
The business plan request came only from angels during seed rounds, never from VCs during Series A or B
Investors who request business plans are a signal of misalignment—none of the ones who asked ultimately invested
Founders often feel guilty or scared when asked for something they skipped, but the 'foolproof solution' is to not treat it as a gap
money_quotes: Whenever an investor asks you for your business plan, send them the same damn packet you send to everyone else.
Don't apologize and don't mention the business plan.
We did this at least a dozen times and had precisely zero complaints.
Investors who want business plans are probably not your target market, if you're founding a high growth technology startup.
We had lots of great followup conversations with the angels who wanted them, but ultimately none of them turned in to investors.
There was a lot of hemming and hawing before we figured out a foolproof solution.
posts.key_insight: When investors ask for a business plan, don't panic or apologize—just send your standard pitch materials, and recognize that the request itself is a signal of investor-founder misalignment.

posts.summary: Shapiro shares a practical strategy for handling VCs who charge excessive travel expenses to portfolio companies. Rather than confronting the board member directly, he created a company-wide travel expense policy and had the board formally approve it. This allowed the issue to be resolved bureaucratically without personal confrontation or bruised egos. He acknowledges the approach was somewhat passive-aggressive but effective, and adds nuance by noting VCs are generally good people who may not even be aware their admins are booking premium travel.
key_points: Direct confrontation with a VC over expenses can be awkward and damaging to the relationship, so systemic solutions are preferable
Creating a formal company-wide expense policy and having the board approve it depersonalizes the issue and makes enforcement routine
VCs may not even know about excessive charges if their admins handle booking and upgrades autonomously
The approach trades directness for diplomacy, solving the problem without assigning blame
money_quotes: Since this will apply to everyone submitting expenses, including me and any other board members who are traveling, I wanted to have it officially adopted by a board action.
So when a first class ticket came in, we priced how much the coach ticket would have cost, and that was the amount of reimbursement sent.
The whole thing was quietly solved with no bruised egos.
Some might say passive-aggressively.
Many see their job as taking care of their boss, not their boss's portfolio, and may book first while the VC thinks they just got a comp upgrade.
The ones I know are almost entirely great folks, which is saying a lot for a job that consists of saying no 99.9% of the time.
posts.key_insight: When a board member's behavior needs correcting, creating a formal policy that applies to everyone is more effective and less damaging than direct confrontation.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro argues that asking contacts for generic introductions to investors is counterproductive and marks you as inexperienced. He outlines three reasons: not all investors are right for your company, it's lazy to offload the matchmaking work to your contact, and unprepared introductions will be lukewarm and ineffective. Instead, he prescribes a five-step process: research specific investors beforehand, pitch your contact first to build their enthusiasm, ask for specific named introductions, then ask for additional suggestions, and follow up with forwardable emails. The core message is that fundraising requires the same rigor and preparation as building the product itself.
key_points: Investors specialize by stage, sector, check size, and style — treating them as interchangeable shows inexperience
Asking for generic intros offloads research work onto your contact that only you are equipped to do well
A lukewarm introduction from someone who hasn't been sold on your company is worse than no introduction at all
The right approach is to research specific investors, pitch your contact first, then ask for named introductions with forwardable materials
Teams that invest the most effort in fundraising process tend to get the best results
money_quotes: There's no way to screw it up faster than going around asking, 'Hey, could you introduce me to some investors?'
Asking for introductions to 'investors' marks you as someone who doesn't really know what they're doing.
Somebody has to be the matchmaker. The right person to do that is you, since you know your company best.
Sound like a lot of work? It is. That's why you should do it, not your friend.
If the nicest thing they can say about your company is that it 'sounds interesting', the intro isn't going to go anywhere.
A great investor intro is about conveying enthusiasm. So you need to sell them, then give them simple tools to sell the investor.
Don't teach them; tease them. Show them just enough to get them to want more.
The teams that invest the most in fundraising seem to have the best results.
posts.key_insight: Fundraising introductions are a sales process — you must do the research, pitch your contact first, and ask for specific named introductions rather than outsourcing the matchmaking to someone else.

posts.summary: Shapiro notices that Sprouter's website looks subtly amateurish and traces the problem to JPEG compression artifacts in their logo. He compares logo schmutz to a kid with raspberry juice on his face — endearing on a child, unprofessional on a business. The fix is simple: use PNG format for logos instead of JPEG, since PNG offers lossless compression that keeps edges clean. He notes that while most visitors won't consciously notice compression artifacts, the subtle cues still signal whether a company looks professional or homegrown. The post is a quick, practical reminder that small design details shape perception.
key_points: JPEG compression artifacts on logos create a subtle but real impression of amateurism
PNG format offers lossless compression and should be the default for logos
Visitors may not consciously notice artifacts, but the cumulative effect undermines credibility
Small, cheap-to-fix details are what separate 'real company' perception from 'toddler company' perception
money_quotes: A kid's adorable with raspberry juice on his face, but your business should put its best foot forward.
JPEG artefacts are not adorable, and while most visitors won't notice them consciously, it just mucks up your image.
It's funny the subtle cues that signal 'Real, grown up company' versus 'Raspberry-stained toddler company'.
Use your logo as a PNG, which offers lossless compression, and not a JPEG (or, heaven forfend, a GIF).
So wipe the schmutz off your logo. Your bubbe will be so pleased.
posts.key_insight: Tiny design oversights like JPEG artifacts on your logo silently signal amateurism, and fixing them is one of the cheapest ways to look like a real company.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts how he registered as a bone marrow donor in college in 1995, then lost track of his registration as his contact information changed over 16 years. After reading about Amit Gupta's leukemia diagnosis on Hacker News, he tried to update his donor registry info but gave up. Months later, while sorting through old mail from his acquired startup Sparkbuy, he discovered the marrow registry had found a match for him — but the letter had sat unread for five months. He called immediately but was told the patient likely found another donor or was 'no longer eligible,' a grim euphemism. The post serves as a urgent plea to join the marrow registry and keep your contact information current.
key_points: Half of people who need marrow transplants cannot locate a matching donor, despite modern donation being minimally invasive
South Asians and other minorities are dramatically underrepresented in the marrow donor registry, making matches harder to find
Outdated contact information in donor registries can have life-or-death consequences, as Shapiro's own experience painfully illustrates
The gap between intending to do good and actually following through — updating an address, opening mail — can be the difference between saving a life and not
money_quotes: I decided that it was a rare occurrence in this world that you actually get to save the life of a stranger, and if skeleton-draining was the price of that, then so be it.
Half of the people who need marrow transplants can't locate a donor.

They said that they'd contact the patient's doctor right away. But they told me the odds were good that, since 5 months had passed, 'they found another match, or that the patient… is no longer eligible.'

Did you know that, when the marrow donation center finds a match, they try desperately to reach the potential donor? Even if that person has moved from their dorm room long ago, even if their contact information has changed, even if they're in a different state, even if 16 years have passed?
I fussed around with the website to update my contact data. I couldn't figure out how to find my old record, so I made a mental note to try and call them some time, and gave up.
posts.key_insight: The bureaucratic friction of outdated contact information and ordinary procrastination can quietly nullify a life-saving commitment made years earlier.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro explains why most businesses—using a taxi company as a concrete example—are fundamentally unsuitable for venture capital funding. Prompted by an email from a Boston taxi operator seeking VC, he lays out six reasons: VCs want explosive growth over profitability, demand outrageous margins, need 10x returns, prefer investing in elite insiders, require months of full-time fundraising with low success rates, and impose board oversight that limits founder autonomy. He then outlines alternatives including angel investors, bank loans, revenue-based financing, and bootstrapping, while acknowledging that VC is genuinely powerful for the rare company that fits the model.
key_points: VCs actively discourage profitability because they want companies to reinvest every dollar into growth, and profitable companies signal they won't grow faster
Venture capital only works for businesses with enormous margins (80-90%) and near-zero marginal costs—people-powered businesses that scale linearly are fundamentally incompatible
Doubling investor money is actually a failure in VC economics; firms need 10x returns, which means targeting hundreds of millions to billions in company value
Fundraising is a 6-12 month near-full-time job with well under 1% success rates, making it a terrible time investment for most founders
Alternatives like angel investment, bank loans, revenue-based lending, and bootstrapping serve the vast majority of businesses far better than VC
money_quotes: VCs don't actually like their companies to be profitable. Someday, sure, but not on their watch.
When the day comes that a VC-backed business generates cash faster than it can effectively spend it? They sell the company, or IPO, or replace the CEO with someone who can spend faster.
VCs don't like reasonable margins. They are exclusively interested in outrageous margins. Ludicrous margins. We're talking about sneering at 50%, and hoping for 80%, 90%, crazy astronomy stuff.
If adding revenue means adding bodies, they don't like it.
VCs really don't want to double their money. Strange though it sounds, their economics make that look like a failure.
The only thing more frustrating and time consuming than raising a VC round is failing to raise a VC round.
If you like your freedom, then bringing on VC may feel somewhat familiar – in an 'I have a boss again' way you probably won't enjoy.
posts.key_insight: Venture capital is designed for a tiny fraction of businesses that can achieve explosive growth with outrageous margins, and most founders would be better served by alternatives like bootstrapping, angel investment, or revenue-based lending.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts crashing a 500 Startups demo day after a chance encounter with a former cofounder on a flight to San Jose. He provides brief reviews of standout companies from the batch of 32 startups, spanning consumer apps, content personalization, fitness gamification, and children's media. The post captures the energy and eclecticism of early-stage startup culture circa 2012, with Shapiro offering candid takes on each company's strengths and weaknesses. He highlights the global reach of 500 Startups, noting companies from Japan to Brazil in the portfolio.
key_points: 500 Startups had $29MM under management with over 250 investments, half outside Silicon Valley, showcasing early global accelerator reach
Shapiro evaluates startups with a practical lens—praising TinyReview's fun factor while questioning its viability as an actual review platform
Several companies exemplified 2012-era trends: content personalization (Spinnakr), gamification (Fitocracy), and user-generated video (Switchcam)
The demo day snapshot captures a pre-AI startup ecosystem focused on social, mobile, and consumer internet plays
money_quotes: As with all good things, it came about via a stupid coincidence.
You go somewhere, take a picture, and put 3 extremely short lines of text on it – two or three words per line, tops.
It looks like a great creative palette but not like a great reviews site.
Not so in love with knowingly hosting large quantities of pirated content, and automatically categorizing it for easy takedown notices.
They're doing online and mobile storybooks for kids – great growth, and spectacular stickiness with an average session length of 29 minutes.
GoVoluntr has the distinction of being the only startup pitched by an actual Got Milk ad model.
posts.key_insight: A strong demo day showcases not just ideas but founder stories and creative positioning, with the best pitches combining clear market insight with memorable presentation.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro calls out casual sexism in the startup and tech community, recounting an incident where a well-credentialed female panel moderator was introduced by referencing her appearance and marital status rather than her professional accomplishments. He cites additional examples of objectifying behavior he's witnessed, including a Techstars team using gratuitous photos of women in a pitch deck and a founder commenting on a receptionist's appearance. He argues that tolerating this behavior will drive away women tech entrepreneurs and urges people to speak up when they see it, while admitting he failed to do so himself in the moment.
key_points: A prominent female tech figure was introduced at a panel by her appearance and marital status rather than her extensive professional credentials
Sexist behavior in startups is widespread and takes many forms, from pitch deck imagery to workplace comments
Excuses like age or cultural background don't justify objectifying behavior in professional settings
Bystanders have a responsibility to call out sexism when they see it, not just critique it after the fact
Shapiro holds himself accountable for not speaking up in the moment, modeling the self-reflection he's asking of others
money_quotes: If we keep this bullshit up, we're going to crap all over another generation of women tech entrepreneurs.
And if you see someone doing this, call them on it. I didn't… that was my nervous laughter in the background of the recording.
Everyone has a reason. One person was older. One person was from another country. It just doesn't matter.
These would all be appropriate topics to use when introducing someone, man or woman.
Better late than never.
posts.key_insight: The tech and startup community must actively confront casual sexism rather than nervously laughing it off, because tolerating it drives women out of the industry.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro profiles Lightsail Energy, a startup founded by 25-year-old middle school dropout Danielle Fong that builds regenerative braking systems for the power grid. The post playfully builds suspense through a series of reveals: the young founder, the compelling energy storage technology, and their Ghostbusters-style firehouse headquarters. Shapiro draws an analogy between how a Prius recaptures braking energy and how Lightsail helps power plants and renewable energy sources store and release electricity on demand. The post is structured as an escalating series of 'that's not the amazing part' beats, culminating in the reveal that they moved from a Ghostbusters firehouse to a chocolate factory.
key_points: Lightsail Energy builds grid-scale energy storage, analogous to regenerative braking in hybrid cars, solving the problem of matching variable energy production to consumer demand
The startup was founded by Danielle Fong, a 25-year-old who dropped out of middle school and serves as chief scientist
Shapiro uses the company's whimsical headquarters choices (a Ghostbusters-style firehouse, then a chocolate factory) as a proxy for startup culture and audacity
The post highlights the appeal of startups that combine serious deep-tech with a sense of fun and personality
money_quotes: Lightsail makes regenerative brakes for the power grid.

You know how your Prius takes the power from the brakes when you don't need it, and dumps it back in to the engine when you do need it? Lightsail does the same thing for energy plants that can't scale their production quickly with the needs of consumers.
Danielle informed me after publication of this article that they have vacated the Ghostbusters firehouse and moved to a chocolate factory.
I rest my case.
posts.key_insight: The best startups combine serious technology with irrepressible personality, and Lightsail Energy — with its young prodigy founder, grid-scale energy storage tech, and Ghostbusters firehouse headquarters — embodies that combination.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro explains that stock vesting is fundamentally a tax hack layered on top of another tax hack, but one that founders desperately need. Using a hypothetical where a departing co-founder walks away with equal equity despite contributing nothing, he illustrates why vesting protects committed founders. He argues that founder vesting is actually founder-friendly—the opposite of common perception—and should be paired with acceleration clauses to prevent losing value during acquisitions. The post provides detailed practical advice on vesting schedules, cliff periods, acceleration triggers, and 83(b) elections.
key_points: Stock grants are a crude hack to avoid taxes, and vesting is a hack on top of that hack—but a necessary one to align equity with actual contribution over time
Founder vesting is founder-friendly, not investor-friendly, because it protects committed founders from co-founders who leave early with unearned equity
Acceleration clauses (single-trigger and double-trigger) are essential complements to vesting that prevent founders from losing value during acquisitions
Founders should implement vesting before raising money to signal competence and reduce negotiation complexity
Filing 83(b) elections immediately upon incorporation is critical—missing the 30-day window is one of the most common and expensive founder mistakes
money_quotes: Stock grants are, at their heart, a crude hack to avoid taxes. Vesting is a hack to the hack – and one almost every founder needs.
It would be like if you paid someone four years worth of salary in a lump sum on their hire date.
He gets a call one afternoon that, for sitting on his duff for the past five years, he's worth a cool $40mm, same as Jill.
Founder vesting is founder friendly, the exact opposite of what most people think. You want it. Don't fight it.
Put founder vesting in place before you start to raise money. Investors will be impressed that you know what you're doing.
Stock is best used for people who are totally committed, so the stock accumulation shouldn't kick in until the commitment does.
If you're not sure if this applies to you, ask your lawyer. If they're not sure, fire them and hire someone else.
posts.key_insight: Founder vesting, despite its reputation as an investor demand, is actually a founder-protective mechanism that should be adopted proactively at incorporation and paired with acceleration clauses.

posts.summary: Shapiro describes his experiment creating a mailing list where each subscriber can only post once per year. Frustrated by the universal decay of mailing list quality — monopolized conversations, flame wars, and topic drift — he explored game mechanics for self-regulation. After nearly reinventing Hacker News with a complex voting system, he stripped the idea down to its most extreme element: a one-year enforced cool-down after every post. The result, 'The Best Thing This Year,' attracted nearly 2,000 subscribers but very few submissions, proving that artificial scarcity of voice dramatically increases both quality and silence.
key_points: Every worthwhile mailing list eventually degrades due to a few loud posters, flame wars, and topic drift
Moderation creates dull dictatorships; game mechanics that encourage self-regulation are more interesting
Extreme posting constraints (once per year) cause most people to simply listen rather than contribute
The experiment attracted 2,000 subscribers but surprisingly few submissions, confirming that scarcity raises the bar for participation
Complex solutions often contain one simple kernel worth extracting — the cool-down period was the real insight
money_quotes: What if every utterance contained within a vow of monastic silence, digitally enforced?
Wouldn't you think twice before invoking Goodwin's Law over a comic book?
The classic solution is moderation, but that tends to turn in to a dull dictatorship, not to mention cause a lot of work for the moderator.
I realized that a) There was no way I was ever going to implement something this complicated, and b) I had pretty much just reinvented Hacker News.
The posts will be, it seems, mostly excellent. And if current trends hold up, fairly rare.
What would people do on a mailing list if they could only speak once a year? Mostly just shut up and listen.
posts.key_insight: When you make every utterance costly, most people choose silence — and the few who speak produce something worth hearing.

posts.summary: Shapiro recounts a formative conversation with his CEO at Wildseed, a quirky Linux-based handset startup in 2002. When Shapiro confronted CEO Eric Engstrom about rumors the company was for sale, Engstrom's blunt response — that of course the company is for sale, because that's what startups do — reshaped his understanding of startup economics. Shapiro contrasts this pragmatic view with a friend who would refuse anything less than a multibillion-dollar IPO. The post argues that for most startup founders, being open to acquisition is simply rational, not a failure of ambition.
key_points: Most startups are inherently for sale — that's the nature of the venture-backed business model
A pragmatic willingness to sell at the right price is distinct from a lack of ambition
Some founders treat anything less than a massive IPO as failure, but this mindset is the exception, not the rule
The Wildseed anecdote illustrates how early-career employees often have idealistic notions about companies that experienced leaders see more transactionally
money_quotes: Dan, we're a startup. Of course we're for sale. This fucking plant on my desk here is for sale, if the price is right. What do you think we're doing here?
He told me – and I quite believe him – that he would sell the company for a billion dollars over his dead body.
He was extremely clear that anything less than a multibillion dollar IPO was a pathetic failure, and he'd resign before he'd let such an abomination happen.
But the rest of us startup types, the story is simple: just about every startup is for sale.
He told me once that he bought them so guests would get annoyed and not stay long.
posts.key_insight: For most startup founders, the company being 'for sale' isn't a betrayal of mission — it's the entire point of the venture.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro reflects on seven years of helping startups and distills his approach into actionable principles. He argues the best way to help the ecosystem is by building a great startup yourself, but for those giving advice, efficiency and directness matter most. He advocates for batch mentoring, prioritizing companies with traction, strict time management, and staying in your lane of expertise. The post culminates in a new policy: he'll ask startups seeking his advice to let him write about their situations for an O'Reilly book, turning individual help into collective wisdom.
key_points: The single greatest contribution to the startup ecosystem is building a successful startup yourself, which injects talent, capital, and optimism
Advice has the highest ROI when directed at startups already past early commitment hurdles — further down the funnel means less chance good advice gets wasted
Mentors should only advise in areas where they have genuine experience, not just opinions
Directness in short meetings beats politeness that wastes limited time
Shapiro introduces a 'pay it forward' policy: startups seeking his advice must agree to let their stories be shared to help others
money_quotes: I've always known that the greatest way to help startups is by building a great startup.
Success breeds success, injecting talent and cash in to the ecosystem.

The further down the funnel the startup is, the lower the chance that good advice will be rendered moot by one of the many random quirks that strike down entrepreneurs, sending them back to the clutches of Big Companies.
It's easy to have an opinion, but if there's no experience behind it, just explain that it's not an area of expertise and move on to a new topic.
Being direct is just a thin grey line away from being an asshole.
Why are you asking me how to marketing your social network when I've never done it before either? Let's focus on questions I'm actually qualified to answer for you.
I think the startup community suffers from a little too much secrecy.
posts.key_insight: The most scalable way to help startups is to convert individual mentoring conversations into shared knowledge, turning private advice into a public good.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro explains why investors refuse to meet with companies that compete with their existing portfolio investments. Drawing from his experience on both sides of the fundraising table, he dismantles three common entrepreneur objections: that the companies aren't really competing, that an initial conversation would clarify things, and that the entrepreneur doesn't mind shared investors. He then reveals the deeper reason: even without any wrongdoing, an investor who meets with competitors risks reputation damage and legal exposure when portfolio companies inevitably converge on similar ideas.
key_points: Entrepreneurs systematically overestimate how different they are from competitors because they only know as much as the last press release
Investors are looking for reasons to say no, not reasons to say yes — a potential conflict is efficient triage
The real risk is reputational and legal: when two companies independently converge on the same idea, the investor gets blamed for leaking information
Fiduciary obligations create impossible conflicts when an investor holds confidential information from competing companies
Conflict is subjective and expands over time as companies pivot, making conservative avoidance the rational default
money_quotes: I didn't actually grok what was going on until I sat on the other side, looking at investments.
Telling an investor that there's no conflict is like telling them what they should eat for dinner – no matter how much you know about the situation, they know more.
Most investors are looking for reasons to say no.
A typical VC is going to say 'yes' to between 1% and 0.1% of pitches; that means they need to triage opportunities aggressively, and guess what? You Just Got Triaged.
Good investors feel a strong obligation to their existing portfolio, and they don't owe you squat.
Your investor chose their horse because they thought it could beat all comers, including your horse. Placing a second bet jeopardizes them both legally and ethically.
posts.key_insight: Investors avoid competitors not out of closed-mindedness but because even innocent exposure to competing pitches creates unavoidable reputational and legal risk when companies inevitably converge on similar ideas.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his experience attending YCombinator Demo Day, where over 70 companies presented. He took notes in a public Google Docs spreadsheet, which unexpectedly became a collaborative live document. A few hundred people joined in real-time, including YC partners and the presenting companies themselves, adding questions and commentary. The post is a brief share of the spreadsheet link for those interested in the YC batch.
key_points: Crowdsourced note-taking via a public Google spreadsheet turned a passive viewing experience into a collaborative one
The spreadsheet attracted hundreds of participants including YC insiders, demonstrating organic community engagement
With 70+ companies presenting, structured real-time documentation was essential for keeping track
money_quotes: With that many companies to keep track of, it helps to take notes!
A few hundred (at least) people joined in, and there were questions and additions from all over (including some of the YC partners and the companies themselves).
posts.key_insight: A simple shared spreadsheet can turn a one-directional demo day into a collaborative, community-driven experience.

posts.summary: Shapiro evaluates three aircraft designs—traditional helicopters, airplanes, and multicopters—for autonomous drone delivery of food (framed around the viral 'Tacocopter' concept). He systematically analyzes each design's strengths and weaknesses across lift capacity, flight efficiency, maneuverability, redundancy, and safety. While airplanes win on range and helicopters on agility, multicopters emerge as the clear winner due to their mechanical simplicity, cheap redundancy, lower prop mass, and ability to support blade guards—all critical for safe urban delivery.
key_points: Traditional helicopters offer unmatched agility but are fragile nightmares of interdependent parts where any single failure is catastrophic
Airplanes are far more fuel-efficient and survivable but fundamentally unsuited for last-mile delivery requiring hover and vertical landing
Multicopters became practical only because smartphones and consumer electronics drove sensor prices down by two orders of magnitude
Multicopter redundancy is cheap—just add more arms—whereas helicopters and planes must double their entire lifting power for backup
The decisive factor for delivery drones is safety and robustness, not raw performance, which makes multicopters the clear winner
money_quotes: Flying autonomous deliverybots are coming. Fast.
Helicopters are basically a giant spinning ball of disaster continuously waiting to happen, and when anything goes wrong, it does.
A single high-quality gyro used to go for a thousand bucks. Now, you can get 3 gyros, 3 accelerometers, and a nice CPU to manage the whole thing for under a sawbuck.
With quadrotors, there's just one moving part: motors. Lots of motors.
To get redundancy in a helicopter or plane, you have to double the lifting power. For a multicopter, you just add arms.
Multi-taco-copters outperform heli-taco-copters in the crucial tiebreaker of 'not killing people with spinning death from above'.
FAA, get a move on. I'm getting hungry and the multicopters are restless.
posts.key_insight: The winning drone delivery design isn't the one with the best performance on any single axis, but the one that's simplest, most redundant, and least likely to hurt someone when something goes wrong.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro introduces the 'Pyramid Pitch' framework, arguing that entrepreneurs should structure their pitches like Maslow's hierarchy of needs — addressing the most fundamental questions first and building upward. Using his startup Ontela as an example, he demonstrates how each layer of the pitch answers the listener's most pressing question at that moment, from 'what do you do?' to 'how do you make money?' The key principle is that if the conversation stops at any point, the listener walks away with a coherent understanding. He contrasts this with three common anti-patterns: the story pitch, the business plan pitch, and the feature pitch, all of which bury the lede.
key_points: Structure pitches so the most critical information comes first, with each subsequent layer answering the next logical question in the listener's mind
Startups are dictated by randomness and chaos — even controlled settings like pitch events can be derailed by wandering attention, so you must grab your audience immediately
Three common pitch failures: leading with backstory (the story pitch), leading with market analysis (the business plan pitch), or leading with a feature list (the feature pitch)
A good pitch should be interruptible at any point and still leave the listener with a solid understanding of what you do
money_quotes: The potent entrepreneur's life is full of inflection moments: brief opportunities to shift the destiny of your company.
It's hard to attain enlightenment when you have to go to the bathroom.
Minds wander, distractions beckon, bladders fill… if you don't grab your audience immediately, you may never get them back.
The most important stuff goes first, and if you stop at any point, they walk away with a solid idea of what you're doing.
This is a great way to say to your new friend with the crossed arms and distracted smile, 'I may ignore strategy for tactics, but at least I'm deaf to social cues.'
The disease is sloppy and meandering pitches. The solution is the Pyramid Pitch.
posts.key_insight: The best pitches are structured like a pyramid — each layer answers the listener's most urgent question, so the conversation remains coherent and compelling no matter when it gets interrupted.

posts.summary: Shapiro recounts how he and his Ontela co-founders confused individual empowerment with collective decision-making, inadvertently creating the same bureaucratic slowness they'd left big companies to escape. By involving all founders in every decision, they consumed two or three people's time for marginal improvement in decision quality. Disagreements compounded the problem, making collaboration even slower. The fix was learning to trust each founder to own their domain and make final calls independently. The post argues that startups win through execution speed, not decision perfection, and that trust is the ingredient that lets small teams move fast.
key_points: Involving multiple people in every decision creates a net productivity loss that far outweighs any improvement in decision quality
Startups don't out-think competitors, they out-execute them — speed matters more than perfect decisions
Trust is the key ingredient that allows co-founders to work independently while staying aligned
There's a critical distinction between empowering individuals and empowering everyone to second-guess each other
Collaboration should mean sharing knowledge and asking for help, not requiring consensus on every decision
money_quotes: If two people work on a task, it takes twice as long.
We confused individual empowerment, which we all wanted, with its precise inverse: decision by committee.
Startups don't out-think their competitors, they out-execute them.
The funny thing about second guesses is that they take twice as long – but they're not twice as good.
Instead of each of us being empowered individually, we empowered ourselves to second-guess each other.

Success – fast success – means that everyone knows who owns a decision, supports that person in making that decision, trusts that person's final decision (even if they're wrong sometimes), and shuts the hell up when that person's decided.
At a startup, you can double your effectiveness and outperform your competitors by simply doing your job… instead of someone else's.
posts.key_insight: Startups lose more from slow, committee-driven decisions than they gain from marginally better ones — trust each founder to own their domain and move fast independently.

posts.summary: Shapiro corrects a common misconception in startup financing: that a convertible note's cap equals the company's valuation. He recounts a conversation where founders conflated YC companies' convertible debt caps of $10-20M with actual valuations. He explains that convertible debt is a loan that converts to equity at either the next investor's price or the cap, whichever is lower. Using a reductio ad absurdum argument—uncapped notes would imply infinite valuation—he demonstrates that a cap merely sets an upper bound, not a value.
key_points: Convertible debt caps are frequently and incorrectly cited as company valuations, even by experienced founders
A cap sets a ceiling on conversion price, not a floor or a definitive valuation—the actual value ranges from zero to the cap
The absurdity is proven by uncapped notes: if cap equals valuation, uncapped companies would be infinitely valuable
This confusion distorts perceptions of startup valuations, making them appear higher than they actually are
money_quotes: A note with a cap does not imply that the company's value is equal to the cap.
If the cap is the valuation, then those companies are infinitely valuable, since the cap is infinite. Which is, of course, absurd.
So what's the valuation of a company that takes a note with a cap? Well, you don't know. It's a range – from zero, to the cap.
Convertible debt is a loan. It's a loan that converts in to equity at one of two prices: the price the next investor pays, or the price of the cap, whichever is lower.
Say it with me: Caps aren't valuations.
posts.key_insight: A convertible note's cap sets a ceiling on conversion price, not a valuation—conflating the two inflates perceived startup valuations and reflects a fundamental misunderstanding of the instrument.

posts.summary: Shapiro uses the conceit of a social game called 'StartupVille' to catalog the shared milestones and indignities of startup life. Born from a late-night conversation with a fellow founder who had just maxed out a credit card, the post lists satirical badges founders can earn for experiences like site outages, awkward CEO conversations, and cleaning the office toilet. The format gamifies the unglamorous reality behind startup mythology. The post closes with a frank acknowledgment that the startup game isn't for everyone, and some players end up broke and begging for a corporate job.
key_points: Startup life follows a predictable pattern of shared milestones that can be catalogued like game achievements
The 'badges' reveal how much of startup culture involves unglamorous, stressful, or absurd experiences rather than the heroic narrative usually told
Financial risk is a recurring theme — maxed credit cards, triple mortgages, and hundred-grand debts are treated as normal gameplay
The game metaphor subtly critiques the way startup culture romanticizes suffering and risk-taking
money_quotes: I just maxed out one of my credit cards. I should get a badge for that.
First time you get to clean the company windows, clean the company floor, or plunge the company toilet
Told your cofounder to tone down the pitch a little bit, as s/he was scaring you with the enthusiasm level
Had the 'OK, one of us has got to be the CEO' conversation with a cofounder
Some wake up with a triple mortgage and a hundred grand in credit card debt, begging for a job at Microsoft.
But you'll never know if you don't give it a try! Do you have what it takes to win Startupville?
posts.key_insight: The shared indignities and financial risks of startup life are so universal they can be reduced to game badges, which says something both funny and unsettling about the culture.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro demystifies NDAs by explaining that they rarely serve their intended purpose of preventing information leaks. He argues that NDAs are practically unenforceable in court because it's nearly impossible to prove someone leaked a specific secret. Instead, NDAs function primarily as tools for legal intimidation—a 'force multiplier in legal blustering.' The key dynamic governing whether NDAs get signed is leverage: whoever has the money or power can demand signatures. Shapiro advises entrepreneurs to stop asking investors to sign NDAs (it signals inexperience) and instead reserve NDA requests for situations where you hold the leverage.
key_points: NDAs are nearly unenforceable because proving someone leaked a secret rather than independently discovered it is extremely difficult
The primary practical function of NDAs is as a threat multiplier for potential lawsuits, not actual confidentiality protection
Whether an NDA gets signed comes down entirely to leverage—whoever controls the money or value in the relationship dictates terms
Asking investors to sign NDAs signals inexperience and lack of understanding of power dynamics in fundraising
Honorable people respect confidentiality without paperwork, and deceptive people ignore it regardless of what they sign
money_quotes: There is no love like a first time entrepreneur's love with nondisclosure agreements.
But that's a giant stinking load of donkey dung. It's almost never going to happen that you actually sue someone for disclosing a secret and prevail.
NDAs are a force multiplier in legal blustering.
I find deceptive folk make a habit of being deceptive, and honorable folk respect these things with or without paperwork.
There's one and only one reason that NDAs get signed: when one side or both have a great deal of leverage in the negotiation.
If you are asking someone for money, don't ask them to sign an NDA. You will come off looking like an ignoramus or a terrorist.
Real wants you to sign an NDA before they let you pee. This is not a dramatic hyperbole to emphasize their vigor in pursuing your signature; it's the literal truth.
posts.key_insight: NDAs are not confidentiality tools but leverage instruments—they get signed when one party has power over the other, and asking for one when you lack leverage only signals inexperience.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a live, world-writable Google Spreadsheet where he's taking notes on startup pitches from Techstars Demo Day 2012. He invites readers to follow along in real-time and contribute questions and comments. This is a repeat of a format he previously used for Y Combinator Demo Day.
key_points: Shapiro uses a collaborative, world-writable spreadsheet to live-document startup demo day pitches
The format was previously tested at Y Combinator Demo Day, suggesting it worked well enough to repeat
He invites real-time audience participation through the shared document
money_quotes: I'm running a live, world-writable spreadsheet of my notes on the pitches from Techstars demo day 2012.
posts.key_insight: A brief logistical post inviting readers to follow along with live collaborative notes on Techstars Demo Day 2012 pitches.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro identifies three common mistakes Kickstarter creators make that drive away potential backers: dodging important product details, rushing out stretch goals without proper planning, and downplaying risks. Using a men's clothing project as a case study, he argues that transparency and honesty matter more than polish. He contends that creators should embrace polarizing decisions and be upfront about limitations rather than trying to appeal to everyone. The post ends on a positive note, with the example company overhauling their page in response to feedback.
key_points: Kickstarter creators hurt themselves by avoiding specific product details that might alienate some buyers, when polarizing decisions are actually a startup virtue
Hastily conceived stretch goals signal lack of planning and erode backer confidence in the team's ability to deliver
Most creators treat the 'Risks and Challenges' section as a marketing opportunity rather than an honest assessment, which backfires with savvy backers
Backers don't expect perfection but do expect honesty and transparency about what they're getting
The best response to criticism is listening and adapting, as demonstrated by the featured company's overhaul
money_quotes: Clever, well meaning, adorable, and more clueless than a general with a gmail account.
As a startup, polarizing decisions are a virtue.
If you're selling dryclean-only T-shirts cut for Arnold Schwarzenegger, own it!
When you don't address issues like these proactively, it makes me think you're more concerned with having lots of customers than having happy customers.
It is terrifying to me that you are now accepting real cash money for a product that you conceived of less than 24 hours ago.
Pro tip: if I, who know your business as well as I know the feeding habits of the Springbok Antelope, can come up with more risks than you can, you're not doing it right.
We can forgive a lot, as long as you're being straight with us.
posts.key_insight: Kickstarter backers are taking a bet on your team, and the fastest way to lose that bet is substituting marketing spin for honest transparency about your product, plans, and risks.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro explains why he deliberately avoided learning about a Techstars startup's plans because they operated in a space overlapping with his day job. Despite generally advocating against excessive confidentiality concerns, he recognized this situation was different — his fiduciary obligations to his employer meant any good ideas shared with him could be used competitively. He shares his candid email exchange with the startup founder to illustrate the nuance between personal trustworthiness and structural conflicts of interest. The core message is that trust alone isn't sufficient protection when someone's obligations run counter to your interests.
key_points: Being trustworthy doesn't mean being safe to confide in — structural obligations can override personal goodwill
Startups rarely die from idea theft, but sharing secrets with someone who has means, motive, and opportunity is an unnecessary risk
Confidentiality paranoia is usually overblown, but conflicts of interest deserve genuine caution
True trustworthiness sometimes means warning people not to trust you with certain information
money_quotes: Believe me when I say that if you give me a good idea, I am obligated by my responsibilities to my employer to make use of it.
In my case, being trustworthy means being completely transparent that I'm running a business that has overlapping opportunities.
I'm usually on the 'don't sweat confidentiality' bandwagon. In fact, I'm generally the guy in the front of that bandwagon in the big fuzzy hat with a baton.
When someone not only has means, motive, and opportunity but actually tells you their full intent… then it's probably time to play it coy.
Trusting someone isn't enough. Even if they are trustworthy, their obligations may not be to you.
Some aspect of that business may be a zero-sum game with yours.
posts.key_insight: Trustworthiness and safety are not the same thing — a person can be entirely honest and still be structurally obligated to act against your interests.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro analyzes Chris Zacharias's unconventional fundraising strategy of pricing his round below market rate to attract value-conscious angel investors. Paul Graham challenged this approach, arguing angel investors shouldn't be price-sensitive and that the evidence was anecdotal. Shapiro argues that regardless of whether Graham is technically correct, Zacharias is employing a brilliant 'contrarian segmentation' strategy. By fishing in a smaller, less competitive pool of investors, Zacharias gets faster access to the best investors in that segment, even if he pays some additional cost for the unconventional approach.
key_points: Chris Zacharias deliberately priced his funding round below market to attract value-conscious angels who wouldn't invest at typical YC valuations
Paul Graham argued that angel price-sensitivity is irrational and that Chris's success was merely anecdotal
Shapiro frames the strategy as 'contrarian segmentation' — deliberately targeting an underserved market segment to face less competition
The core insight is that competing in a smaller pool with no competition can be superior to competing in a larger, crowded one
money_quotes: I think Paul may be correct, but he misses Chris's genius.
I believe what Chris is doing is an example of one of my favorite startup strategies, the contrarian segmentation.
There is no competition in this investment pool.
Paul may be right that 'there is no value-investing in startups' – that is, Chris's investors are making a mistake.
Chris is doing something very smart: he's fishing in a smaller pool. There may be fewer fish, but he's got them all to himself.
posts.key_insight: Deliberately targeting an underserved, less competitive market segment — even one that seems irrational — can be a winning strategy because having no competition outweighs the costs of the unconventional approach.

posts.summary: Shapiro reviews three job postings from Hacker News's YC-exclusive jobs board, evaluating how well each uses its limited headline real estate to attract the right candidates. He finds that most postings waste their space on company names and generic job titles, while the best ones prioritize what makes the role compelling. The post argues that a job listing headline's purpose is not to inform but to get the perfect person to click. He scores the 22 active postings and finds only 7 attempted anything beyond a bare company-name-plus-title format.
key_points: A job posting headline's goal is not to inform or educate but to get the ideal candidate to click on it
Most YC companies on Hacker News waste their limited headline space listing their unknown company name and a generic job title
The best posting cut the company name entirely to make room for what made the role compelling — and that was 'genius'
FarmLogs succeeded by leading with their unusual mission, which would attract exactly the right kind of curious person
money_quotes: The goal of the title is not to inform, or promote, or educate: it's to get the perfect person to click on it.
Given limited real estate, they prioritized wisely. They tell the reader what kind of person will be a good fit. They explain why the job is going to be different and interesting.
So what did they cut? Their company name. Genius.
Their perfect candidate's gonna smirk, imagine themselves as Jack/David/Sophie/Ender for a second, then click and find out the company anyway.
It's a target rich environment, so you know the right people are reading.
posts.key_insight: When you have limited space to attract talent, cut your company name and lead with what makes the role irresistible — the right people will click to find out who you are.

posts.summary: Shapiro recounts a coffee meeting with a promising startup CEO who casually said he needed 'someone with a sexy voice' for cold-calling. Rather than letting it slide, Shapiro agreed with the underlying sales strategy but pushed back on the language, explaining it signals judging staff on criteria other than effectiveness. The CEO was receptive, later emailing that he hadn't realized how his language might alienate candidates. Shapiro frames this as a lesson in how casual sexist language in startups is counterproductive, legally risky, and filters for the wrong people.
key_points: Casual sexist language in hiring signals that staff are judged on attributes other than effectiveness, which alienates thoughtful people and attracts the wrong culture.
The real business need was someone persuasive, not someone attractive — conflating the two is counterproductive to actual sales goals.
Language like this creates legal liability and is essentially a textbook example of discriminatory job descriptions.
Giving direct, honest feedback to peers — even when uncomfortable — is part of being a good mentor and community member.
The addendum notes the critique applies regardless of gender: evaluating employees on 'sexiness' is the core problem.
money_quotes: While the explicit message he told me was about sales strategy, there was a very strong implicit message: 'I'm judging my staff by something other than how effective they are'.
A sultry-sounding idiot will get him nowhere. What he really wants is someone persuasive.
Whether that's Tony Robbins, Oprah Winfrey, or Jessica Rabbit – he just needs someone who gets the job done.
The language will both alienate some people he interacts with (let's call them 'thoughtful people') and encourage others (for the sake of argument, we'll call them 'jerks').
He doesn't need a sexy creature, he needs an effective creature.
Thoughtful people don't want to work in places where the CEO is evaluating how sexy they are in the course of conducting business.
Sometimes we all misspeak; I'm glad I could give him a friendly nudge.
posts.key_insight: Casual language around hiring reveals implicit values — and when those values prioritize attractiveness over effectiveness, they repel good candidates, invite legal risk, and undermine the actual business goal.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro proposes five product ideas he'd love to see funded on Kickstarter, ranging from customizable notebooks and tablet-powered laser shows to combination RC vehicles, machine-washable dress shirts, and a sleek key consolidator. Each idea is presented with specific bonus features and price points, reflecting a maker's eye for practical innovation. The post closes with a self-aware joke about the glut of 3D printers on Kickstarter. Overall, it's a lighthearted wish list that reveals Shapiro's product sensibility—favoring practical, well-designed physical goods over hype.
key_points: Each product idea is grounded in a real gap Shapiro has experienced as a consumer and maker, not abstract market analysis
