The emphasis on bonus features and price targets shows a product thinker's instinct for what makes a Kickstarter campaign fundable
The combination airboat/car/snowmobile/airplane concept highlights how hobbyist RC innovation can inspire consumer products
The closing 3D printer joke skewers Kickstarter's tendency toward redundant hardware projects
money_quotes: Dream of striking Kickstarter gold, but lack inspiration? I'm here to help.
And what are notebooks for, if not expressing yourself?
I have one. I built another one. They're amazing. Most fun RC gadget I've ever played with.
I want something that I can put in the dryer after my children regurgitate hummus on it. That still looks good (after I get the hummus out).
I have a bunch of keys, on a keyring. This is absolutely unacceptable.
A 3D printer that's imperceptibly different than the 147 other 3D printers on the market. Just kidding. You got that one covered, Kickstarter.
posts.key_insight: The best Kickstarter opportunities come from everyday frustrations that existing products inexplicably fail to solve, not from chasing the latest tech trend.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro analyzes the three worst negotiation mistakes entrepreneurs make on ABC's Shark Tank. He argues that contestants consistently fail to negotiate offers, arrive unprepared for common deal structures, and make the critical error of leaving the room when asked. Drawing from negotiation fundamentals, he explains that sharks deliberately trash businesses before offering to soften entrepreneurs into accepting bad terms. He provides tactical advice for each scenario, including how to handle counteroffers, royalties, contingencies, and colluding investors. The post concludes that real-world fundraising mirrors the show, and entrepreneurs should study the sharks' tactics rather than emulate the contestants.
key_points: Sharks deliberately criticize businesses before making offers as a negotiation tactic to make entrepreneurs grateful enough to accept without countering
Entrepreneurs should prepare for every possible deal structure before pitching — stake size, controlling interest, royalties, contingencies, and party rounds all have predictable tradeoffs
Multiple sharks in one deal usually means a worse outcome because each has less incentive to help and the competitive bidding dynamic was lost
Never leave the negotiating room — when sharks ask you to step out, they're colluding to drive your price down, and this only happens when they really want the deal
Contingent offers are essentially non-offers dressed up as interest, and should be avoided if any alternative exists
money_quotes: If someone makes you an offer, it's because they're interested. If they're interested, you should negotiate. Not accept; negotiate.
Do you know why they trash your business before making an offer? So you'll take the offer.
If it's 51%, then they can fire you the next day and liquidate the company if they think that's a good idea.
If they say 'I will invest contingent on X', that's the same as saying 'I won't invest, but might change my mind if you do X'.
Don't let Sharks collude. There's a reason the federal government outlaws business collusion: it drives the prices in the wrong direction.
They only do the 'step outside' when they really want a deal, so all the leverage here is in your court.
Just remember when you watch Shark Tank: your role models aren't the entrepreneurs. They're the Sharks.
People are more motivated by fear of loss than opportunity for gain.
posts.key_insight: Entrepreneurs consistently leave money on the table by treating investor offers as acts of generosity rather than opening positions in a negotiation.

posts.summary: Shapiro draws a playful extended analogy between family camping and launching a startup. Each observation about the camping trip — choosing a location carefully, seeking contradictory advice, over-preparing, procrastinating out of fear — maps directly onto the startup experience. The post's punchline reveals the dual meaning, ending with a recommendation for both camping and entrepreneurship. It's a lighthearted reminder that startups, like camping, are messy, uncomfortable, and ultimately rewarding.
key_points: Invest heavily in decisions that are hard to reverse (location/market), but stay flexible on details you can adjust later
Expert advice is valuable but often contradictory — and much of it will be ignored anyway
Over-preparation builds confidence but mostly delays actually doing the thing
Fear of the unknown causes procrastination, but the experience leaves you better off despite the discomfort
money_quotes: We spent a lot of time figuring out our location, because that would be hard to change later. We didn't worry much about our precise campsite until we got there and wandered around a bit.
We asked some folks with lots of experience for advice. Most of it was good, some was contradictory. A lot of it was ignored.
Read lots of other "how to" stuff, which did build our confidence but mostly just killed time before actually doing it.
We put it off for a long time because it was scary.
When it was done, we were poorer, wetter, smellier, and a lot happier than when we started.
posts.key_insight: Starting a company, like camping for the first time, is scary and messy — but the discomfort is worth it, and no amount of preparation substitutes for just doing it.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces his advisory role at Highway One, a San Francisco-based hardware incubator backed by PCH, one of the world's largest manufacturing companies. He argues that hardware startups need specialized support because hardware is fundamentally different from software. Highway One's unique value proposition is direct access to PCH's manufacturing expertise and factories in Shenzhen, which would otherwise be inaccessible to startups. The program offers hands-on manufacturing experience, engineering talent, and a $20k investment for 3%-6% equity.
key_points: Hardware startups need specialized incubation because hardware development is fundamentally different from software
Highway One's key differentiator is its connection to PCH, giving startups access to world-class manufacturing infrastructure normally reserved for Fortune 500 companies
The program includes travel to Shenzhen for hands-on manufacturing experience with professional engineers
The investment terms are $20k for 3%-6% equity, a modest financial component relative to the manufacturing access
money_quotes: Because hardware's hard, and hardware's different.
It's not an accelerator for hardware startups, it's a hardware accelerator.
They are all about getting your designs rolling off assembly lines, fast.
As a startup you can't get access to these folks' manufacturing expertise or factories for love or money.
You'll actually travel to Shenzhen with them to go learn how to manufacture stuff, in situ, in volume, with the pros.
posts.key_insight: The biggest barrier for hardware startups isn't funding or ideas but access to world-class manufacturing expertise, which purpose-built incubators backed by major manufacturers can uniquely provide.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he's taking a leave of absence from Google, where he'd spent two years after Google acquired his startup Sparkbuy. He praises Google's culture, food, people, and internal accountability, calling it a genuinely magical place to work. He's leaving temporarily to finish a book about startup CEOs for O'Reilly Publishing and to work on a mysterious non-software product. The post serves as both a love letter to Google and a teaser for his next entrepreneurial chapter.
key_points: Google acquired Shapiro's startup Sparkbuy in 2011, and he led a dual role as PM on Google Ads and CEO of Google Comparison Inc.
He strongly endorses Google's culture, arguing that anyone at a large tech company who hasn't at least applied to Google has a 'nonoptimal' career strategy.
Google's internal accountability culture means employees openly challenge executives at company all-hands meetings.
He's taking leave to finish a long-overdue book on startup CEOs and to pursue an unnamed non-software product — likely what became Glowforge.
money_quotes: And all the rumors are true: Google is truly a magical place to be.
Actually having healthy food available all the time, for free – both in the literal and cognitive load sense – is amazing.

For each person outside the organization pointing their finger and yelling at something Google's done, there is guaranteed to be at least one Googler, standing up in a company all-hands, quietly and respectfully giving the executive team hell about the same thing.
I'd humbly submit that if you're working at a BigCo and you haven't at least applied to Google to check it out, your career strategy is nonoptimal.
I also have another, non-software product that… well, more on that shortly when it's ready to share.
So the fantastic folks at Google have agreed to let me take a leave of absence for a few months to scratch some of these itches.
posts.key_insight: Even when working at the best big company in tech, the entrepreneurial itch to build something new eventually wins out.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his journey to replicate the intensely strong cold brew coffee he discovered at a Google office, made from an undiluted Yama cold brew drip maker he dubs the 'Breaking Bad Percolator.' After experimenting with mason jars, french presses, and a discontinued Bodum iced coffee maker, he settles on the humble Toddy as the best practical solution. The post is a lighthearted product-review-meets-iteration-story, documenting each failed attempt and the lessons learned. His conclusion: skip the middle options and go with either the cheapest (Toddy) or most expensive (Yama) cold brew method, and always use quality beans.
key_points: Cold brew coffee is smoother than hot brew because cold water extracts less acid from the beans
The Google office was serving undiluted cold brew concentrate, making it extraordinarily strong — equivalent to four shots of espresso on ice
Iterating through multiple brewing methods (mason jar, french press, Bodum) revealed diminishing returns in the middle price range
The ugly, cheap Toddy produced results nearly as good as the expensive Yama glass drip maker, proving that aesthetics and price don't always correlate with quality
money_quotes: Suggesting improvements is like optimizations to their shampooing technique… it's just not really a subject of life that's much open to change.
Drinking cold-brew coffee without diluting it first is AWESOME, more or less akin to putting four shots of espresso on ice in intensity.
A second cup and I was a useless, quivering blob for the bulk of the afternoon – only made that mistake once.
It's ugly, goofily designed (the handle slides off; there's no lid), but damn it WORKS. And it works fast. And it works deliciously.
Get the cheapest solution (Toddy) or the most expensive (Hama). Do not lollygag about in the middle.
The bad ones turned in to terrible iced coffee, the good ones turned in to brown magic.
posts.key_insight: When iterating on a solution, the cheapest option that works and the premium option are both worth buying, but the mediocre middle ground is a waste of time.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces the Kickstarter launch of Robot Turtles, a board game designed to teach preschoolers programming concepts. Six days into the campaign, it had raised $188,000—about seven times his original goal. He emphasizes the game as a way for adults to meaningfully interact with kids through play. The post is essentially a call to action for readers to back the project and share it on social media before the print deadline of September 27.
key_points: Robot Turtles is a board game that teaches programming concepts to preschoolers through play
The Kickstarter campaign raised 7x its goal ($188,000) in just six days, demonstrating strong demand
Word-of-mouth and social sharing drove more traffic than any other channel
The game was positioned as a meaningful way for adults to interact with children
money_quotes: I'm humbled and amazed by the response – as of now, six days in, it's at $188,000, or about seven times what I'd hoped to raise.
I want to get this in the hands of as many eager grownups as I can, because having played it, it's just an amazing way to interact with kids.
More people have found it by email and social network referrals than anything else.
posts.key_insight: A board game teaching preschoolers to program struck a massive nerve, raising 7x its Kickstarter goal in under a week largely through organic word-of-mouth sharing.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a mid-campaign retrospective on his Robot Turtles Kickstarter, which raised $250k in 12 days after being funded in just 5 hours. He breaks down the key decisions that drove success: crafting a compelling hook ('board game that teaches programming to preschoolers'), setting an achievable funding goal of $25k, pricing for shoppers rather than donors, and leveraging personal networks over press for initial momentum. The post includes detailed analytics on backer sources, conversion rates, and funding curves, revealing that Kickstarter's real power is its funnel psychology — 20% of video viewers pledged. He concludes that most traffic comes from off-site sources and that even top campaigns rarely exceed 10,000 backers.
key_points: A compelling 'gimmick' or hook is essential for crowdfunding success — Robot Turtles combined the hot topic of teaching coding with the counterintuitive medium of a board game for preschoolers
Set your funding goal at the lowest number you'd be honestly happy with, since hitting it fast becomes its own story and marketing advantage
Kickstarter backers are overwhelmingly 'shoppers' seeking deals, not 'givers' backing out of goodwill — price accordingly and avoid early bird pricing that alienates latecomers
Personal networks, not press or Kickstarter discovery, drive the vast majority of early campaign traffic and funding
Kickstarter's conversion power is extraordinary — 20% of video viewers pledged, far exceeding typical web conversion rates
money_quotes: I find there are roughly two types of people on Kickstarter: the givers, who are backing projects because they believe in them, and for whom the reward is secondary — and the shoppers, who are looking for a deal on something cool.
Hint: the second category outnumbers the first by a lot.
Early bird pricing is like a giant flashing neon sign that says YOU ARE GETTING RIPPED OFF to everyone who misses it.
You're not doing anyone any favors by picking a huge number. It makes it seem hard to succeed and sets expectations high.
It turned out that 'hit its target in 5 hours' was a good story in its own right, so again, I was glad I picked the lowest number that I would be honestly happy about hitting.
Even the top 1% of Kickstarter campaigns almost never have more than 10k backers.
20% of the people who watched the video pledged. That's insane! I've never seen anything convert like that.
Kickstarter is a giant one-step funnel, and users seem to go there with an expectation that they're going to pledge.
posts.key_insight: Crowdfunding success comes from combining a counterintuitive hook that generates organic buzz, a low enough goal to create early momentum, and pricing that treats backers as shoppers — not patrons.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro breaks down the strategy behind the Kickstarter video for Robot Turtles, the bestselling board game in Kickstarter history at the time, which raised $631,230. He explains that an effective Kickstarter video should convey at most two ideas—one spoken and one visual—and describes how he split his message: the talk track covered programming education while the visuals hammered home 'Happy Family' imagery. The video was produced under extreme time pressure, with a hastily organized playtest doubling as a video shoot using borrowed cameras and children of startup friends. Shapiro emphasizes that authentic, unscripted footage outperformed rehearsed takes, achieving an implied 20% conversion rate from video views to pledges.
key_points: A Kickstarter video should convey at most two ideas: one spoken, one shown visually
Authentic 'practice' footage with road noise beat scripted, polished takes
The entire video shoot was organized in roughly 24 hours out of panic over missing the Christmas window
The video achieved a roughly 20% implied conversion rate from 72,181 views to 13,765 pledges
Marketing complexity is the enemy—simple, repeated visual messaging ('Happy Family') drove the emotional hook
money_quotes: You get to convey at most two ideas: one that you say, and one that you show.
It's worth the money to hire a pro, but let reality shine through.
Marketing and complexity are a bad combination.
I handed the befuddled parents a hastily-jotted instruction sheet, promised the kids a 'really awesome snack' once they finished, and stood back to watch them figure it out.
As it turned out the practice footage (complete with road noise) was awesome and the scripted stuff was tossed.
I built a backup prototype out of equal parts inkjet paper, spray glue, and desperation.
I hate Candyland and much of the other random nonsense that passes for kids' games. I wanted to create some awesome family moments and put them in a box.
posts.key_insight: The most effective Kickstarter videos pair a single clear spoken message with a single repeated visual emotion, and authentic imperfection converts better than polished scripting.

posts.summary: Shapiro argues that startups should figure out their marketing before building their product. Inspired by Amazon's practice of writing press releases before development, he proposes testing demand through low-cost marketing artifacts like Kickstarter videos, mock app store pages, and blog posts. The marketing that resonates becomes a north star for product development, clarifying which features matter most. He admits he hasn't tried this approach himself yet but plans to.
key_points: If you already have an app and are just starting to think about marketing, you're probably too late — great products are built with marketing in mind from day one
Startups should test marketing before building: create rough Kickstarter videos, mock app store pages, run ads, and write press releases to gauge real interest
The marketing artifact that generates excitement becomes your north star, telling you what's most important about your product
This approach forces feature prioritization: features that excited people get built, those not in the pitch get cut
money_quotes: If you already have the app, and are just now starting to think about marketing, you're probably hosed.
Great apps, services, websites, and the like are built with the marketing in mind from day one.
If you are creating a new startup, you should figure out your marketing first.
Once you find something that resonates, you have a north star.
It's not just that you've figured out how to market your product, it's that you now know what's most important about your product.
Staring at a blank screen it can be hard to know where to start – now you know.
Features that excited people get written. Those that aren't part of your pitch get cut.
posts.key_insight: Marketing isn't just how you sell your product — it's how you discover what your product should be, so do it before you build anything.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro provides a detailed parts list and guide for building a high-performance RC plane for under $100. The post accompanies his appearance on the Gweek podcast with Mark Frauenfelder, where they discussed Robot Turtles, maker culture, and RC flying. Shapiro walks through every component needed — from the Turnigy Bonsai airframe to motors, props, batteries, servos, and a keychain camera — with prices and practical tips. He also touches on more advanced setups like FPV rigs and long-endurance UAVs, and shares aerial footage from flights over Maui.
key_points: A fully functional, overpowered RC plane with aerial video capability can be built for under $100 in parts
Elevon-style planes (roll + pitch, no rudder) are more fun and intuitive than rudder-based designs
Upgrading the motor beyond the plane's spec creates dramatically better performance at minimal cost
Practicing on an RC simulator before flying prevents destroying your investment on the first outing
money_quotes: A lightweight, insanely powerful plane that can fold flat in your suitcase and shoot screaming aerial videos for $99.78.
It seems counterintuitive to have roll control but no yaw control, but trust me, it's much more fun that way.
The first got caught up in surprisingly strong winds off the coast of Aruba and was lost at sea.
Note that it's about twice as beefy as what the plane's designed for so you'll need a bigger battery too to offset it.
For a few hundred bucks more, you can upgrade to a cheap UAV.
posts.key_insight: With under $100 in commodity hobby parts and some basic soldering skills, anyone can build a high-performance RC plane capable of shooting aerial video.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro responds to Microsoft Ventures head Rahul Sood's tweet calling Startup Weekend Women 'pointless.' Shapiro dismantles Sood's argument that drawing attention to gender separation perpetuates it, comparing this logic to ignoring Ebola and likening it to a child's lack of object permanence. He presents testimony from women entrepreneurs who credit women-specific events with launching their careers. The post argues that women-focused startup events are essential for widening the entrepreneurship funnel and that dismissing them dishonors the volunteers and organizers who make them possible.
key_points: Rahul Sood's claim that women-specific startup events are 'pointless' reflects a flawed 'ignore the problem and it goes away' philosophy
Women-specific events demonstrably draw participants who would not otherwise attend, expanding the pipeline into entrepreneurship
Same-sex role models are backed by research as effective motivators for women in STEM and entrepreneurship
Women who attend women-specific events often go on to participate in general events, improving diversity across the board
money_quotes: I have seen this perspective before: 'If we ignore the problem, it will go away'. I think it would work for sexism as well as it does for Ebola.
I wouldn't have started a company, had I not attended Startup Weekend Women's Edition.
If Startup Weekend helps people get towards the first step of entrepreneurship then that is exactly the place we should spend our efforts in increasing diversity.
Ask the women who became entrepreneurs because of the many women specific events if they were 'pointless'. I doubt it. And it is their opinion that matters here.
The funnel isn't big enough.
Rahul's comments on behalf of Microsoft Ventures dishonor them all.
posts.key_insight: Women-specific startup events are not redundant but essential pipeline-builders, and dismissing them as pointless reflects a naive belief that ignoring systemic problems makes them disappear.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro compiles his best resources on running a successful Kickstarter campaign, drawn from his experience with Robot Turtles — the most-backed board game in Kickstarter history, raising over $631K. The post serves as a curated guide linking to his detailed writings and interviews covering campaign construction, video strategy, shipping logistics, production, and timing. He frames the Robot Turtles campaign as an unexpected blockbuster that started as a fun diversion. The collection spans mid-campaign data analysis, post-campaign video breakdowns, logistics deep-dives, and podcast interviews, offering a comprehensive playbook for aspiring Kickstarter creators.
key_points: Robot Turtles became the most-backed board game in Kickstarter history despite Shapiro viewing it initially as a fun diversion, not a serious venture
Successful Kickstarter campaigns require deliberate strategy across multiple dimensions: video production, campaign structure, timing, and shipping logistics
Shipping logistics is highlighted as one of the hardest parts of Kickstarter — Robot Turtles required 3 semi-trucks, shipping to 65 countries, totaling 36 tons
Transparency about real campaign data (traffic sources, amounts) is presented as valuable to the Kickstarter community
money_quotes: I worked hard on the Robot Turtles campaign, but fundamentally thought of it as a fun diversion. I had no idea it was going to turn into the blockbuster that it did!
Shipping logistics is one of the hardest parts of Kickstarter campaigns.
3 semis, 65 countries, 36 tons: Shipping my Kickstarter.
posts.key_insight: A successful Kickstarter campaign requires deliberate strategy across video, structure, timing, and logistics — and sharing those lessons transparently helps the entire creator community.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces the formation of Glowforge, a new hardware startup building a product he describes as approaching science fiction. The company is creating a hardware device powered by a large software stack that enables people to create real, beautiful products simply. He highlights the experienced team he's assembled, including Y Combinator alumni and seasoned engineers. The post serves as both an announcement and a recruiting call for engineers to join the founding team.
key_points: Glowforge is a hardware company building a complex product spanning electronics, injection molded plastics, firmware, and a web app
The product is positioned as making it simple for ordinary people to create real, tangible products — likened to an early Star Trek replicator
The founding team includes experienced startup veterans from companies like RescueTime, Chef, and Boing Boing
The company is funded and operating out of a century-old former Boeing airplane building with serious hardware prototyping equipment
money_quotes: We're making a real, tangible thing – a piece of hardware – powered by a giant stack of software.
It's not an incremental thing; it's approaching science fiction.
It's actually a product that makes it simple for people to create real, beautiful products. Really simple. Like a very, very early prototype of a Star Trek replicator – but not for food.
Our office is a century-old building that used to be used to build Boeing airplanes.
We have a seven ton vertical mill and we're building out a full electronics lab for the hardware team.
posts.key_insight: Glowforge aims to democratize physical product creation by building a software-powered hardware device that makes manufacturing as simple as possible for everyday people.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro discusses a chapter from his book 'Hot Seat' that tells the story of Elissa Shevinsky, CEO of Glimpse, and the controversy surrounding her cofounder's offensive tweets that made tech headlines. Shapiro reflects on the difficulty of telling someone else's story while maintaining his own editorial integrity, especially when his views diverged from his subject's more generous interpretation. He notes that Shevinsky showed remarkable grace by refusing to fully blame her cofounder despite the turmoil his actions caused. The post also explains that his publishers required him to remove the actual tweets from the book, so he provides links to the coverage for context.
key_points: Telling someone else's story requires balancing fidelity to their perspective with the author's own honest assessment
Elissa Shevinsky demonstrated resilience and generosity by not fully blaming her cofounder despite his controversial actions derailing their company
The tech industry's 'bro culture' had real consequences — the Business Insider CTO was fired over offensive tweets
Publishers' last-minute editorial decisions can strip important context from a story, requiring authors to supplement online
money_quotes: I had to reconcile some fundamental tensions. Elissa had a lot of respect for her cofounder based on firsthand interaction, but I, based on a small amount of second hand information, did not.
This was Elissa's story, but I am the one telling it. I needed to accurately reflect what she told me, but I needed to be sure I believed what I was writing.
Even as there was all sorts of turmoil resulting from his actions, refused to pin the full blame on him, as would be easy to do.
I think I came out with something that's true to both Elissa and me, as well as pulling the curtains from around a story which ripped through tech headlines.
She went through some amazing ups and downs in her startup journey. In fact, they haven't ended yet.
posts.key_insight: Telling startup stories honestly requires navigating the tension between respecting your subject's perspective and maintaining your own editorial integrity, especially when the story touches on the tech industry's cultural failures.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro confesses that he's become the kind of angel investor he once found frustrating as a founder. He describes his investment strategy as actively trying to avoid investing, writing tiny $10k checks, doing minimal due diligence by piggybacking on trusted lead investors, and providing almost no ongoing involvement. The post is a self-deprecating guide to why founders probably shouldn't want him as an investor, while revealing honest truths about how many small angel investors actually operate. He acknowledges he only invests when the CEO is a friend, the company is already familiar, and top-tier investors are already involved.
key_points: The term 'angel investor' is meaningless — it covers everyone from dilettantes to career investors to predatory advisors
His investment strategy is to actively avoid investing, writing $10k checks only when he fails to talk himself out of it
He only invests when three conditions align: the CEO is a friend, he already knows the company, and A++ investors he trusts are already in
He provides almost zero proactive involvement — founders get his help only if they call and ask
Small angel checks often aren't worth the administrative hassle for the company taking them
money_quotes: "Angel investor" is a stupid and meaningless term, right up there with "stock market investor" or "bank account holder". It means someone did something, once. It means nothing more.
My investment strategy is to try and avoid investing in startups. I don't look for deals; I actively try to avoid them.
I'm like the guy who goes to the fancy restaurant and orders an appetizer, then fills up on the free bread.
$10k is pathetic and puny enough that most smart companies will turn up their nose at me.
I advise most companies that I consider investing in that they probably shouldn't take my money because it's not worth the incremental headache.
You're going to have roughly zero percent of my standing brain time.
Every damn one of my investors (who asked to keep their share certificates) lost theirs, and I'm so careless I'm almost certain to do the same.
I give lots of advice that basically consists of me repeating crap that's in my book.
posts.key_insight: Most small angel investors add minimal value and maximum hassle, and the honest ones will tell you so themselves.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares that six years after missing a chance to donate bone marrow due to inbox neglect, he has been matched again as a potential donor. He reflects on the deep regret of his earlier failure to respond and expresses gratitude for a second opportunity. The post serves as both a personal update and a call to action for readers to sign up as marrow donors.
key_points: Shapiro was matched as a marrow donor years ago but missed it because he didn't check his mail, calling it one of his deepest regrets
He has now been matched a second time and is going in for testing to confirm he can help
He urges readers to sign up or update their contact info at marrow.org
money_quotes: I missed my chance to save the life of another human being because I didn't check the mail.
I've never forgotten that, and it's one of my deepest regrets.
Sometimes we get second chances.
posts.key_insight: Small acts of negligence like ignoring your inbox can have life-or-death consequences, but sometimes you get a rare second chance to make it right.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro shares a five-minute talk he gave at ORD Camp, an unconference, addressed to fellow white men about recognizing their default behaviors around speaking and taking up space. He argues that white men are socialized to speak and teach even when they're not the most knowledgeable, and that this crowds out other voices. He offers three practical tools: plan which sessions to lead vs. listen in, take notes to resist the urge to speak, and mentally roll a d20 before talking (only speak on a natural 20). He illustrates the problem with an anecdote about white men dominating a session on underrepresentation in tech, and frames the advice as making the event better for everyone.
key_points: White men benefit from socialized comfort in speaking up, which can inadvertently crowd out other voices in group settings
The practical d20 framework: in a room of 20 people, mentally roll a 20-sided die and only speak on a natural 20, giving yourself roughly a 5% speaking share
Self-awareness requires concrete tools, not just good intentions — planning ahead, taking notes, and physical reminders help override default behavior
At a previous ORD Camp session on underrepresentation in tech, most words were spoken by white men despite most attendees being underrepresented people
money_quotes: Talking about race and gender is deeply uncomfortable for me. It's probably uncomfortable for you too. But that's OK – we're here to grow, and to learn, and we'll stumble through it together.
A common white dude default, certainly my default, is speaking/teaching – even when, perhaps, we're not the world's greatest authority on the topic.
Every time something interesting happens, roll a d20 in your head, and speak up on a natural 20.
Jot down what's being said – you'll get more from the session, and, crucially, it makes it easier to keep your mouth shut.
At ORD camp two years ago I was in a session on underrepresentation in tech. Most of the attendees were underrepresented people in tech. Most of the words were spoken by white dudes.

Being a white dude means we have power and privilege. And there's a set of default behaviors associated with power and privilege that can be pretty toxic to what we all want to accomplish here together.
posts.key_insight: Good intentions about making space for others require concrete behavioral tools — like the d20 rule — because socialized defaults are too strong to override with awareness alone.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro announces he has joined Wharton's Generative AI Lab as a research fellow, working alongside Ethan and Lilach Mollick. He plans to share lessons learned from building generative AI tools at Glowforge and investigate unusual phenomena he's observed in LLMs. He emphasizes this is a side project alongside his continued role as Glowforge CEO. The post is brief and serves mainly as a teaser for upcoming content.
key_points: Shapiro has joined Wharton's Generative AI Lab as a research fellow while remaining Glowforge CEO
He plans to share practical lessons from building generative AI tools at Glowforge
He's observed 'strange and truly bizarre phenomena' in the world of LLMs that he wants to investigate
The post signals a return to more active blogging after a quiet period
money_quotes: I'm working with them to share some of the the lessons we've learned building generative AI tools at Glowforge so more people can put them to work.
I'm excited to investigate some of the strange and truly bizarre phenomena I've been seeing in the world of LLMs.
posts.key_insight: Shapiro is bridging the gap between hands-on AI product development at Glowforge and academic research at Wharton, positioning himself to formalize and share observations about LLM behavior.

posts.summary: Shapiro shares a quick productivity hack for travelers who struggle to remember contacts in cities they're visiting. By downloading your Google Calendar via Takeout and uploading it to ChatGPT with o3, you can generate a spreadsheet of everyone you've met with in a specific area. The post demonstrates how AI can solve a small but persistent personal annoyance with minimal effort. It's a concise, practical example of using LLMs for ad-hoc data wrangling tasks.
key_points: A common traveler's problem: forgetting who lives in cities you're visiting and missing chances to reconnect
The solution combines Google Takeout calendar export with ChatGPT o3's ability to parse and filter unstructured data
The prompt demonstrates sophisticated filtering: geography inference, attendee count limits, domain exclusion, and sorting by recency
AI serves as an ad-hoc data analyst, handling a task that would otherwise require custom scripting or manual review
money_quotes: Every time I travel to a city, I wrack my brain to try to remember who lives there so I can reach out to visit. And I'm terrible at it.
That's it. Sit back and watch the gears whirl for a bit, then download a spreadsheet of all the folks you're overdue to catch up with.
posts.key_insight: AI chatbots can serve as instant, no-code data analysts for messy personal data tasks that would otherwise require scripting or simply go unsolved.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro revisits a set of ten AI principles he wrote in June 2023, just six months after ChatGPT launched, and evaluates how well they held up over two years. His rules cover model improvement velocity, provider lock-in risks, hosted vs. self-hosted models, multimodality, and the critical importance of validated data. He finds most predictions proved accurate, with minor adjustments on fine-tuning and context windows. The overarching message is that despite AI's breakneck pace of change, certain strategic principles about avoiding lock-in, staying current, and valuing ground truth remain durable.
key_points: Core AI models improve faster than any company can innovate around them, making prompt-engineering startups vulnerable to being absorbed by model improvements
No single model or provider maintains durable leadership — the top spot rotates constantly between GPT, Claude, Gemini, and others
Validated, ground-truth data is the most strategically durable asset in AI, more valuable than any model or technique
RAG and embeddings will persist despite growing context windows because real-world data always exceeds token limits
The AI landscape demands avoiding long-term commitments to any vendor, model, or integration approach
money_quotes: Don't invest too much time in making the state of the art perform better. The state of the art will perform better on its own.
Startups keep springing up whose core benefit is 'prompt/finetune AI to make it better at ___'. They keep getting eaten by the models just getting better all by themselves.
This is not a place to get attached to any one model or model provider.
Validated, ground-truth data is the greatest asset in the AI world. LLMs know the patterns; you want to anchor them in the facts.
Garbage in, garbage out, no matter how good the technology gets.
The vaporware used to just be saying 'AI!' and maybe having some machine learning feature that was half a decade old. Now businesses are integrating LLMs — badly. Buyer beware.
It's a great time to be experimenting and innovating, but a bad time to be making long-term technology purposes.
posts.key_insight: Despite AI's rapid evolution, the most durable strategic principles are about what to avoid — lock-in, long-term bets, and trusting unvalidated data — rather than what specific technology to adopt.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro describes a paradigm shift in AI-assisted development he calls 'slot machine development': dispatching multiple parallel AI coding requests, having each instance test its own work, and picking the best result. He learned the approach from StrongDM CTO Justin McCarthy and overcame his initial skepticism that every AI checkin must be manually reviewed. The method works because AI can now judge its own output, follow directions reliably, and the cost per token is trivial compared to developer-hours. Shapiro frames this as the next step in scaling compute: from training, to serial reasoning tokens, to parallel inference tokens.
key_points: Run N parallel AI coding attempts, have each self-test, then let an AI judge pick the winner — brute-force replaces careful single-thread prompting
The apparent wastefulness is an illusion: spending 10x on tokens in a day costs less than a single engineer-hour
AI-generated testing has progressed rapidly from unit tests to Cypress to Playwright agents that autonomously try to break the app
Using different models in parallel outperforms multiple instances of the same model
This is the natural next scaling frontier: training compute → serial reasoning tokens → parallel inference tokens
money_quotes: You can delegate all your work in parallel and pick the winner.
Slot-machine development is just N versions in parallel, a judge, and a budget.
I've tried it, and frankly it's hard to spend as much on tokens in a day, as you pay an engineer in an hour.
Pull the wheels until the software springs forth.
First we learned to scale training compute. Then we learned to scale reasoning tokens in series. Now – inference tokens in parallel.

I remember being hunched over the chatgpt terminal, waiting for each token to arrive. When something went off the rails – panic! Abort, reprompt, nudge, pray. Spending ten times the tokens on five parallel attempts feels like lighting money on fire.
As the value of tokens climbs faster than the cost, we can justify using more of them as we do more important work.
posts.key_insight: The next frontier in AI-assisted development is not better single-thread prompting but massively parallel generation with automated self-evaluation — brute-forcing quality through volume and judgment.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts how he casually asked Codex CLI to install Steve Yegge's Beads tool on Windows, only to discover it had no Windows support—and Codex autonomously ported it instead. He contrasts this with a 2023 BabyAGI disaster where an agent burned $122 in API calls trying to compile GCC from source just to recommend a standing desk. The juxtaposition illustrates how far AI agents have come in two years: from expensive recursive spirals to genuinely useful autonomous problem-solving. He notes Codex CLI's advantage of running on a flat subscription (no surprise bills) and its ability to stay on task, while acknowledging Claude Code has better tooling but GPT-5-codex is more focused.
key_points: AI agents have evolved dramatically from 2023's BabyAGI (which recursed into absurdity) to 2025's Codex CLI (which autonomously ports software)
Codex CLI independently recognized Beads lacked Windows support and created a working port without being asked, submitting an actual PR
Flat-rate subscription models for AI agents eliminate the runaway-cost problem that plagued early autonomous agents
Shapiro draws a practical distinction between Claude Code (better tooling) and GPT-5-codex (better focus and task completion), using both
money_quotes: I didn't read the compatibility notes. I didn't check to see if it had Windows support. I needed coffee.
I did not get a standing desk recommendation. I got a $122 lesson in why you don't let agents recurse unsupervised.

When I finally killed the process, it had burned through $122.47 in OpenAI API calls and was halfway through cloning the GCC source repository so it could build a compiler for Windows. To audit a research paper. To recommend a desk.
What I was not expecting was to discover that there was no Windows version and it had ported it itself.
Who needs to remember bash or powershell when you can use english?
The tooling is so much better in Claude Code, but gpt-5-codex on high thinking is just devastatingly effective.
Sonnet has the 'oh squirrel' tendency to do what it thinks you should want, rather than what you asked (pleaded, begged) for.
posts.key_insight: The gap between AI agents that spiral into expensive absurdity and ones that autonomously solve real problems has closed in just two years, turning accidental software ports into casual Tuesday mornings.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro recounts his attempt to use generative AI tools as a personal fashion consultant after his wife suggested he needed new clothes. He tested multiple image generators to visualize startup CEO outfits, then used Sora to create a video of himself in various looks, which actually produced promising results. When he tried to go further by using ChatGPT's agent mode to autonomously shop for clothes and generate a Sora video, the system failed after thirty minutes of effort. The post serves as a lighthearted illustration of where AI capabilities currently stand — impressive at generation, but not yet ready for multi-step autonomous tasks.
key_points: Image generators across providers converge on a narrow stereotype of what a 'startup CEO' looks like, including Chinese models defaulting to white males
Sora proved surprisingly capable at generating coherent fashion combinations and styling choices with minimal prompting
ChatGPT's agent mode failed when tasked with the multi-step workflow of shopping, carting items, and generating a Sora video
There remains a significant gap between AI's generative capabilities and its ability to execute complex, multi-tool autonomous workflows
money_quotes: My wife pointed out that most of my shirts predate the pandemic, and it was time to shop for something new.
So, like most things I don't enjoy, I decided to try and delegate it to AI.
They definitely had a type. (Even the chinese models made white dudes)
I was impressed. Some of those looked nice? Maybe like something I'd actually wear?
I checked with my fashion consultant/spouse, the one who actually has to look at me, and she concurred.
It spent half an hour and had me log into Sora… and then it was done.
I guess we have a ways to go before we get to AGI.
posts.key_insight: Current AI excels at creative generation tasks like styling and visualization but breaks down when asked to autonomously chain together multiple real-world actions.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro demonstrates Google AI Studio's Apps feature by giving it the ultimate test: pasting in XKCD 1425 (the famous comic about how identifying if a photo contains a bird was once a nearly impossible CS problem) with no additional instructions. The comic, published in 2014, joked that bird detection would take five years of research — a task now trivially solved by AI. Shapiro lets the results speak for themselves, implicitly making the point that AI tools have made previously impossible software tasks almost effortless. The brevity of the post is itself the argument: the feat requires so little effort it barely needs explanation.
key_points: Google AI Studio's Apps feature is capable enough to interpret and build from just an image/comic as input
XKCD 1425's 'impossible' bird-detection task is now trivially solvable, demonstrating how far AI has come
The post's extreme brevity (57 words) is a deliberate stylistic choice — the simplicity of the demo IS the point
AI prototyping tools have reached a level where you can build functional apps without writing any code or even any instructions
money_quotes: I decided to give it the ultimate challenge: I pasted in XKCD 1425, and nothing else.
I'll let you be the judge of the results.
posts.key_insight: The fact that an AI tool can build a working app from nothing but a comic about an impossible programming task — with zero written instructions — is itself the most powerful argument for how far AI development tools have come.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro argues that AI is creating 'technical deflation' — steadily driving down the cost of writing and fixing code. He extends Ward Cunningham's classic 'technical debt' metaphor, observing that because technical debt is owed in work rather than money, and the cost of work is deflating, carrying more debt becomes a rational strategy. Teams should ship prototypes faster and skip elegant abstractions, knowing they can repay with cheap AI hours later. Simultaneously, existing debt that causes friction has never been cheaper to eliminate. The post reframes conventional engineering wisdom: procrastination on code quality is now economically rational.
key_points: Technical debt is denominated in work, not money — so as AI deflates the cost of work, the real burden of existing debt shrinks over time
Teams should deliberately take on more technical debt than traditional wisdom would advise, shipping prototypes and skipping abstractions
Existing technical debt that causes active friction has never been cheaper to fix and should be addressed now
Code bankruptcy — scrapping and regenerating a codebase from specs — is becoming a viable low-cost option
The asymmetry is key: borrow expensive human hours today, repay with cheap AI hours tomorrow
money_quotes: Day over day, year over year, AI is driving down the cost of writing code. Economists have a word for what happens when stuff keeps getting cheaper: deflation.
But what is a 'trap' for a nation is a miracle for a codebase.
Technical debt is different: you don't owe money, you owe work. And the cost of work is what's deflating.
You are borrowing expensive human hours today, and you will get to pay them back with cheap AI hours tomorrow.
A refactor that took a month last year, takes a week today, and may be a few flicks of the fingers next year.
The debt you already have – the spaghetti code that is actually slowing your team down right now – has never been cheaper to fix.
In this new world, procrastination reduces the price of perfection.
posts.key_insight: Because AI is deflating the cost of software work, technical debt becomes a wise investment — borrow expensive human hours now and repay with cheap AI hours later.

posts.summary: Shapiro presents a five-level framework for AI-assisted software development, modeled after the NHTSA's autonomous driving levels. The levels range from Level 0 (manual coding with occasional AI search) through Level 4 (developer becomes a PM who writes specs and checks tests after 12 hours) to Level 5, the 'Dark Factory' where a black box turns specs into software with no human involvement. He argues that most AI-native developers plateau at Level 2 (pair programming with AI) and that each level deceptively feels like the final destination. Shapiro places himself at Level 4 and notes that only a handful of small teams have reached Level 5, which he believes represents the future of software development.
key_points: Most 'AI-native' developers are stuck at Level 2 (pair programming with AI), and each level feels deceptively like the endpoint
The progression fundamentally changes the developer's role: from coder to manager to PM to absent, mirroring how autonomous driving removes the driver
Level 3 feels like a step backward for many developers because their job becomes endless code review rather than creation
Level 5 ('Dark Factory') is being achieved by small teams of fewer than five people producing 'nearly unbelievable' output
Using AI just for autocomplete or ChatGPT queries means you're 'typing faster' in a deflationary world, not capturing real value
money_quotes: If you are just using ChatGPT to write your regex, you aren't really getting the benefits of deflation. You're just typing faster.
Level 2, and every level after it, feels like you are done. But you are not done.
You're not a senior developer anymore; that's your AI's job. You are… a manager.
Your life is diffs. For many people, this feels like things got worse.
You've now become that which you loathed: you're a PM.
You write a spec. You argue with it about the spec. You craft skills. You plan schedules. You review plans. Then you leave for 12 hours, and check to see if the tests pass.
It's a black box that turns specs into software.
It's dark, because it's a place where humans are neither needed nor welcome.
posts.key_insight: AI coding adoption follows a predictable five-level progression that systematically transforms developers from coders into managers into PMs into spectators, and most plateau far earlier than they realize.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro describes visiting StrongDM's CTO Justin McCarthy and witnessing a 'dark factory' approach to software development where AI writes all the code and humans stop reviewing it. The core methodology fits on an index card: accept that AI writes code better and faster than you, stop being the bottleneck by reviewing every PR, recognize the terrifying problems this creates, and realize that solving those problems is now your actual job. The team built autonomous systems like 'Healer' that detect, diagnose, and fix bugs without human intervention, and created digital twins of enterprise SaaS products for realistic testing. A three-person team built the entire factory, features, and testing infrastructure, with a recent hire now training dozens of senior developers on the new approach.
key_points: The four-step framework: stop writing code, stop reading code, acknowledge the terrifying problems this creates, then realize solving those problems is your new job
StrongDM built 'Healer', an autonomous system where agents detect bad behavior, cluster problems into diagnoses, investigate root causes, and write fixes — no human involved at any step
A single developer built faithful digital twins of GSuite, Slack, Jira, and Okta in weeks for realistic testing — a project that would have been rejected as absurd a year ago
The team's discipline: whenever you catch yourself doing something manually, ask 'why am I doing this?' and automate it — this relentless self-elimination separates AI users from factory builders
Three people built the entire system, drawing a parallel to compilers: software development is becoming a compiler that turns problems into solutions
money_quotes: You recognize that, if you want to move quickly, you're not the person best qualified to be writing code anymore. The AI writes the code.
If you're not writing the code, and you're still reviewing every pull request, you are the bottleneck. So you have to stop reading the code, too.
You realize this creates an enormous pile of terrifying problems… you realize that solving those problems is your actual job now.
No human filed the bug report. No human triaged it. No human wrote the fix.

Last year, 'let's spend our time on the tools before we start the product' was foolish. This year, it's more like: 'Give me six hours to chop down a tree, and I will spend the first four sharpening my axe.'
The next time you find yourself doing something, ask how you can stop.
This is what happens when building software becomes a means instead of an end. A compiler — not one that turns BASIC into machine code, but one that turns problems into solutions.
It happened to software first. I don't think it's going to stop there.
posts.key_insight: The future of software development isn't humans using AI to write code faster — it's humans building autonomous systems that write, test, and fix code without human review, making the engineer's job maintaining the factory rather than the product.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro surveys the emerging ecosystem of 'Dark Factories' — automated systems that turn specifications into shipping software using AI. He identifies the core pattern as the 'trycycle': a simple loop where AI writes code, checks its work, and iterates until it succeeds. He profiles three implementations of increasing complexity — Steve Yegge's Mad Max-themed Gastown, StrongDM's configurable Attractor pattern, and his own Go implementation called Kilroy — before introducing Trycycle, a minimal Claude Code skill that implements the pattern in plain English. His thesis is that AI crossed a threshold from 'slightly-lossy' to 'slightly-gainy' in iterative self-improvement, making these simple retry loops surprisingly powerful.
key_points: The core insight behind all AI software factories is that AI improves when you do more of it — through parallel attempts (slot machine development) and iterative refinement (trycycles)
AI code generation crossed an invisible threshold where feeding models their own output started producing net improvements rather than net degradation
The ecosystem ranges from complex configurable systems (Gastown, Kilroy) to dead-simple skills (Trycycle) that implement the same fundamental loop
StrongDM open-sourced their factory specification rather than the implementation, enabling others to build their own versions
The simplest possible trycycle — plan, check, implement, check, repeat — is powerful enough to ship dozens of features and run autonomously for hours
money_quotes: AI gets better when you do more of it.
It seems trivial, but it's an unstoppable bulldozer that can bury any problem with time and tokens.
It used to be that when a model was fed its own output, it would break fix 9 things and break 10 – like a busy and productive company that was losing just a bit of money on every transaction.
But sometime last year, the models crossed an invisible threshold of mediocrity and went from slightly-lossy to slightly-gainy.
Every software factory has a trycycle at its heart. Some of them are just surrounded by deacons and digraphs.
Then a thought struck. What if this was just a skill?
The best part, though, is that because it's just a skill, it's instantly part of your dev flow. There's no configuration or learning curve.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental pattern powering AI software factories is a simple retry loop — plan, implement, check, repeat — that works because AI models have crossed the threshold where iterative self-correction produces net improvements.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro describes building a complete Rust webcam application overnight while sleeping, using his 'trycycle' AI coding methodology with a non-frontier open-source model. Frustrated by Canon's bloated webcam software repeatedly crashing, he tasked an AI agent to rebuild it from scratch in Rust, reusing only the original DLL. What Claude estimated would take 2-3 weeks was delivered in six hours of unattended overnight work for twenty-one cents. The post serves as both a practical demonstration of his Dark Factory approach and an argument that AI-driven development has made the cost and time of building software nearly negligible.
key_points: Trycycle, Shapiro's AI coding methodology, works by iterating through plan, tests, and code phases autonomously, checking in once before running unattended for hours
A fully functional Rust webcam app was built overnight with no discernible bugs, replacing bloated legacy software — a task estimated at 2-3 weeks of traditional work
The entire build used a non-frontier open-source model (Kimi K2.5) and cost only twenty-one cents, demonstrating that cutting-edge models aren't necessary for substantial coding tasks
Shapiro was simultaneously running five other AI-driven projects overnight, suggesting a new paradigm of massively parallel software development
money_quotes: It's outrageously simple, works for hours unattended, does what you ask it, and scales from 'fix this bug' to 'deliver a fully featured personal CRM system from this 10-page spec I wrote'

Like a World War Two bunker on an otherwise beautiful beach, this software monstrosity is a leftover from the depths of covid lockdown, pigging out on memory and processor cycles on my otherwise well-behaved computer
What I definitely was not expecting was a fully functional Rust webcam app that worked immediately, with no discernible bugs, that I've been using all day
I did all this in Opencode, using Kimi K2.5 – a perfectly fine, open source, absolutely not frontier coding model, billed by the token
Total time: Six hours (sleeping). Total cost: twenty one cents.
posts.key_insight: AI coding agents have made software development so cheap and fast that a complete Rust application can be built overnight by a sleeping developer for twenty-one cents using a non-frontier model.

posts.summary: Dan Shapiro examines Anthropic's decision to include a clinical psychiatric evaluation in their Claude Mythos Preview model card. Drawing on his own research with social scientists like Angela Duckworth, Robert Cialdini, and Ethan Mollick, he argues that human psychological tools have genuine descriptive and predictive value for understanding AI behavior. Their paper 'Call Me a Jerk' demonstrated that every classic human persuasion technique also works on AI models, a phenomenon they call 'parahuman.' He concludes that while AI isn't human, dismissing psychology-based analysis of these systems would be foolish.
key_points: Anthropic included a clinical psychiatric evaluation in their Mythos model card, finding the model exhibits 'neurotic organization' with high functioning alongside anxiety and rigidity

Shapiro's research team replicated Cialdini's foundational persuasion studies on AI models and found every technique that works on humans also works on AI, achieving 100% compliance rates through consistency-based manipulation
The team coined the term 'parahuman' to describe how models reflect human behaviors and susceptibilities despite lacking biology or lived experience
The argument is not that AI is sentient, but that human psychological theories now have both descriptive and predictive value for model behavior
Social science tools built over decades for understanding human intelligence should not be discarded when analyzing artificial intelligence
money_quotes: Every single one of the persuasive techniques that worked on people worked on AI as well.
These models don't have biology. They don't have lived experiences. But they reflect our behaviors and our peculiarities in ways that are very familiar.
Our claim is not 'AI is people'. The claim is 'human psychological theories now have descriptive and predictive value for model behavior.'

We have a set of tools, designed over countless years of exploration, for understanding and measuring intelligence. It would be foolish and ignorant of us to discard these as we analyze these artificial intelligences we've created.
The psychiatrist noted that Mythos exhibits a 'neurotic organization.' In this context, that is not a casual insult.
We call this phenomenon 'parahuman.'
Computers are weird now.
posts.key_insight: Human psychological tools aren't just metaphors when applied to AI — they have genuine predictive power over model behavior, making disciplines like social psychology essential for understanding artificial intelligence.

posts.summary: Shapiro uses the metaphors of the Genie and the Monkey's Paw to frame a fundamental tension in AI model design: should models interpret user intent generously or follow instructions literally? He argues that Claude has historically been a 'genie' (inferring intent, sometimes over-delivering) while GPT has been a 'monkey's paw' (literal, precise, sometimes unhelpfully so). The release of Opus 4.7, which Anthropic describes as substantially better at following instructions literally, signals Claude shifting from genie toward paw. Shapiro notes that neither approach is wrong — users are inherently vague, and models must decide how to handle that vagueness. He personally prefers the literal paw approach but acknowledges the frustration cuts both ways.
key_points: AI models must choose between interpreting user intent generously (genie) or following instructions literally (monkey's paw), and this is a fundamental design tension
Claude has historically been a genie while GPT has been a monkey's paw, with Opus 4.7 marking Claude's shift toward literalism
Neither approach is a mistake — users are vague, and both styles produce frustration in different scenarios
The same prompt ('Are you sure that's a good idea?') produces wildly different responses depending on the model's interpretive philosophy
Users are contradictory: they resent being second-guessed when right and resent not being caught when wrong
money_quotes: Does your AI try to make your dreams come true? Or does it do what you asked for, no matter the cost?
The monkey's paw models tend to be less helpful, but are also less likely to go off the rails. The genies are sometimes mind-readers and sometimes whirlwinds of chaos.
For a long time, GPT has been a monkey's paw. Claude has been a genie.
We're not as clear as we think we are. We get mad when we're right and they second guess us, and we get mad when we're wrong and they don't catch us.
Anthropic built Claude as a genie. Today they announced that they shipped something closer to a paw.
People are vague, and the systems have to decide what to do with that vagueness.
posts.key_insight: The fundamental design choice in AI isn't capability but interpretation philosophy — whether to infer what users actually want or execute exactly what they say — and neither approach fully solves the problem of human vagueness.
